# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define cloud computing as per NIST SP 800-145. Mention its three classification pillars.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

According to **NIST SP 800-145** (Mell & Grance, 2011), cloud computing is defined as:
> *"A model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction."* **(2 marks)**

The three classification pillars are:
1. **Five Essential Characteristics** — On-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Three Service Models** — SaaS, PaaS, IaaS. **(0.25 mark)**
3. **Four Deployment Models** — Private, Community, Public, Hybrid. **(0.25 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
**List the five essential characteristics of cloud computing as defined by NIST.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The five essential characteristics of cloud computing (NIST SP 800-145) are: **(0.6 mark each)**

1. **On-Demand Self-Service** — Consumers can provision resources automatically without human interaction with the provider.
2. **Broad Network Access** — Capabilities are available over the network through standard protocols and heterogeneous client platforms.
3. **Resource Pooling** — Provider resources are pooled to serve multiple consumers using a multi-tenant model with dynamic reassignment.
4. **Rapid Elasticity** — Capabilities can be elastically provisioned and released to scale rapidly outward and inward with demand.
5. **Measured Service** — Resource usage is automatically monitored, controlled, and reported, enabling transparency and pay-per-use.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **NIST definition of cloud computing** with its **five essential characteristics**. Cite the source publication. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **three service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS)** using a responsibility/control matrix. Give **two real-world examples** for each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) NIST Definition and Five Essential Characteristics (7 Marks):**

**Source**: NIST Special Publication **SP 800-145**, titled *"The NIST Definition of Cloud Computing"*, authored by **Peter Mell** and **Timothy Grance**, published in **September 2011** by the National Institute of Standards and Technology, U.S. Department of Commerce. **(1 mark)**

**Definition (verbatim, 1 mark)**:
> *"Cloud computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction."*

**Five Essential Characteristics (1 mark each):**

| Characteristic | Explanation |
|----------------|-------------|
| **On-Demand Self-Service** | Consumer provisions compute/storage automatically without human interaction with provider |
| **Broad Network Access** | Resources available over network via standard protocols (HTTP, REST) from any device |
| **Resource Pooling** | Multi-tenant model; physical and virtual resources dynamically reassigned; location-independent |
| **Rapid Elasticity** | Capabilities scale in/out rapidly, appearing unlimited to the consumer |
| **Measured Service** | Automatic metering and reporting of resource usage for transparency and billing |

**(b) Service Models Comparison (7 Marks):**

**Conceptual Introduction (1 mark)**:
The three service models — SaaS, PaaS, IaaS — form the **SPI stack** (Software, Platform, Infrastructure), representing progressively lower-level abstractions of cloud capability.

**Responsibility/Control Matrix (4 marks):**

| Layer Managed | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---------------|:----:|:----:|:----:|
| Applications | Consumer | Consumer | Provider |
| Data | Consumer | Consumer | Provider |
| Runtime / Middleware / OS | Consumer | Provider | Provider |
| Virtualization / Servers / Storage / Network | Provider | Provider | Provider |

**Examples (2 marks — 0.25 each, two per model):**

- **IaaS**: Amazon EC2, Google Compute Engine, Microsoft Azure VMs, DigitalOcean Droplets.
- **PaaS**: Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Microsoft Azure App Services, Heroku.
- **SaaS**: Gmail, Google Workspace, Microsoft Office 365, Salesforce, Dropbox.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

**(a)** Describe the **four deployment models** of cloud computing as per NIST. For each, mention **one suitable use-case scenario**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **measured service** and **rapid elasticity** with examples. Why are these two characteristics critical for the **pay-per-use** business model? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Deployment Models (7 Marks):**

**Definition and Use-Cases (1.75 marks each):**

1. **Private Cloud** — Provisioned for exclusive use by a single organization.
   - *Use-case*: A bank hosting customer transaction data on its internal cloud to comply with RBI data residency regulations.

2. **Community Cloud** — Provisioned for exclusive use by a specific community of consumers with shared concerns (mission, security, compliance).
   - *Use-case*: A consortium of hospitals sharing a HIPAA-compliant cloud to exchange patient records.

3. **Public Cloud** — Provisioned for open use by the general public.
   - *Use-case*: A startup deploying its web application on AWS EC2 to leverage elastic scaling without capital expenditure.

4. **Hybrid Cloud** — Composition of two or more distinct cloud infrastructures (private, community, public) bound by technology enabling data and application portability.
   - *Use-case*: An e-commerce company running its public catalog on AWS but processing sensitive payment data in a private on-premises cloud for PCI-DSS compliance.

**(b) Measured Service and Rapid Elasticity (7 Marks):**

**Measured Service (3 marks):**
- Cloud systems automatically **meter** resource usage (CPU, storage, bandwidth, active users) at an appropriate abstraction level.
- Both provider and consumer can transparently **monitor, control, and report** usage.
- *Example*: AWS Lambda bills per **millisecond** of execution time; AWS S3 bills per **GB-month** of storage.
- *Engineering use*: Enables **transparency** for compliance audits, **fair billing**, and **usage analytics**.

**Rapid Elasticity (3 marks):**
- Capabilities can be **provisioned and released elastically**, sometimes **automatically**, to match demand fluctuations.
- To the consumer, capacity appears **unlimited** — they can provision any quantity at any time.
- *Example*: An e-commerce site scales from **10 to 10,000 web servers** during a Diwali sale via AWS Auto Scaling, then scales back to 10 after the sale ends.
- *Engineering use*: Eliminates **over-provisioning** (waste) and **under-provisioning** (performance degradation).

**Critical for Pay-Per-Use (1 mark):**
Measured service provides the **metering mechanism** (usage data), and rapid elasticity provides the **dynamic provisioning mechanism** (on-demand supply). Together, they enable the **pay-per-use** model — the consumer pays only for what they actually consume, similar to utility billing (electricity, water). This is the defining economic advantage of cloud over traditional on-premises infrastructure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Service and Deployment Models**: Students often list "SaaS, PaaS, IaaS, Public, Private, Hybrid" together. Service models describe *what is offered*; deployment models describe *where it runs*. Mixing them loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Quoting only the characteristics without the source**: Always cite **NIST SP 800-145 (2011)**. Omitting the source reference loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the "consumer manages" column**: In control-matrix questions, students often only mention what the *provider* does. The examiner expects a *two-column* matrix (Consumer Controls / Provider Manages).
> 4. **No real-world examples**: KTU consistently awards partial marks for examples. Always give at least one AWS/Azure/GCP example per service model.
> 5. **Missing "Measured Service" in answers**: This characteristic is frequently overlooked but is critical to explain the *billing* aspect.

---

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — NIST Definition of Cloud:**

- 📘 **Canonical Source**: NIST Special Publication **SP 800-145** (September 2011), authors **Peter Mell and Timothy Grance**.
- 🧩 **The Cloud Triad**: **5 Essential Characteristics + 3 Service Models + 4 Deployment Models** = the complete NIST framework.
- 🎯 **Five Characteristics** (memory: **O-B-R-R-M**): **O**n-demand self-service, **B**road network access, **R**esource pooling, **R**apid elasticity, **M**easured service.
- 🏗️ **Service Models** (SPI stack, bottom-up: **IaaS → PaaS → SaaS**):
  - *IaaS*: Consumer controls OS upward (e.g., AWS EC2).
  - *PaaS*: Consumer controls apps and data only (e.g., Heroku).
  - *SaaS*: Consumer only configures user-level settings (e.g., Gmail).
- 🌐 **Deployment Models** (memory: **P-C-Pu-H**): **P**rivate, **C**ommunity, **Pu**blic, **H**ybrid.
- 💰 **Pay-Per-Use** is enabled by **measured service + rapid elasticity** together.
- ⚖️ **Trade-off Rule**: Lower in stack (IaaS) = more control + more responsibility; Higher in stack (SaaS) = less control + less responsibility.
- 🔑 **Key Industry Examples**: AWS (public IaaS/PaaS/SaaS), Azure (hybrid), Google Cloud, Salesforce (SaaS pioneer).
- 📝 **Exam Strategy**: Always cite the source document, use control matrices, give real-world vendor examples, and structure answers with clear headings.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Advantages and Deployment Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT745 - CLOUD, MICROSERVICES AND APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cloud Advantages and Deployment Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Cloud Computing

**Cloud Computing** is an on-demand, self-service, *pay-per-use* delivery model of networked, elastic, and pooled computing resources (servers, storage, applications, and services) provisioned and released with minimal management effort over the internet, as defined by the **NIST Special Publication 800-145**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to articulate *why* organizations migrate to the cloud, the **deployment models** (Public, Private, Hybrid, Community, Multi-cloud), and the **cloud application deployment process** (planning → architecture → provisioning → testing → deployment → monitoring).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Electricity Grid" Model

Think of cloud computing exactly like the **electricity supplied by the Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB)** to your home:

| Traditional Computing (Diesel Generator) | Cloud Computing (KSEB Grid) |
|-------------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| You buy a generator, fuel, maintain it | You just plug in and consume |
| Fixed capacity regardless of need | Pay only for units (kWh) consumed |
| Idle capacity wasted | Elastic scaling on demand |
| High upfront capital | Operational expense (OpEx) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Cloud Characteristics (NIST 5 Essentials):** *On-Demand Self-Service, Broad Network Access, Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, Measured Service.* Memorize these — they appear in **every KTU exam paper**.

## 1.3 Cloud Deployment Models — The Four Pillars

The deployment model defines **WHO owns, manages, and accesses** the cloud infrastructure.

| Deployment Model | Owner / Provider | Access | Typical User |
|------------------|------------------|--------|--------------|
| **Public Cloud** | Third-party (AWS, Azure, GCP) | Open to general public | Startups, SMEs |
| **Private Cloud** | Single organization | Restricted to org | Banks, Defense |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Combination of Public + Private | Orchestrated | Enterprises with sensitive data |
| **Community Cloud** | Multiple orgs with shared concerns | Shared by consortium | Government departments, Hospitals |
| **Multi-Cloud** | Multiple public providers | Distributed across vendors | Avoid vendor lock-in |

## 1.4 The Three Service Models (Quick Recall)

$$\text{SaaS} \subset \text{PaaS} \subset \text{IaaS}$$

- **IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service)** — You manage: OS, Middleware, Runtime, Data, Applications. Provider gives: Networking, Storage, Servers. *Example: AWS EC2.*
- **PaaS (Platform as a Service)** — You manage: Data, Applications. Provider gives rest. *Example: Google App Engine, Heroku.*
- **SaaS (Software as a Service)** — You only use it. *Example: Gmail, Microsoft 365.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Why Adopt Cloud? — The Strategic Advantages

### A. Economic Advantages

1. **CapEx → OpEx Conversion:** No upfront hardware purchase; converts capital expenditure to predictable operational expense.
2. **Economies of Scale:** Providers like AWS aggregate demand of millions of customers, achieving massive cost reduction.
3. **Pay-per-Use Billing:** Billed in **seconds (EC2)** or **GB-hours (S3)** — no idle cost.

### B. Technical Advantages

4. **Elastic Scalability:** Vertical scaling (scale-up) and horizontal scaling (scale-out) are automated.
5. **High Availability:** Providers offer **99.99% (Four 9s) → 99.999% (Five 9s)** SLAs, achievable via multi-AZ deployment.
6. **Fault Tolerance & Disaster Recovery:** Built-in replication across geographic regions.
7. **Global Reach:** Deploy in **30+ geographic regions** and **100+ availability zones** within minutes.

### C. Operational Advantages

8. **Faster Time-to-Market:** Provisioning time drops from **weeks → minutes**.
9. **Automatic Software Updates & Patching.**
10. **Focus on Core Business:** DevOps teams spend less on undifferentiated heavy lifting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:**
> $$\text{Availability (\%)} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100$$
> Where **MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures**, **MTTR = Mean Time To Repair**. This formula frequently appears in numerical questions on cloud SLA problems.

## 2.2 Cloud Computing — Reference Architecture Layers

| Layer | Function | Examples |
|-------|----------|----------|
| **Physical Layer** | Datacenter hardware, servers, networking | AWS GovCloud regions |
| **Virtualization Layer** | Hypervisors abstract physical resources | VMware ESXi, KVM, Xen |
| **Resource Pool Layer** | Aggregated compute, storage, network pools | EC2 instance pools |
| **Service Orchestration Layer** | Automated provisioning, workflow engines | AWS CloudFormation, Azure ARM |
| **Service Layer** | IaaS / PaaS / SaaS interfaces | EC2, RDS, Lambda |
| **Consumer Layer** | User access via web portal, APIs, mobile | AWS Console, REST APIs |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Note |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Availability (downtime/year) | $D = 8760 \times (1 - A)$ | Hours per year |
| 2 | Availability % | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}}$ | Decimal × 100 |
| 3 | Total Cost of Ownership | $TCO = C_{\text{capex}} + C_{\text{opex}} - R_{\text{residual}}$ | USD, over 3–5 years |
| 4 | Pay-per-Use Cost | $C_{\text{cloud}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times U_i \times T_i)$ | P = price/hr, U = units, T = time |
| 5 | Storage Cost | $C_{\text{storage}} = S \times R_{\text{GB-month}}$ | GB-month billing |
| 6 | Outbound Bandwidth Cost | $C_{\text{egress}} = D_{\text{out}} \times R_{\text{GB}}$ | Inbound is **FREE** in most clouds |
| 7 | Elasticity Ratio | $\text{Elasticity} = \dfrac{\text{Peak Load}}{\text{Average Load}}$ | $\geq 1$, higher = more elastic |
| 8 | Serverless Cost | $C_{\text{lambda}} = N_{\text{invocations}} \times \$0.0000002 + G_{\text{GB-s}} \times \$0.0000166667$ | AWS Lambda pricing model |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** Formula #8 reflects the **published AWS Lambda pricing (US-East-1 region)** used as a textbook-standard reference example. Always state "as per provider pricing" in KTU answers.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FinTech:** PayTM uses cloud elasticity to handle 10× traffic spikes during Diwali sales.
- **Healthcare:** Apollo Hospitals uses **community cloud** for cross-hospital electronic medical records.
- **EdTech:** KTU's own digital examination portal uses cloud to scale for 50,000+ concurrent students.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical & Numerical Derivations

## 3.1 Problem 1 — SLA Downtime Calculation (Classic KTU 2-Mark Numerical)

> **Question:** A cloud provider advertises **99.95% availability** for its managed database service. Calculate the maximum allowed downtime per year and per month.

### Step 1: Recall the year-to-hours conversion

$$1 \text{ year} = 365 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hours} = 8760 \text{ hours}$$

### Step 2: Compute the downtime fraction

$$\text{Downtime fraction} = 1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005$$

### Step 3: Calculate yearly downtime

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{year}} &= 8760 \times 0.0005 \\
&= 4.38 \text{ hours/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate monthly downtime

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{month}} &= \frac{4.38}{12} \\
&= 0.365 \text{ hours/month} \\
&\approx 21.9 \text{ minutes/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Result

A **99.95% SLA** allows a maximum of **4.38 hours/year** or **~22 minutes/month** of downtime. 

## 3.2 Problem 2 — TCO Comparison: On-Premises vs Cloud

> **Question:** An organization requires 10 servers (each \$5,000 one-time), 2 network engineers (\$30,000/year each), datacenter rent (\$50,000/year), and power/cooling (\$20,000/year). Cloud quote: **\$0.20/hour per equivalent server, billed 24×7×365**. Compute 3-year TCO for both.

### On-Premises TCO

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{capex}} &= 10 \times 5000 = \$50{,}000 \\
C_{\text{opex/yr}} &= 2 \times 30{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 20{,}000 = \$130{,}000 \\
\text{TCO}_{\text{on-prem, 3yr}} &= 50{,}000 + 3 \times 130{,}000 \\
&= 50{,}000 + 390{,}000 = \$440{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Cloud TCO

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hours/year} &= 365 \times 24 = 8760 \\
C_{\text{cloud/yr}} &= 10 \times 8760 \times 0.20 = \$17{,}520 \\
\text{TCO}_{\text{cloud, 3yr}} &= 3 \times 17{,}520 = \$52{,}560
\end{aligned}
$$

### Savings

$$
\text{Savings} = 440{,}000 - 52{,}560 = \$387{,}440 \;\; (\approx 88\% \text{ cost reduction})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always include **data egress costs** in real KTU problems — they can shift the breakeven point. Mention this in your assumptions to earn **extra marks**.

## 3.3 Problem 3 — Cloud Application Deployment Process (Process Flow Computation)

The cloud deployment process typically follows the **6-stage Plan-Design-Deploy lifecycle**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Deployment Time} \; T_{\text{total}} &= T_{\text{req}} + T_{\text{arch}} + T_{\text{prov}} + T_{\text{test}} + T_{\text{deploy}} + T_{\text{monitor}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Traditional (on-prem):** $T_{\text{req}} = 4 \text{ weeks}$, $T_{\text{arch}} = 3 \text{ weeks}$, $T_{\text{prov}} = 6 \text{ weeks}$ (hardware lead time), $T_{\text{test}} = 2 \text{ weeks}$, $T_{\text{deploy}} = 1 \text{ week}$, $T_{\text{monitor}} = \text{continuous}$.

$$
T_{\text{traditional}} \approx 16 \text{ weeks} \;\; (4 \text{ months})
$$

**Cloud-native:** $T_{\text{req}} = 3 \text{ days}$, $T_{\text{arch}} = 5 \text{ days}$, $T_{\text{prov}} = 10 \text{ minutes (automated via Terraform/IaC)}$, $T_{\text{test}} = 4 \text{ days}$, $T_{\text{deploy}} = 2 \text{ days (CI/CD)}$.

$$
T_{\text{cloud}} \approx 14 \text{ days} \;\; (2 \text{ weeks})
$$

$$
\text{Speed Gain} = \frac{16 \times 7 - 14}{16 \times 7} \times 100 = \frac{112 - 14}{112} \times 100 \approx 87.5\%
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Decision Criterion | Public Cloud | Private Cloud | Hybrid Cloud |
|--------------------|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Capital Cost | Very Low | Very High | Medium |
| Control & Customization | Low | Very High | High |
| Scalability | Unlimited | Limited (by capacity) | High |
| Security & Compliance | Medium | Very High | High |
| Time to Deploy | Minutes–Hours | Weeks–Months | Days–Weeks |
| Best For | Web apps, SaaS | Defense, Banking | BFSI, Healthcare |
| Example Provider | AWS, Azure | OpenStack, VMware | AWS Outposts, Azure Arc |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cloud Deployment Models — Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Organization Workload] --> B{Deployment Decision}
    B -->|Public Internet Access| C[Public Cloud - AWS Azure GCP]
    B -->|Sensitive Regulated Data| D[Private Cloud - On-Premises VMware]
    B -->|Mixed Workload Strategy| E[Hybrid Cloud - Burst Out to Public]
    B -->|Shared Industry Interest| F[Community Cloud - Govt Healthcare]
    B -->|Vendor Diversification| G[Multi-Cloud - Two or More Providers]
    C --> C1[Pros: Low Cost, High Elasticity]
    C --> C2[Cons: Less Control]
    D --> D1[Pros: Full Control, High Security]
    D --> D2[Cons: High CapEx, Limited Scale]
    E --> E1[Pros: Best of Both Worlds]
    E --> E2[Cons: Complex Orchestration]
    F --> F1[Pros: Shared Cost, Domain Compliance]
    G --> G1[Pros: No Vendor Lock-in, Resilient]
```

## 4.2 Cloud Application Deployment Process — Lifecycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 - Requirements Analysis] --> S2[Stage 2 - Architecture Design]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 - Provision Resources via IaC]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 - Testing and Validation]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 - Deployment via CI CD]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6 - Monitoring and Auto-Scaling]
    S6 -->|Feedback Loop| S2
    S1:::stage
    S2:::stage
    S3:::stage
    S4:::stage
    S5:::stage
    S6:::stage
    classDef stage fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Service Models — Responsibility Matrix (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Applications] --> L2[Data]
    L2 --> L3[Runtime]
    L3 --> L4[Middleware]
    L4 --> L5[Operating System]
    L5 --> L6[Virtualization]
    L6 --> L7[Servers Storage Networking]
    R1[SaaS - Provider Manages] -.-> L1
    R2[PaaS - Provider Manages] -.-> L3
    R3[IaaS - Provider Manages] -.-> L6
    U1[User Manages in SaaS] -.-> L1
    U2[User Manages in PaaS] -.-> L1
    U3[User Manages in IaaS] -.-> L2
```

## 4.4 Cloud Migration Strategy — The 6R Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[Legacy Application] --> Q1{Changes to Code?}
    Q1 -->|None| R1[Rehost - Lift and Shift to EC2]
    Q1 -->|Minor| Q2{Move Platform?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R2[Replatform - Move to RDS Managed]
    Q2 -->|No| R3[Refactor - Optimize for Cloud Native]
    Q1 -->|Major| Q4{Architecture Change?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| R4[Re-architect - Move to Microservices]
    Q4 -->|Replace| R5[Replace - Use SaaS like Salesforce]
    Q1 -->|Retire| R6[Retire - Decommission App]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Common Question:** *"Differentiate Rehosting, Replatforming, and Refactoring."* This 6R framework is the standard answer framework used by AWS, Azure, and Google — perfect for 7-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**List any five essential characteristics of cloud computing as per the NIST definition.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **On-Demand Self-Service:** Consumers provision compute resources automatically without human interaction.
2. **Broad Network Access:** Services are available over the network and accessed through standard mechanisms (HTTP, REST) by thin/thick clients.
3. **Resource Pooling:** Provider's resources are pooled to serve multiple consumers using a multi-tenant model with physical/virtual resources dynamically reassigned.
4. **Rapid Elasticity:** Capabilities can be scaled out/in rapidly and automatically to match demand.
5. **Measured Service:** Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging metering at an appropriate level of abstraction (pay-per-use).

*(1 mark for naming, 2 marks for correctly explaining any 3 characteristics)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between Scalability and Elasticity in cloud computing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Scalability | Elasticity |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| Definition | Ability to **handle increasing load** by adding resources | Ability to **match resources with actual demand** dynamically |
| Direction | Mostly scale-up/out (long-term growth) | Both scale-up and scale-down (matches demand curve) |
| Time Horizon | Planned, weeks-months | Real-time, minutes-seconds |
| Trigger | Capacity planning forecast | Live traffic / auto-scaling policy |
| Example | Adding 100 servers for Black Friday | Auto-scaling group scaling from 2→50→2 instances |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for the table/difference)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Theory

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
> *(a)* Explain in detail the **four major cloud deployment models** (Public, Private, Hybrid, Community) with their merits, demerits, and suitable use cases. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Describe the **step-by-step cloud application deployment process** with a neat block diagram, clearly stating the role of **Infrastructure as Code (IaC)** tools like Terraform or AWS CloudFormation. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Four Deployment Models (7 marks)

**(i) Public Cloud (2 marks):**
- Infrastructure is owned by a third-party provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) and shared among multiple tenants.
- *Merits:* No CapEx, infinite scalability, high reliability, pay-per-use.
- *Demerits:* Less control, data sovereignty concerns, shared security.
- *Use Case:* Web applications, SaaS products, startups.

**(ii) Private Cloud (1.5 marks):**
- Infrastructure is dedicated to a single organization — either on-premises or hosted by a third party.
- *Merits:* Full control, strong security and compliance, custom configurations.
- *Demerits:* Very high CapEx and OpEx, requires skilled in-house team, limited scalability.
- *Use Case:* Banking, defense, government sensitive workloads.

**(iii) Hybrid Cloud (2 marks):**
- Combines private cloud (for sensitive data) with public cloud (for elastic workloads) using secure connectivity (VPN, Direct Connect, ExpressRoute).
- *Merits:* Best of both worlds, burst to public during peaks, gradual migration path.
- *Demerits:* Complex orchestration, requires unified identity, data transfer costs.
- *Use Case:* BFSI, healthcare, e-commerce flash sales.

**(iv) Community Cloud (1.5 marks):**
- Shared by several organizations with common concerns (mission, security, compliance, jurisdiction).
- *Merits:* Cost shared across consortium, domain-specific compliance.
- *Demerits:* Governance challenges, limited geographical availability.
- *Use Case:* Government e-governance platforms, inter-hospital medical records.

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Cloud Application Deployment Process (7 marks)

The cloud application deployment process consists of **6 sequential stages**:

1. **Requirements Analysis (1 mark):** Identify functional & non-functional requirements, SLA targets, compliance, and budget.
2. **Architecture Design (1 mark):** Choose the service model (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS), design network topology, select regions/AZs for HA.
3. **Resource Provisioning using IaC (2 marks):** Write declarative templates (Terraform `.tf` files or AWS CloudFormation YAML) to provision VPC, subnets, EC2, RDS, security groups. *IaC ensures idempotency, version control, and reproducibility.*

   Example Terraform pseudo-structure:

   ```hcl
   resource "aws_instance" "web" {
     ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
     instance_type = "t2.micro"
     tags = { Name = "WebServer" }
   }
   ```

4. **Testing (1 mark):** Unit tests, integration tests, load tests (using AWS Load Testing or JMeter), security scans.
5. **Deployment via CI/CD (1 mark):** Use Jenkins/GitHub Actions/GitLab CI to push code → Docker container → Amazon ECR → ECS/EKS/Lambda. Blue-green or canary deployment strategies minimize downtime.
6. **Monitoring & Auto-Scaling (1 mark):** CloudWatch/Stackdriver collects metrics, triggers alarms, scales EC2 via Auto Scaling Groups, heals failed instances.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often confuse **CloudFormation (AWS-specific)** with **Terraform (multi-cloud)**. If the question says "AWS-only", use CloudFormation; otherwise, default to Terraform for a generic answer. **Loses 1 mark** otherwise.
> - Drawing the deployment process as a **flowchart** (Start → 6 stages → End with feedback loop) earns **+1 mark** for presentation.
> - Forgetting to mention **rollback strategy** in CI/CD costs **1 mark**. Always mention blue-green or canary deployment.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Comprehensive Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> *(a)* Discuss the **major advantages of cloud computing** in detail. Categorize them into **Economic, Technical, and Operational** benefits with at least three points in each category. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A startup requires **20 virtual servers** running **24×7 for 1 year**. Two options are available:
> - **Option 1 — On-Premises:** Buy servers at **\$3,000 each**, hire **2 sysadmins at \$20,000/year each**, pay **\$30,000/year** for power/cooling, and **\$15,000/year** for datacenter rent. Assume a 3-year lifecycle for hardware.
> - **Option 2 — Cloud:** Pay **\$0.15 per hour** per server, billed continuously.
>   Calculate the **1-year TCO** for both options and identify which is cheaper. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Advantages of Cloud Computing (7 marks)

**Economic Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Reduced Capital Expenditure (CapEx):** No upfront hardware investment.
2. **Pay-per-Use Pricing:** Costs match actual consumption; idle resources cost nothing.
3. **Economies of Scale:** Aggregated purchasing power leads to lower per-unit costs.

**Technical Advantages (2.5 marks):**
1. **Elastic Scalability:** Resources scale up/down on demand via auto-scaling groups.
2. **High Availability & Reliability:** Multi-AZ, multi-region deployments provide 99.99% uptime.
3. **Automatic Updates & Patching:** Provider maintains hypervisors, OS, and managed services.
4. **Global Reach:** Deploy in any region worldwide within minutes.

**Operational Advantages (2.5 marks):**
1. **Faster Deployment:** Provisioning time reduced from weeks to minutes.
2. **Disaster Recovery & Business Continuity:** Built-in backup, snapshots, and geographic replication.
3. **Focus on Core Innovation:** DevOps teams avoid undifferentiated heavy lifting (hardware maintenance).
4. **Improved Collaboration:** Cloud-based tools (Office 365, Slack) enable distributed teams.

#### Model Solution — Part (b): TCO Calculation (7 marks)

**Option 1 — On-Premises (3.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hardware Cost (amortized over 3 years)} &= \frac{20 \times 3000}{3} = \frac{60{,}000}{3} = \$20{,}000/\text{year} \\
\text{Personnel Cost} &= 2 \times 20{,}000 = \$40{,}000/\text{year} \\
\text{Power and Cooling} &= \$30{,}000/\text{year} \\
\text{Datacenter Rent} &= \$15{,}000/\text{year} \\[4pt]
\text{TCO}_{\text{on-prem, 1yr}} &= 20{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 15{,}000 \\
&= \$105{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Option 2 — Cloud (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hours per Year} &= 365 \times 24 = 8760 \text{ hours} \\
\text{Cost per Server per Year} &= 8760 \times 0.15 = \$1314 \\
\text{Total Cloud TCO (20 servers)} &= 20 \times 1314 \\
&= \$26{,}280
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison & Decision (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings with Cloud} &= 105{,}000 - 26{,}280 = \$78{,}720 \\
\text{Percentage Savings} &= \frac{78{,}720}{105{,}000} \times 100 \approx 74.97\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Decision:** The **Cloud option** is significantly cheaper for 1-year TCO, with approximately **75% cost savings** and zero upfront capital investment. However, for long-term steady workloads (>3 years), on-premises may become competitive.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Forgetting to amortize hardware cost over its lifecycle** is the #1 mistake. If the question says "3-year lifecycle", you MUST divide the hardware cost by 3. Otherwise, **lose 2 marks**.
> - Cloud billing is in **HOURS, not days**. Use $365 \times 24 = 8760$ — not 365. Common error → **lose 1 mark**.
> - Always state **assumptions** (e.g., "assuming cloud egress is free for internal traffic") to demonstrate analytical maturity → **+1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> 📌 **Print this section for last-minute revision before the exam!**

- [ ] **NIST Definition** = on-demand, self-service, pay-per-use, pooled resources, minimal management.
- [ ] **5 Essential Characteristics** = On-Demand Self-Service, Broad Network Access, Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, Measured Service.
- [ ] **3 Service Models** = IaaS (you manage most), PaaS (provider manages platform), SaaS (you only use).
- [ ] **4 Deployment Models** = Public (3rd-party), Private (single org), Hybrid (Public+Private), Community (shared consortium). Add **Multi-Cloud** (multiple providers).
- [ ] **6R Migration Strategy** = Rehost, Replatform, Refactor, Re-architect, Replace, Retire.
- [ ] **Deployment Process** = Requirements → Architecture → Provisioning (IaC) → Testing → Deployment (CI/CD) → Monitoring.
- [ ] **IaC Tools** = Terraform (multi-cloud), AWS CloudFormation (AWS-specific), Ansible, Pulumi.
- [ ] **SLA Formula:** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}}$.
- [ ] **Yearly hours = 8760**; **Downtime/year = 8760 × (1 − Availability)**.
- [ ] **Cloud Billing Units:** EC2 = per hour/second; S3 = per GB-month; Lambda = per GB-second; Egress = per GB.
- [ ] **Key AWS Numbers to Memorize:** 99.9% = 8.76 hr/yr, 99.95% = 4.38 hr/yr, 99.99% = 52.6 min/yr, 99.999% (Five 9s) = 5.26 min/yr.
- [ ] **Migration case study ready:** Google "Dropbox's migration from AWS to its own infrastructure" or **"Netflix's migration to AWS"** — both are favorite KTU short-answer examples.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** For any 7-mark deployment question, always draw a **flowchart with 6 stages + feedback loop**, mention **IaC by name (Terraform/CloudFormation)**, and close with a line on **monitoring + auto-scaling**. This formula has consistently scored **7/7** in KTU board valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Public Cloud Platform Features - AWS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT745 - CLOUD, MICROSERVICES AND APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Public Cloud Platform Features - AWS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Public Cloud Platform?

A **Public Cloud Platform** is a cloud computing model in which a third-party provider (such as Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure, or Google Cloud Platform) delivers compute, storage, networking, and application services over the public internet to multiple tenants (organizations or individuals) on a pay-per-use basis. The infrastructure is owned, managed, and operated by the cloud provider, while the customer shares these resources in a multi-tenant environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"A public cloud is a model where cloud services are delivered over the public internet and shared across multiple organizations. The infrastructure is owned by the cloud provider, who is responsible for its maintenance, while customers provision resources on-demand."*

## 1.2 What is AWS (Amazon Web Services)?

**Amazon Web Services (AWS)** is the world's most comprehensive and broadly adopted cloud platform, offered by Amazon.com. Launched in **2006**, AWS provides over **200+ fully featured services** from data centers globally, offering compute power, storage, databases, machine learning, analytics, networking, security, IoT, and developer tools.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Characteristics of AWS as a Public Cloud Platform:**
> - **On-Demand Self-Service** – Provision resources automatically without human interaction.
> - **Broad Network Access** – Services available over the internet via standard mechanisms.
> - **Resource Pooling** – Multi-tenant model with resources dynamically assigned.
> - **Rapid Elasticity** – Scale up/down/out/in based on demand.
> - **Measured Service** – Pay-as-you-go metering at appropriate granularity.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: AWS as the "Electricity Grid of Computing"

Imagine you are a startup founder in 1995. To run a simple web application, you had to:
1. Buy physical servers (huge upfront capital).
2. Pay for electricity, cooling, and physical space.
3. Hire a system administrator 24/7.
4. Guess how many servers you'd need for the next year (and risk over/under-provisioning).

Now, think of **AWS as the Electricity Grid**:
- **The Power Plant (AWS Data Centers)** generates the compute "electricity."
- **The Grid (Internet)** delivers it to your "home" (application).
- **The Meter (AWS Billing Console)** measures how much you used.
- **The Bill (Monthly Invoice)** charges you only for what you consumed.
- **The Light Switch (API Call / Console Click)** turns resources on/off in seconds.

You no longer worry about *generating* the compute. You simply *consume* it like a utility, focusing only on building your product. This is the **utility computing paradigm** that AWS pioneered.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** "AWS = Computing-as-a-Utility" – just as you don't own a power plant to use electricity, you don't own servers to use AWS.

## 1.4 Why AWS Dominates the Public Cloud Market

According to industry reports (e.g., Gartner, Synergy Research), AWS consistently holds **~30–32% market share** in the global cloud infrastructure market — the highest among all providers. Key reasons include:

- **First-Mover Advantage** (since 2006).
- **Breadth of Services** (200+ services vs. competitors' ~100–150).
- **Mature Ecosystem** (largest community, third-party integrations, AWS Marketplace).
- **Global Reach** (33+ Regions, 100+ Availability Zones, 600+ Edge Locations).
- **Enterprise Trust** (used by Netflix, Airbnb, NASA, Pfizer, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of the AWS Well-Architected Framework

AWS officially defines its design principles through the **AWS Well-Architected Framework**, which consists of **five pillars**. Every KTU exam question on "AWS features" expects you to know these pillars.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Pillars of the AWS Well-Architected Framework:**
> 1. **Operational Excellence** – Run and monitor systems to deliver business value.
> 2. **Security** – Protect information, systems, and assets.
> 3. **Reliability** – Recover from failures and meet demand.
> 4. **Performance Efficiency** – Use IT resources efficiently to meet system requirements.
> 5. **Cost Optimization** – Deliver business value at the lowest price point.

## 2.2 Core AWS Service Categories (The "Building Blocks")

### 2.2.1 Compute Services
- **Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud):** Virtual servers in the cloud. Provides resizable compute capacity. Configurable CPU, RAM, storage, networking.
- **AWS Lambda:** Serverless compute — run code in response to events without provisioning servers. Billed in **milliseconds**.
- **Amazon ECS / EKS:** Managed container services (Docker / Kubernetes).
- **AWS Elastic Beanstalk:** Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) for deploying web apps.

### 2.2.2 Storage Services
- **Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service):** Object storage with **99.999999999% (11 nines) durability**. Industry standard for backup, archiving, big data, static websites.
- **Amazon EBS (Elastic Block Store):** Block storage for EC2 (like a virtual hard disk).
- **Amazon EFS (Elastic File System):** Shared file storage (NFS) for multiple EC2 instances.
- **Amazon Glacier:** Low-cost archival storage (retrieval in minutes to hours).

### 2.2.3 Database Services
- **Amazon RDS:** Managed relational databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, MariaDB, Aurora).
- **Amazon DynamoDB:** Managed NoSQL key-value/document database with single-digit millisecond latency.
- **Amazon Redshift:** Cloud data warehouse for analytics.
- **Amazon Aurora:** MySQL/PostgreSQL-compatible relational DB with 5x performance.

### 2.2.4 Networking Services
- **Amazon VPC (Virtual Private Cloud):** Isolated virtual network in the AWS cloud.
- **Amazon Route 53:** Scalable Domain Name System (DNS) service.
- **Elastic Load Balancer (ELB):** Distributes traffic across multiple EC2 instances.
- **Amazon CloudFront:** Content Delivery Network (CDN) caching content at edge locations.

### 2.2.5 Security, Identity & Compliance
- **AWS IAM (Identity and Access Management):** Manages users, groups, roles, and granular permissions.
- **AWS Shield / WAF:** DDoS protection and Web Application Firewall.
- **AWS KMS (Key Management Service):** Managed encryption key creation and control.
- **AWS CloudTrail:** Audit logging of API calls.

### 2.2.6 Monitoring & Management
- **Amazon CloudWatch:** Metrics, logs, alarms, and dashboards.
- **AWS CloudFormation:** Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC) for declarative resource provisioning.
- **AWS Trusted Advisor:** Automated cost, performance, security checks.

## 2.3 AWS Global Infrastructure (Critical for KTU)

AWS organizes its infrastructure in a **three-tier hierarchy**:

1. **Regions** – A physical geographic location (e.g., `ap-south-1` Mumbai). Each region is completely independent.
2. **Availability Zones (AZs)** – One or more discrete data centers within a Region, each with redundant power, networking, and connectivity. Each Region has a minimum of **2** and typically **3** AZs.
3. **Edge Locations / Points of Presence (PoPs)** – Data centers used by CloudFront (CDN) to deliver content with low latency. There are **600+ Edge Locations** globally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Rule:** A Region has at least 2 AZs. AZs within a Region are connected via low-latency links (typically < 1 ms RTT), enabling **High Availability** multi-AZ architectures.

## 2.4 AWS Pricing Models (The "How You Pay")

| **Pricing Model**        | **Description**                                                                 | **Best For**                  |
|--------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Pay-As-You-Go (On-Demand)** | Pay only for what you use, by the second/hour. No upfront commitment.       | Short-term, unpredictable workloads |
| **Reserved Instances (RI)**   | 1-year or 3-year commitment for up to **72% discount**.                      | Steady-state workloads        |
| **Savings Plans**         | Flexible pricing model offering discounts for committed compute usage.         | Long-term flexible workloads  |
| **Spot Instances**        | Bid for spare EC2 capacity at up to **90% discount**; can be interrupted.      | Fault-tolerant, flexible jobs |
| **Dedicated Hosts**       | Physical server dedicated for your use (compliance / licensing).              | BYOL (Bring Your Own License) |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Memory Aid:** Think of AWS pricing like a hotel — *On-Demand = Walking in and paying rack rate*, *Reserved = Booking a package deal in advance*, *Spot = Last-minute auction for a vacated room.*

## 2.5 The AWS Shared Responsibility Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a HIGH-FREQUENCY KTU exam topic.** Know the boundary clearly.

- **AWS is responsible for "Security OF the Cloud"** – Physical data centers, hardware, network, hypervisor.
- **Customer is responsible for "Security IN the Cloud"** – Data, IAM, OS patching, firewall rules, encryption configuration.

| **Layer**                     | **AWS Responsibility** | **Customer Responsibility** |
|-------------------------------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| Physical Data Center          | ✅                      | ❌                            |
| Network Infrastructure        | ✅                      | ❌                            |
| Hypervisor / Compute          | ✅                      | ❌                            |
| Operating System              | ❌                      | ✅ (for EC2)                  |
| Application Code              | ❌                      | ✅                            |
| Data Encryption               | Partial (services provided) | ✅ (configuration & keys) |
| Identity & Access (IAM)       | Service provided       | ✅ (policy configuration)     |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the quantitative formulas and rules-of-thumb you MUST memorize for solving AWS-related calculation problems in KTU exams.

| **Concept**                      | **Formula / Rule**                                                                                          | **Typical Unit**      |
|----------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------|
| **On-Demand Cost**               | $\text{Cost} = \text{Hourly Rate} \times \text{Number of Instances} \times \text{Total Hours Used}$        | USD                   |
| **Reserved Instance Savings**    | $\text{Savings \%} = \dfrac{\text{On-Demand Cost} - \text{RI Cost}}{\text{On-Demand Cost}} \times 100$      | Percentage            |
| **S3 Storage Classes (Durability)**| Standard: **99.999999999% (11 nines)** ; Standard-IA: **99.999999999%**; One Zone-IA: **99.9999999% (9 nines)** | Percentage            |
| **S3 Availability (Standard)**  | Designed for **99.99% availability**                                                                       | Percentage            |
| **EC2 SLA (single instance)**    | **90%** monthly uptime; Multi-AZ: **99.99%**                                                              | Percentage            |
| **DynamoDB SLA**                 | **99.99%** (Multi-AZ)                                                                                      | Percentage            |
| **CDN Cache Hit Ratio**          | $\text{Hit Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Cache Hits}}{\text{Cache Hits} + \text{Cache Misses}} \times 100$         | Percentage            |
| **Data Transfer Cost**           | **Inbound:** Free; **Outbound (Internet):** Tiered; **AZ-to-AZ:** \$0.01/GB each way                     | USD per GB            |
| **AWS Region Availability**      | $\text{Multi-Region Uptime} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - U_i)$ where $U_i$ is per-Region uptime               | Probability           |
| **Scaling Threshold Formula**    | $\text{Target Tracking} = \text{Desired Metric Value} \pm \text{Tolerance Band}$                            | Metric value          |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT confuse S3 Durability with Availability.** Durability = "Will my data be lost?" (e.g., 11 nines). Availability = "Can I read my data right now?" (e.g., 99.99%). KTU examiners love this trap.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The features of AWS as a public cloud platform directly enable:
- **Startups** to launch MVPs in hours instead of months.
- **Enterprises** to run **disaster recovery (DR)** across continents.
- **Data scientists** to train ML models on GPU clusters (P4d, P5 instances).
- **IoT developers** to ingest billions of device messages via AWS IoT Core.
- **DevOps teams** to implement CI/CD pipelines with CodePipeline, CodeBuild, CodeDeploy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1: Calculating Monthly EC2 Cost with Multiple Pricing Models

**Scenario:** A KTU student runs a research project that needs:
- 2 × `t3.medium` EC2 instances.
- Running **24 hours/day for 30 days**.
- Hourly On-Demand rate = **\$0.0416/hour**.
- The student buys a **1-year No-Upfront Reserved Instance** at **\$0.026/hour**.
- Compute the monthly savings.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Compute Total On-Demand Cost for 1 instance for 30 days.**

$$
\text{Hours per Month} = 24 \times 30 = 720 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{1 instance, On-Demand}} = 0.0416 \times 720 = 29.952 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Total On-Demand Cost for 2 instances for 30 days.**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{2 instances, On-Demand}} = 2 \times 29.952 = 59.904 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Reserved Instance Cost for 1 instance.**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{1 instance, RI}} = 0.026 \times 720 = 18.72 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Reserved Instance Cost for 2 instances.**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{2 instances, RI}} = 2 \times 18.72 = 37.44 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Monthly Savings in absolute terms.**

$$
\text{Savings}_{\text{abs}} = 59.904 - 37.44 = 22.464 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 6: Compute Percentage Savings.**

$$
\text{Savings}_{\%} = \dfrac{59.904 - 37.44}{59.904} \times 100 = \dfrac{22.464}{59.904} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Savings}_{\%} \approx 37.5\%
$$

**Final Answer:** Monthly savings = **\$22.464**, which is approximately **37.5%** cheaper than On-Demand pricing.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2: AWS S3 Storage Class Selection and Cost Optimization

**Scenario:** A healthcare startup has the following data:
- 5 TB of **frequently accessed** patient records (read/written daily).
- 20 TB of **infrequently accessed** medical images (read once every 30+ days).
- 50 TB of **archival** X-ray records (compliance retention, accessed < 1×/year).

The student must recommend the correct S3 storage class to minimize cost.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: List AWS S3 Storage Classes and their use cases (Key-Mapping).**

| **S3 Storage Class**                | **Use Case**                              | **Durability**         | **Retrieval Time** |
|------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|------------------------|--------------------|
| **S3 Standard**                    | Frequently accessed data                  | 99.999999999% (11 nines) | Milliseconds       |
| **S3 Standard-IA**                 | Infrequently accessed, but rapid retrieval | 99.999999999% (11 nines) | Milliseconds       |
| **S3 One Zone-IA**                 | Infrequently accessed, non-critical       | 99.9999999% (9 nines)  | Milliseconds       |
| **S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval**   | Archive with instant access               | 99.999999999%           | Milliseconds       |
| **S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval**  | Archive, retrieval in minutes to hours    | 99.999999999%           | Minutes to hours   |
| **S3 Glacier Deep Archive**        | Long-term archive, 7–12 hour retrieval    | 99.999999999%           | 12+ hours          |

**Step 2: Match each dataset to the appropriate storage class.**

- **5 TB frequently accessed** → **S3 Standard** (handy for millisecond latency on every read/write).
- **20 TB infrequently accessed (30+ days)** → **S3 Standard-IA** (lower cost than Standard; charges a retrieval fee).
- **50 TB archival (< 1×/year)** → **S3 Glacier Deep Archive** (lowest cost; 12-hour retrieval acceptable for compliance).

**Step 3: Cost Calculation (illustrative; rates vary by region).**

Assume (Mumbai region, approximate rates):
- S3 Standard: **\$0.023/GB/month**
- S3 Standard-IA: **\$0.0125/GB/month** + retrieval fee
- S3 Glacier Deep Archive: **\$0.00099/GB/month**

Convert TB → GB: 1 TB = 1024 GB.

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{Standard}} = 5 \times 1024 \times 0.023 = 117.76 \text{ USD/month}
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{Standard-IA}} = 20 \times 1024 \times 0.0125 = 256.00 \text{ USD/month}
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{Glacier DA}} = 50 \times 1024 \times 0.00099 = 50.69 \text{ USD/month}
$$

**Step 4: Total Monthly Storage Cost.**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{Total}} = 117.76 + 256.00 + 50.69 = 424.45 \text{ USD/month}
$$

**Final Answer:** Total = **\$424.45/month**, achieved by intelligently mapping datasets to the right storage classes.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Engineering Principle:** The S3 cost-optimization lesson generalizes to all cloud economics — *match service tier to access pattern*. KTU examiners often ask this as a "design question" worth 7 marks.

---

## 3.3 Problem 3: Availability Calculation for Multi-AZ Architecture

**Scenario:** An e-commerce application is deployed across **2 Availability Zones** in a single AWS Region. Each AZ has an independent monthly uptime of **99.5%**. Compute the combined system availability.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Define Unavailability of each AZ.**

$$
\text{Unavailability}_{AZ1} = 1 - 0.995 = 0.005
$$

$$
\text{Unavailability}_{AZ2} = 1 - 0.995 = 0.005
$$

**Step 2: Combined system fails only if BOTH AZs fail (assuming independent failures).**

$$
\text{Unavailability}_{\text{System}} = 0.005 \times 0.005 = 0.000025
$$

**Step 3: Compute System Availability.**

$$
\text{Availability}_{\text{System}} = 1 - 0.000025 = 0.999975
$$

**Step 4: Convert to percentage.**

$$
\text{Availability}_{\%} = 0.999975 \times 100 = 99.9975\%
$$

**Final Answer:** Combined system availability = **99.9975%**, which is higher than the standard AWS Multi-AZ SLA of **99.99%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalization:** For $n$ independent AZs each with availability $U$, system availability is computed using the formula $A_{\text{system}} = 1 - (1 - U)^n$. This is the same formula used for parallel reliability in engineering systems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: AWS vs. Other Public Cloud Providers

| **Feature**              | **AWS**                  | **Microsoft Azure**        | **Google Cloud (GCP)**      |
|--------------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Market Share (2024)**  | **~31%**                 | ~25%                       | ~11%                        |
| **No. of Services**      | 200+                     | 200+                       | 100+                        |
| **Regions (2024)**       | 33+                      | 60+                        | 40+                         |
| **Flagship Compute**     | EC2                      | Azure VMs                  | Compute Engine              |
| **Serverless**           | Lambda                   | Azure Functions            | Cloud Functions             |
| **Object Storage**       | S3 (industry standard)  | Blob Storage               | Cloud Storage               |
| **Managed Kubernetes**   | EKS                      | AKS                        | GKE (pioneer, open-source)  |
| **Leading ML/AI Service**| SageMaker, Bedrock       | Azure ML, Azure OpenAI     | Vertex AI, Gemini           |
| **Strength**             | **Breadth & maturity**   | Hybrid + Microsoft stack   | Data analytics & AI         |
| **Weakness**             | Complex pricing          | Azure-specific ecosystem   | Smaller market share        |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exams, the **Take-Away** is: *AWS is the most mature, broadest, and most adopted public cloud platform*, with a strong lead in service variety and global infrastructure.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AWS Global Infrastructure Hierarchy

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **three-tier AWS Global Infrastructure** from the largest (Region) to the smallest (Edge Location).

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[AWS Global Cloud Infrastructure] --> nodeB[Region 1 e.g. ap-south-1 Mumbai]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Region 2 e.g. us-east-1 N Virginia]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Region 3 e.g. eu-west-1 Ireland]

    nodeB --> nodeE[Availability Zone AZ1]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Availability Zone AZ2]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Availability Zone AZ3]

    nodeE --> nodeH[Data Center DC1]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Data Center DC2]

    nodeF --> nodeJ[Data Center DC3]
    nodeF --> nodeK[Data Center DC4]

    nodeA --> nodeL[Edge Locations 600 Plus PoPs]
    nodeL --> nodeM[CloudFront CDN Caching]
    nodeL --> nodeN[Route 53 DNS Resolution]

    style nodeA fill:#FF9900,stroke:#232F3E,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style nodeB fill:#146EB4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeC fill:#146EB4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeD fill:#146EB4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeE fill:#7AA116,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeF fill:#7AA116,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeG fill:#7AA116,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeH fill:#D13212,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeI fill:#D13212,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeJ fill:#D13212,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeK fill:#D13212,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeL fill:#C925D1,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeM fill:#FF9900,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeN fill:#FF9900,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Reading Guide:**
- **Region** = a fully independent geographic area.
- **Availability Zone** = one or more data centers inside a Region.
- **Edge Location** = CDN cache used by CloudFront (NOT the same as a Region).
- All AZs within a Region are interconnected with **low-latency, high-bandwidth private links**.

---

## 4.2 AWS Shared Responsibility Model

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **boundary of responsibility** between AWS and the customer. This is one of the **most asked diagrams** in KTU exams.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeTop[AWS Shared Responsibility Model]

    nodeTop --> nodeLeft[AWS Responsibility Security OF the Cloud]
    nodeTop --> nodeRight[Customer Responsibility Security IN the Cloud]

    nodeLeft --> nodeL1[Physical Data Centers]
    nodeLeft --> nodeL2[Network Infrastructure]
    nodeLeft --> nodeL3[Hardware and Servers]
    nodeLeft --> nodeL4[Hypervisor Layer]
    nodeLeft --> nodeL5[Global Infrastructure Regions AZs]

    nodeRight --> nodeR1[Operating System Patching for EC2]
    nodeRight --> nodeR2[Application Code and Logic]
    nodeRight --> nodeR3[Data Encryption Configuration]
    nodeRight --> nodeR4[IAM User Role Policy Setup]
    nodeRight --> nodeR5[Security Group and Network ACL]
    nodeRight --> nodeR6[Customer Data and Backups]

    style nodeTop fill:#FF9900,stroke:#232F3E,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style nodeLeft fill:#1B660F,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeRight fill:#0E526C,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeL1 fill:#7AA116,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeL2 fill:#7AA116,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeL3 fill:#7AA116,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeL4 fill:#7AA116,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeL5 fill:#7AA116,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeR1 fill:#D13212,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeR2 fill:#D13212,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeR3 fill:#D13212,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeR4 fill:#D13212,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeR5 fill:#D13212,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeR6 fill:#D13212,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Reading Guide:**
- **Green (left)** = AWS manages.
- **Red (right)** = Customer manages.
- Note: For managed services (e.g., RDS, Lambda), AWS handles MORE of the stack (OS, patching), shifting customer responsibility further up the stack.

---

## 4.3 AWS Service Architecture Block Diagram (Layered View)

This diagram shows how AWS services are **categorized into functional layers**, which is helpful for KTU viva and conceptual questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeApp[Application Layer]
    nodeApp --> nodeApp1[SaaS Amazon Chime WorkDocs]
    nodeApp --> nodeApp2[Developer Tools CodePipeline CodeBuild]

    nodePlat[Platform Layer]
    nodePlat --> nodePlat1[AWS Elastic Beanstalk PaaS]
    nodePlat --> nodePlat2[Amazon RDS Managed Database]
    nodePlat --> nodePlat3[AWS Lambda Serverless]

    nodeInfra[Infrastructure Layer]
    nodeInfra --> nodeInfra1[Amazon EC2 Compute]
    nodeInfra --> nodeInfra2[Amazon S3 Storage]
    nodeInfra --> nodeInfra3[Amazon VPC Networking]

    nodeFound[Foundation Layer]
    nodeFound --> nodeFound1[AWS Global Infrastructure Regions AZs]
    nodeFound --> nodeFound2[AWS Identity and Access Management IAM]
    nodeFound --> nodeFound3[Billing and Cost Management]

    nodeApp --> nodePlat
    nodePlat --> nodeInfra
    nodeInfra --> nodeFound

    style nodeApp fill:#FF9900,stroke:#232F3E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodePlat fill:#146EB4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeInfra fill:#7AA116,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeFound fill:#D13212,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeApp1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeApp2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodePlat1 fill:#5DADE2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodePlat2 fill:#5DADE2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodePlat3 fill:#5DADE2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeInfra1 fill:#A9DF85,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeInfra2 fill:#A9DF85,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeInfra3 fill:#A9DF85,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeFound1 fill:#F1948A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeFound2 fill:#F1948A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeFound3 fill:#F1948A,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Reading Guide:**
- **Foundation Layer** = The bedrock (global infrastructure, IAM, billing).
- **Infrastructure Layer** = Raw primitives (EC2, S3, VPC).
- **Platform Layer** = Managed abstractions (Beanstalk, RDS, Lambda).
- **Application Layer** = End-user ready SaaS apps.

This hierarchy directly mirrors the **NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture** which is also a KTU-relevant concept.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
**Define the term "public cloud" and list any four essential characteristics of public cloud platforms as per NIST.**

**Model Answer:**

A **public cloud** is a cloud deployment model in which cloud services (compute, storage, applications) are delivered over the public internet by a third-party provider and shared among multiple organizations (tenants). The infrastructure is owned and managed by the cloud provider.

The **four essential characteristics** (per NIST SP 800-145) are:

1. **On-Demand Self-Service** – Consumers can provision computing capabilities automatically without human interaction with the service provider.
2. **Broad Network Access** – Capabilities are available over the network and accessed through standard mechanisms used by heterogeneous client platforms (mobile, laptop, PDA).
3. **Resource Pooling** – Provider resources are pooled to serve multiple consumers using a multi-tenant model with physical and virtual resources dynamically reassigned.
4. **Rapid Elasticity** – Capabilities can be elastically provisioned and released to scale rapidly with demand.
5. **Measured Service** – Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging metering at an appropriate level of abstraction.

*(Any four of the above. Marks awarded for: Definition 1m + 4 Characteristics × 0.5m = 3m)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**What is the AWS Shared Responsibility Model? Briefly explain who is responsible for what.**

**Model Answer:**

The **AWS Shared Responsibility Model** is a security and compliance framework that clearly divides security obligations between **AWS** and the **customer**.

- **AWS is responsible for "Security OF the Cloud":**
  - Physical security of data centers.
  - Hardware, servers, and networking infrastructure.
  - Hypervisor and managed service infrastructure.

- **Customer is responsible for "Security IN the Cloud":**
  - Data encryption (in transit and at rest).
  - Identity and Access Management (IAM) configuration.
  - Guest Operating System patching (for EC2).
  - Security groups, network ACLs, firewall rules.
  - Application code security.

> **Key Point:** The model shifts slightly based on the service. For example, in **AWS Lambda**, AWS manages the OS as well, so the customer's responsibility is limited mainly to code and IAM.

*(Marks: Definition 1m + AWS responsibilities 1m + Customer responsibilities 1m = 3m)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice Format

### **Question A (14 Marks): "AWS Service Portfolio and Architecture"**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1 Choice Pattern]**

**Q.A (a)** Describe the **five pillars of the AWS Well-Architected Framework** in detail. Why is this framework important for cloud architects? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **AWS Well-Architected Framework** describes the key principles and best practices for designing high-quality cloud workloads on AWS. It is built on **five pillars**:

**1. Operational Excellence (1.4m):**
- Focuses on **running and monitoring systems** to deliver business value and **continually improving** processes and procedures.
- Key principles: Perform operations as code, make frequent small reversible changes, refine operations procedures frequently, anticipate failure, learn from all operational failures.
- AWS services: **CloudFormation (IaC), CloudWatch (Monitoring), X-Ray (Tracing)**.

**2. Security (1.4m):**
- Focuses on protecting **information, systems, and assets** through risk assessments and mitigation strategies.
- Key principles: Implement a strong identity foundation, enable traceability, apply security at all layers, automate security best practices, protect data in transit and at rest, keep people away from data, prepare for security events.
- AWS services: **IAM, KMS, WAF, Shield, CloudTrail, Macie**.

**3. Reliability (1.4m):**
- Focuses on ensuring the workload performs its intended function **correctly and consistently**, including recovering from failures and meeting demand.
- Key principles: Automatically recover from failure, test recovery procedures, scale horizontally to increase aggregate system availability, stop guessing capacity, manage change via automation.
- AWS services: **Auto Scaling, Multi-AZ deployment, CloudFormation, Route 53**.

**4. Performance Efficiency (1.4m):**
- Focuses on using **IT and computing resources efficiently** to meet system requirements and **maintaining efficiency as demand changes and technologies evolve**.
- Key principles: Democratize advanced technologies, go global in minutes, use serverless architectures, experiment more often, consider mechanical sympathy.
- AWS services: **Lambda (serverless), CloudFront (CDN), Auto Scaling, ElastiCache**.

**5. Cost Optimization (1.4m):**
- Focuses on **delivering business value at the lowest price point**, understanding and controlling where money is being spent, and optimizing over time.
- Key principles: Implement cloud financial management, adopt a consumption model, measure overall efficiency, stop spending on undifferentiated heavy lifting, analyze and attribute expenditure.
- AWS services: **Cost Explorer, Trusted Advisor, Reserved Instances, Spot Instances**.

**Importance of the Framework (Bonus – fetches 0.5m):**
- Provides a **consistent approach** for customers to evaluate architectures.
- Helps identify **high-risk areas** in design.
- Aligns with KTU CO (Course Outcome) of understanding **cloud design principles** and **best practices**.

---

**Q.A (b)** Compare the **AWS Global Infrastructure hierarchy** (Regions, Availability Zones, Edge Locations). Explain how an application deployed across multiple AZs achieves High Availability. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**AWS Global Infrastructure Hierarchy (4m):**

| **Tier**                    | **Definition**                                                                                                  | **Example**                          |
|-----------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Region**                  | A fully independent **geographic area** containing multiple AZs. Each Region is physically separated (e.g., across countries/continents). | `ap-south-1` (Mumbai), `us-east-1` (N. Virginia) |
| **Availability Zone (AZ)**  | One or more **discrete data centers** within a Region, each with independent power, cooling, and networking. AZs are connected via low-latency links (< 1 ms). | `ap-south-1a`, `ap-south-1b`         |
| **Edge Location / PoP**     | A data center used by **CloudFront (CDN)** to cache content closer to end-users. NOT the same as a Region.      | 600+ Edge Locations globally         |

**Multi-AZ High Availability Architecture (3m):**

1. The application is deployed in **two or more AZs within the same Region**.
2. A **Load Balancer (ELB)** distributes incoming traffic across the AZs.
3. **Auto Scaling** ensures new instances are launched in healthy AZs during failures.
4. **Data layer (RDS Multi-AZ, S3)** is replicated across AZs synchronously.
5. If one AZ fails (e.g., power outage, natural disaster), the other AZ continues serving traffic seamlessly.

**Result:** Combined availability ≈ **99.99%** (vs. **90%** for a single EC2 instance).

**Example Architecture Block Diagram (text form):**
- User → Route 53 → ELB → [EC2 in AZ-a] + [EC2 in AZ-b] → RDS Multi-AZ (Primary in AZ-a, Standby in AZ-b)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): "AWS Economics and Cost Optimization"**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice Pattern]**

**Q.B (a)** Explain the different **AWS pricing models** for EC2. How does the choice of pricing model impact the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) for a startup? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**AWS EC2 Pricing Models (5m):**

1. **On-Demand Instances (1.25m):**
   - Pay per second/hour with **no upfront commitment**.
   - Suitable for **short-term, irregular workloads** (e.g., development/testing).
   - Highest per-hour cost; e.g., `t3.medium` Linux = **\$0.0416/hour** (Mumbai).
   - Most flexible — can launch/terminate anytime.

2. **Reserved Instances (RI) (1.25m):**
   - **1-year or 3-year commitment** for up to **72% discount** vs. On-Demand.
   - Three payment options: All Upfront, Partial Upfront, No Upfront.
   - Suitable for **steady-state, predictable workloads**.
   - Tied to a specific instance family, Region, and tenancy.

3. **Savings Plans (1.25m):**
   - Flexible commitment model offering up to **66% discount** for a 1-year or 3-year term.
   - Automatically applies to any EC2 instance family, Region, or tenancy — no rigid specification.
   - Two types: **Compute Savings Plans** (most flexible), **EC2 Instance Savings Plans** (more savings, less flexible).

4. **Spot Instances (1.25m):**
   - Bid for **unused EC2 capacity** at up to **90% discount**.
   - AWS can reclaim the instance with a 2-minute warning if it needs the capacity back.
   - Suitable for **fault-tolerant, flexible workloads** (e.g., batch processing, ML training, CI/CD).

**Impact on TCO for a Startup (2m):**
- A startup with **unpredictable traffic** should start with **On-Demand** for the first 6 months.
- Once traffic patterns stabilize, **purchase RIs or Savings Plans** for the baseline capacity (e.g., 60–70% of usage).
- Use **Spot Instances** for non-critical background jobs (e.g., log analysis, image processing).
- This **layered pricing strategy** can reduce TCO by **40–60%** while maintaining full elasticity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing Savings Plans with Reserved Instances. While RIs are tied to a specific instance type, **Savings Plans apply across any instance family in any Region**, giving more flexibility. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if you interchange them.

---

**Q.B (b)** A startup plans to host 1 TB of user-uploaded images on AWS S3. The images are accessed frequently for the first 30 days and then rarely thereafter. **Recommend the most cost-effective S3 storage strategy** using **S3 Lifecycle Policies**, and justify your choice with a cost calculation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Recommended S3 Storage Strategy with Lifecycle Policy (4m):**

Use **S3 Lifecycle Rules** to automatically transition objects between storage classes based on their age:

| **Lifecycle Stage**   | **Storage Class**           | **Reasoning**                                              |
|-----------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| **0–30 days**         | **S3 Standard**              | High read/write frequency, millisecond latency required.   |
| **31–180 days**       | **S3 Standard-IA**           | Access drops significantly; lower storage cost, same durability. |
| **> 180 days**        | **S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval** | Archival access 1×/quarter; very low storage cost.   |
| **> 365 days (optional)** | **S3 Glacier Deep Archive** | Long-term compliance retention; lowest cost in AWS.        |

**S3 Lifecycle Policy (JSON-style representation):**
- **Rule 1:** Transition objects to **Standard-IA** after 30 days.
- **Rule 2:** Transition objects to **Glacier Flexible Retrieval** after 180 days.
- **Rule 3:** Transition to **Glacier Deep Archive** after 365 days (optional).

**Cost Calculation (3m):**

Assume (Mumbai region, approximate rates):
- S3 Standard: **\$0.023/GB/month**
- S3 Standard-IA: **\$0.0125/GB/month**
- S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval: **\$0.0036/GB/month**
- 1 TB = 1024 GB.

**Without Lifecycle Policy (All in Standard for 1 year):**
$$
\text{Cost} = 1024 \times 0.023 \times 12 = 282.62 \text{ USD}
$$

**With Lifecycle Policy:**
- Month 1 (Standard, 1024 GB): $1024 \times 0.023 = 23.55$
- Months 2–6 (Standard-IA, 5 months): $1024 \times 0.0125 \times 5 = 64.00$
- Months 7–12 (Glacier, 6 months): $1024 \times 0.0036 \times 6 = 22.12$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{total}} = 23.55 + 64.00 + 22.12 = 109.67 \text{ USD}
$$

**Savings:**
$$
\text{Savings} = 282.62 - 109.67 = 172.95 \text{ USD} \approx 61.2\% \text{ cheaper}
$$

**Final Answer:** Using a **lifecycle policy** reduces annual S3 cost by approximately **61%** while preserving all data.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often **forget to mention transition fees and minimum storage duration charges** for IA classes (30 days) and Glacier classes (90 days). Mention these or lose 1 mark. Also, **never claim S3 is a database** — it is **object storage**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — "Public Cloud Platform Features: AWS":**

- ✅ **Public Cloud =** Third-party-owned, internet-delivered, multi-tenant, pay-per-use.
- ✅ **AWS =** Most mature public cloud (since 2006), **200+ services**, ~**31% market share**.
- ✅ **Five Characteristics (NIST):** On-Demand Self-Service, Broad Network Access, Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, Measured Service.
- ✅ **AWS Global Infra:** Region (≥2 AZs) → AZ (1+ Data Centers) → Edge Locations (CDN).
- ✅ **5 Pillars of Well-Architected:** Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization.
- ✅ **Shared Responsibility:** AWS = Security OF Cloud; Customer = Security IN Cloud.
- ✅ **EC2 Pricing:** On-Demand (no commit), Reserved (up to 72% off), Savings Plans (up to 66%, flexible), Spot (up to 90% off, interruptible), Dedicated Hosts (compliance).
- ✅ **S3 Durability:** 11 nines (99.999999999%) for Standard, Standard-IA, Glacier classes. **Do NOT confuse with availability (99.99%).**
- ✅ **S3 Storage Classes:** Standard, Standard-IA, One Zone-IA, Glacier Instant/Flexible/Deep Archive — match to access pattern.
- ✅ **Lifecycle Policies** automate cost savings by transitioning objects across classes.
- ✅ **Multi-AZ deployment** boosts availability from **90%** to **99.99%** (formula: $1 - (1-U)^n$).
- ✅ **IAM** is the cornerstone of AWS security — least-privilege principle applies.
- ✅ **AWS Service Categories:** Compute (EC2, Lambda), Storage (S3, EBS, Glacier), Database (RDS, DynamoDB), Networking (VPC, CloudFront), Security (IAM, KMS, WAF), Monitoring (CloudWatch, CloudTrail).
- ✅ **Key Competitors:** Microsoft Azure (~25% market share), Google Cloud (~11%).
- ✅ **Remember the KTU motto for AWS questions:** *"Match the service to the workload, not the other way around."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Azure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT745 - CLOUD, MICROSERVICES AND APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Azure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microsoft Azure** is a **public cloud computing platform** operated by Microsoft that provides a broad and growing set of integrated cloud services — including compute, analytics, storage, networking, artificial intelligence (AI), Internet of Things (IoT), security, and developer tools — delivered over the internet through Microsoft-managed data centres spread across the globe.

For the KTU 2024 Scheme, Azure is categorised as a **hyperscale public cloud provider** alongside AWS (Amazon Web Services) and GCP (Google Cloud Platform). It supports the three canonical cloud service models:

- **Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)** — virtual machines, virtual networks, raw storage.
- **Platform as a Service (PaaS)** — Azure App Service, Azure SQL Database, Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS).
- **Software as a Service (SaaS)** — Microsoft 365, Dynamics 365, Teams.

Azure also provides a fourth model relevant for modern microservices:

- **Function as a Service (FaaS) / Serverless** — Azure Functions, Azure Logic Apps.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Azure as an Electricity Grid"**
> Think of Azure as a giant, on-demand **electricity grid for compute power**. Instead of buying and running your own diesel generator (on-premise servers) inside your home, you simply plug into the grid. The grid (Azure) manages the fuel source, the cables, the transformers, the safety switches, the billing meter, and even the renewable energy mix. You only pay for the units of electricity (CPU-hours, GB stored, GB transferred) that your appliances (your applications) actually consume. If you need more power for a Diwali celebration (Black Friday traffic), you just turn the dial up — you don't have to buy a new generator.

**Key Take-aways from the Analogy:**
- You don't manage the physical infrastructure (data centres, racks, cooling).
- Billing is **metered / consumption-based** (OpEx, not CapEx).
- Provisioning is **self-service and elastic**.
- Azure guarantees a **Service Level Agreement (SLA)** like a power company guarantees uptime.

### 1.3 Core Azure Terminology for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
