> **Must-Know Terminology (High Frequency in KTU Papers):**
>
> - **Region** — A geographical area on the planet containing one or more data centres (e.g., `Central India`, `East US`).
> - **Availability Zone (AZ)** — One or more physically separate, independent data centres within an Azure region, each with its own power, cooling, and networking.
> - **Region Pair** — Two regions within the same geography, paired for disaster recovery (e.g., `East US` ↔ `West US`).
> - **Resource Group (RG)** — A logical container that holds related Azure resources for an application.
> - **Subscription** — A billing and access boundary; one Azure account can contain multiple subscriptions.
> - **Azure Active Directory (Azure AD / Microsoft Entra ID)** — The identity and access management (IAM) service.
> - **Azure Resource Manager (ARM)** — The deployment and management service for Azure; all REST APIs, PowerShell cmdlets, and CLI commands pass through ARM.
> - **Tenant** — A dedicated and trusted instance of Azure AD representing a single organisation.

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Azure Responsibility

| Layer | Who Manages What? | Example Service |
|---|---|---|
| **Application & Data** | Customer | Your code, OS, middleware |
| **Runtime / Middleware** | Customer (PaaS) / Azure (SaaS) | App Service runtime, SQL engine |
| **OS, Virtualisation, Servers, Storage, Networking** | Azure (always) | Hosts, SAN, switches |
| **Physical Data Centre** | Azure (always) | Building, cooling, security |

This shared-responsibility model is a **favourite KTU 2-mark / 3-mark question**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Azure Architectural Stack (Bottom-Up)

> [!NOTE]
> Azure's architecture is best understood in **logical layers**, identical to the OSI-like view you studied in Computer Networks, but applied to cloud resources.

1. **Physical Layer** — Microsoft's data centres, hardware, optical fibres, power substations.
2. **Virtualisation Layer** — Hyper-V (Microsoft's hypervisor) creating Virtual Machines.
3. **Fabric Layer** — The **Azure Fabric Controller**, the distributed operating system that manages and orchestrates VMs, hosts, and services.
4. **Service Layer** — Publicly exposed services (Compute, Storage, Network, Database, AI/ML).
5. **Management Layer** — Azure Portal, Azure CLI, PowerShell, ARM templates, Bicep, Terraform.
6. **Security & Identity Layer** — Entra ID (Azure AD), Key Vault, Defender for Cloud, Sentinel.

### 2.2 Core Azure Services — KTU High-Yield Reference

#### 2.2.1 Azure Compute Services

| Service | Category | Use Case | KTU Memory Trick |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Virtual Machines (VMs)** | IaaS | Lift-and-shift migration, full OS control | "Your own rented computer" |
| **VM Scale Sets (VMSS)** | IaaS | Auto-scaling identical VMs | "Identical twins that clone on demand" |
| **App Service** | PaaS | Web apps, REST APIs, mobile backends | "Just deploy your code" |
| **Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)** | PaaS | Container orchestration (microservices) | "Managed Kubernetes" |
| **Container Instances (ACI)** | PaaS | Run a single container without orchestration | "Quick container" |
| **Azure Functions** | FaaS / Serverless | Event-driven, short-lived code | "Pay only when code runs" |
| **Azure Batch** | IaaS / PaaS | HPC, parallel batch jobs | "Big compute on tap" |

#### 2.2.2 Azure Storage Services

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Azure provides **four primary storage types**, each with a distinct access pattern. KTU repeatedly asks the difference between **Blob, Queue, File, and Table** storage.

| Storage Type | Data Structure | Access Pattern | Protocol | Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Blob Storage** | Unstructured (binary, JSON, video, images) | REST API over HTTP/HTTPS | HTTP/REST | "A giant hard drive on the internet" |
| **File Storage** | Hierarchical file shares | SMB 3.0, NFS | SMB/NFS | "A network-attached drive" |
| **Queue Storage** | Messages (up to 64 KB) | REST | HTTP/REST | "A post-office box for components" |
| **Table Storage** | NoSQL key-value / column-family | REST / OData | HTTP/REST | "A lightweight NoSQL DB" |

**Storage Account Redundancy Options (Most Tested Table):**

| Redundancy | Number of Copies | Failover Scope | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LRS** (Locally Redundant Storage) | 3 | Single data centre | Cheapest, dev/test |
| **ZRS** (Zone Redundant Storage) | 3 | Across **3 AZs** in one region | High availability within region |
| **GRS** (Geo-Redundant Storage) | 6 | Primary region + secondary region pair | Disaster recovery |
| **RA-GRS** (Read-Access GRS) | 6 | Same as GRS + read access to secondary | Read-from-secondary, analytics |

#### 2.2.3 Azure Networking Services

| Service | Purpose | KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| **Virtual Network (VNet)** | Private network in the cloud | "Your own private LAN in Azure" |
| **Subnet** | Logical segment inside a VNet | IP-range partition |
| **Network Security Group (NSG)** | Stateful firewall for VNets/subnets | "Cloud firewall" |
| **Application Gateway** | Layer-7 load balancer with WAF | URL routing, SSL offload |
| **Azure Load Balancer** | Layer-4 (TCP/UDP) load balancer | High-throughput L4 |
| **Azure Front Door** | Global Layer-7 load balancer with CDN | Multi-region routing |
| **VPN Gateway** | Site-to-site / point-to-site VPN | Encrypted tunnel to on-prem |
| **ExpressRoute** | Private dedicated fibre connection | Highest security, no internet |
| **Azure DNS** | Hosted DNS service | Domain name resolution |
| **Private Endpoint** | Private IP inside VNet for PaaS services | Avoid public internet |

#### 2.2.4 Azure Database Services

| Service | Type | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Azure SQL Database** | Managed relational (PaaS) | OLTP, lift-and-shift SQL |
| **Azure Cosmos DB** | Globally distributed NoSQL | Planet-scale, multi-model |
| **Azure Database for MySQL / PostgreSQL** | Managed OSS RDBMS | Open-source stacks |
| **Azure Cache for Redis** | In-memory key-value | Session, cache layer |
| **Azure Synapse Analytics** | Data warehouse + big data | Analytics, BI |

#### 2.2.5 Azure Identity & Security

- **Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD)** — Identity-as-a-Service (IDaaS), supports OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, SAML 2.0.
- **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** — Fine-grained authorisation. Built-in roles: Owner, Contributor, Reader, User Access Administrator.
- **Azure Key Vault** — HSM-backed secret / key / certificate management.
- **Microsoft Defender for Cloud** — Cloud Workload Protection Platform (CWPP) + Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM).
- **Azure Sentinel** — Cloud-native SIEM + SOAR.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Azure Metrics, SLAs & Billing

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the **numerical values and formulas** most commonly asked in KTU numerical / short-answer questions on Azure. Memorise the **SLA percentages** — they appear every year.

| Metric / Concept | Formula or Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Azure VM SLA (single instance, Premium SSD)** | **99.99 %** | uptime / month | ≈ 52.6 min downtime / year |
| **Azure VM SLA (single instance, Standard SSD)** | **99.5 %** | uptime / month | ≈ 43.8 hrs / year |
| **Azure VM SLA (Availability Set, 2 VMs)** | **99.95 %** | uptime / month | ≈ 4.38 hrs / year |
| **Azure VM SLA (Availability Zones, 2 VMs)** | **99.99 %** | uptime / month | Cross-AZ redundancy |
| **Azure SQL Database (Business Critical)** | **99.995 %** | uptime / month | ≈ 26.3 min / year |
| **Blob Storage (LRS hot tier)** | **99.9 %** | availability | Read SLA |
| **Blob Storage (RA-GRS)** | **99.99 %** read | availability | Secondary readable |
| **Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG)** | Pay per second (Linux) / per hour (Windows) | USD | Default billing |
| **Reserved Instances (1-yr)** | ~**40–50 %** discount | % saving | Commit upfront |
| **Reserved Instances (3-yr)** | ~**60–70 %** discount | % saving | Maximum saving |
| **Spot VMs** | Up to **90 %** discount | % saving | Pre-emptible |
| **Outbound Data Transfer (same region)** | Free | USD/GB | Stay-in-region is cheap |
| **Outbound Data Transfer (inter-region / internet)** | $0.05 – $0.12 per GB | USD/GB | First 100 GB / month free |

#### 2.3.1 SLA-to-Downtime Conversion (Important)

The conversion between an SLA percentage $S$ (as a decimal) and the maximum allowed yearly downtime $D$ is:

$$
D = (1 - S) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \;\text{minutes}
$$

> This formula is **almost always** asked in Part A. The reciprocal (downtime → SLA) uses:
>
> $$
> S = 1 - \dfrac{D}{365 \times 24 \times 60}
> $$

#### 2.3.2 Composite SLA Formula

When two services run **in series** (each request must touch both), the composite SLA is the **product**:

$$
S_{\text{series}} = S_1 \times S_2 \times S_3
$$

When two services run **in parallel** (redundant paths), the composite SLA is:

$$
S_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \left[ (1 - S_1) \times (1 - S_2) \right]
$$

**Why is this important?** AWS, Azure, and GCP architect solutions such that *redundant parallel paths* dominate, dramatically raising the effective SLA without raising the cost of each individual service. KTU expects you to compare, e.g., an Active-Active multi-region deployment (parallel) vs. a simple HA pair (mostly series dependencies).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microservices deployment** — AKS hosts containers; Azure Front Door + Application Gateway terminate TLS; Cosmos DB and Azure Cache for Redis store state; Key Vault holds secrets; Entra ID authenticates users.
- **Disaster Recovery (DR)** — Use GRS for storage + Azure Site Recovery (ASR) for VMs; replicate to the region pair.
- **Cost optimisation** — Use Azure Advisor + Reserved Instances + Spot VMs + auto-scaling.
- **DevOps** — Azure DevOps (Repos, Pipelines, Boards, Artifacts, Test Plans) plus GitHub Actions for CI/CD.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — SLA-to-Downtime Conversion (Model KTU 3-Mark Question)

> **Problem:** An Azure VM in an Availability Set with two instances has an SLA of **99.95 %**. Calculate the maximum allowable downtime **per year, per month, and per week**.

**Step 1 — Identify the SLA as a decimal.**

$$
S = 99.95\,\% = 0.9995
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total minutes in a year (the reference window).**

$$
T_{\text{year}} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525\,600\;\text{minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the SLA-to-Downtime formula.**

$$
D_{\text{year}} = (1 - S) \times T_{\text{year}}
$$

$$
D_{\text{year}} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 525\,600
$$

$$
D_{\text{year}} = 0.0005 \times 525\,600
$$

$$
D_{\text{year}} = 262.8\;\text{minutes per year}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hours for readability.**

$$
D_{\text{year}} = \dfrac{262.8}{60} = 4.38\;\text{hours per year}
$$

**Step 5 — Per month (assuming 30-day month for simplicity, KTU convention).**

$$
T_{\text{month}} = 30 \times 24 \times 60 = 43\,200\;\text{minutes}
$$

$$
D_{\text{month}} = 0.0005 \times 43\,200 = 21.6\;\text{minutes per month}
$$

**Step 6 — Per week.**

$$
T_{\text{week}} = 7 \times 24 \times 60 = 10\,080\;\text{minutes}
$$

$$
D_{\text{week}} = 0.0005 \times 10\,080 = 5.04\;\text{minutes per week}
$$

> **Final Answer:** A 99.95 % SLA permits **≈ 4.38 hours of downtime per year**, **≈ 21.6 minutes per month**, and **≈ 5 minutes per week**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Composite SLA of a 3-Tier Azure Application (Model KTU 7-Mark Question)

> **Problem:** A KTU start-up architect proposes a 3-tier application on Azure:
> - **Front-end** — Azure App Service (SLA = **99.95 %**)
> - **API** — AKS (SLA = **99.95 %**)
> - **Database** — Azure SQL Database Business Critical (SLA = **99.995 %**)
>
> Every request **must** pass through all three tiers in **series**. Compute the composite SLA and the maximum yearly downtime. Now suppose they add a **read-replica Azure SQL** with the same 99.995 % SLA, and route **read traffic in parallel** to the replica. Recompute.

**Step 1 — Series composite SLA (original design).**

$$
S_{\text{series}} = S_{\text{front}} \times S_{\text{aks}} \times S_{\text{db}}
$$

$$
S_{\text{series}} = 0.9995 \times 0.9995 \times 0.99995
$$

$$
S_{\text{series}} = 0.9995^2 \times 0.99995
$$

First, compute $0.9995^2$:

$$
0.9995^2 = (1 - 0.0005)^2 \approx 1 - 2(0.0005) + 0.0005^2 = 0.99900025
$$

Now multiply by $0.99995$:

$$
S_{\text{series}} = 0.99900025 \times 0.99995 \approx 0.99895
$$

So the composite series SLA is:

$$
S_{\text{series}} \approx 99.895\,\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute yearly downtime for the series design.**

$$
D_{\text{series}} = (1 - 0.99895) \times 525\,600
$$

$$
D_{\text{series}} = 0.00105 \times 525\,600 \approx 551.88\;\text{minutes} \approx 9.20\;\text{hours per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Add a read-replica in parallel for the DB tier.**

The parallel DB block has SLA:

$$
S_{\text{db-parallel}} = 1 - (1 - 0.99995)(1 - 0.99995) = 1 - (0.00005)^2 = 1 - 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \approx 0.9999999975
$$

That's effectively **99.99999975 %** for the parallel DB block — practically a "five-nines-plus" DB.

**Step 4 — Recompute series composite with the parallel DB block.**

$$
S_{\text{series-parallel}} = 0.9995 \times 0.9995 \times 0.9999999975
$$

$$
S_{\text{series-parallel}} \approx 0.99900025 \times 0.9999999975 \approx 0.99900025
$$

Wait — let us re-compute more carefully:

$$
S_{\text{series-parallel}} \approx 0.99900025 \times (1 - 2.5 \times 10^{-9})
$$

$$
\approx 0.99900025 - 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.99900025
$$

$$
\approx 0.99900025 - 2.5 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
S_{\text{series-parallel}} \approx 99.900025\,\%
$$

**Step 5 — Yearly downtime of the new design.**

$$
D_{\text{new}} = (1 - 0.99900025) \times 525\,600
$$

$$
D_{\text{new}} = 0.00099975 \times 525\,600 \approx 525.42\;\text{minutes} \approx 8.76\;\text{hours per year}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The bottleneck is the **App Service + AKS front tiers (each 99.95 %)**. Adding a read-replica SQL — though it boosts the DB tier close to 100 % — only shaves a few minutes of yearly downtime. The student should be able to identify that the **front tiers** must be hardened next (e.g., deploy each tier across two regions in parallel) to make a meaningful SLA jump.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What on Azure

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as a decision matrix in your KTU Part-B answers.** It is the single most useful cheat-sheet for 7-mark "compare-and-contrast" questions.

| Decision Criterion | Use **VM** | Use **App Service** | Use **AKS** | Use **Functions** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Need full OS control? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ⚠️ Limited (nodes) | ❌ No |
| Lift-and-shift legacy app? | ✅ Perfect | ❌ Not suitable | ❌ Need containerisation | ❌ Not suitable |
| Microservices with HTTP/gRPC? | ⚠️ Possible | ✅ Good | ✅ **Best fit** | ✅ Good |
| Event-driven, short jobs (< 5 min)? | ❌ Overkill | ❌ Overkill | ⚠️ Possible | ✅ **Best fit** |
| Predictable, long-running workloads? | ✅ Best | ✅ Best | ✅ Good | ❌ Cold start penalty |
| Need GPU / specialised hardware? | ✅ N-series, ND-series | ❌ Limited | ✅ Possible | ❌ No |
| Lowest management overhead? | ❌ Highest | ✅ Low | ⚠️ Medium | ✅ **Lowest** |
| Pay model | Per-hour VM | Per-plan | Per-node + control plane | Per-execution (GB-s) |

### 3.4 Cost Estimation Pattern (Frequently Asked 7-Mark)

> **Problem:** A B.Tech project team runs **2 × Azure B2s VMs (Linux)** continuously, with **500 GB of Blob Storage (Hot LRS)**, and **2 TB outbound data transfer to the internet** per month. Use indicative rates:
> - B2s Linux: **\$0.0416 / hour**
> - Blob Hot LRS: **\$0.0184 / GB / month**
> - Outbound: first **100 GB free**, then **\$0.087 / GB**
>
> **Compute the monthly bill.**

**Step 1 — VM cost (2 VMs × 24 × 30 hours).**

$$
\text{Hours} = 2 \times 24 \times 30 = 1\,440\;\text{VM-hours}
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{VM}} = 1\,440 \times 0.0416 = \$59.90
$$

**Step 2 — Storage cost.**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{storage}} = 500 \times 0.0184 = \$9.20
$$

**Step 3 — Outbound data transfer cost.**

First 100 GB free:

$$
\text{Billable GB} = 2\,000 - 100 = 1\,900\;\text{GB}
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{transfer}} = 1\,900 \times 0.087 = \$165.30
$$

**Step 4 — Total.**

$$
\text{Total} = 59.90 + 9.20 + 165.30 = \$234.40\;\text{per month}
$$

> **Examiner Tip:** Always state assumptions, list the formula, show the unit arithmetic, and present a clean final figure with the correct currency. KTU examiners reward clarity more than the final digit.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Azure Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UserLayer[USER LAYER]
        userA[End User Browser]
        userB[Mobile App Client]
    end

    subgraph EdgeLayer[AZURE EDGE LAYER]
        fd["Azure Front Door Global LB and CDN"]
        agw["Application Gateway WAF Layer 7"]
    end

    subgraph IdentityLayer[IDENTITY LAYER]
        ent["Microsoft Entra ID Azure AD"]
        rbac["RBAC Role Based Access Control"]
    end

    subgraph AppLayer[APPLICATION LAYER]
        app1["App Service Web Tier"]
        aks1["AKS Kubernetes API Tier"]
        fn1["Azure Functions Event Worker"]
    end

    subgraph DataLayer[DATA LAYER]
        sql1["Azure SQL Business Critical"]
        cos1["Cosmos DB Globally Distributed"]
        red1["Azure Cache for Redis"]
        blob1["Blob Storage GRS"]
    end

    subgraph OpsLayer[MANAGEMENT AND OPS LAYER]
        mon1["Azure Monitor Logs and Metrics"]
        adv1["Azure Advisor Recommendations"]
        kv1["Azure Key Vault Secrets"]
        arm1["Azure Resource Manager ARM"]
    end

    userA --> fd
    userB --> fd
    fd --> agw
    agw --> app1
    agw --> aks1
    app1 --> aks1
    aks1 --> sql1
    aks1 --> cos1
    aks1 --> red1
    fn1 --> blob1
    ent -. auth .-> app1
    ent -. auth .-> aks1
    rbac -. authz .-> app1
    kv1 -. secrets .-> aks1
    mon1 -. telemetry .-> app1
    mon1 -. telemetry .-> aks1
    adv1 -. cost .-> app1
    arm1 -. deploy .-> app1
    arm1 -. deploy .-> aks1
```

### 4.2 Azure Hierarchy of Organisation (Subscription / RG / Resource)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    tent["Azure AD Tenant One organisation"]
    sub1["Subscription Billing and quota boundary"]
    sub2["Subscription Dev Test"]
    rg1["Resource Group rg-prod-web"]
    rg2["Resource Group rg-prod-data"]
    rg3["Resource Group rg-dev"]
    resA["App Service Web App"]
    resB["AKS Cluster"]
    resC["Azure SQL DB"]
    resD["Storage Account"]
    resE["VNet and Subnets"]
    resF["Key Vault"]

    tent --> sub1
    tent --> sub2
    sub1 --> rg1
    sub1 --> rg2
    sub2 --> rg3
    rg1 --> resA
    rg1 --> resB
    rg1 --> resE
    rg2 --> resC
    rg2 --> resD
    rg2 --> resF
    rg3 --> resA
```

### 4.3 Azure Deployment Model Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Need a cloud app"]
    q1{"Need full OS and network control?"}
    q2{"Container based microservices?"}
    q3{"Event driven short jobs?"}
    q4{"Just deploy source code as web app?"}
    a1["Choose Azure VM IaaS"]
    a2["Choose Azure Kubernetes Service AKS"]
    a3["Choose Azure Functions Serverless FaaS"]
    a4["Choose Azure App Service PaaS"]

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|Yes| a1
    q1 -->|No| q2
    q2 -->|Yes| a2
    q2 -->|No| q3
    q3 -->|Yes| a3
    q3 -->|No| q4
    q4 -->|Yes| a4
    q4 -->|No| a1
```

### 4.4 Storage Redundancy Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LRS["LRS Single DC 3 copies"]
        lrs1["Copy 1"]
        lrs2["Copy 2"]
        lrs3["Copy 3"]
    end

    subgraph ZRS["ZRS One Region 3 AZs"]
        zrsa["AZ 1"]
        zrsb["AZ 2"]
        zrsc["AZ 3"]
    end

    subgraph GRS["GRS Two Regions 6 copies"]
        gpa["Primary Region 3 copies"]
        gsa["Secondary Region 3 copies"]
    end

    LRS --> ZRS --> GRS
```

> **Read order:** LRS (cheapest) → ZRS (HA in-region) → GRS (DR cross-region). RA-GRS adds read access to the secondary region.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
> *Define Microsoft Azure. List any **three** core services offered by Azure with one-line descriptions.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Microsoft Azure** is a public cloud computing platform from Microsoft that provides on-demand compute, storage, networking, database, AI/ML, and developer services hosted in Microsoft-managed data centres and delivered over the internet.
>
> **Three core services:**
> 1. **Azure Virtual Machines (VMs)** — On-demand, scalable compute with full OS control (IaaS).
> 2. **Azure Blob Storage** — Massively scalable object storage for unstructured data such as images, videos, backups, and logs.
> 3. **Azure App Service** — A managed PaaS for hosting web applications, REST APIs, and mobile backends without managing the underlying infrastructure.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
> *Differentiate between **LRS**, **ZRS**, and **GRS** redundancy in Azure Storage. State the recommended use case for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Copies | Scope | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LRS (Locally Redundant Storage)** | 3 | Single data centre | Dev/test, non-critical data, lowest cost |
| **ZRS (Zone Redundant Storage)** | 3 | 3 Availability Zones in one region | High-availability in-region workloads |
| **GRS (Geo-Redundant Storage)** | 6 | Primary region + paired secondary region | Disaster recovery, regulatory cross-region copies |

> **RA-GRS** additionally provides read access to the secondary region, useful for analytics offload.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model QP]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **shared responsibility model** of Microsoft Azure with a neat diagram. Compare it with the on-premises model and the IaaS / PaaS / SaaS divisions. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A B.Tech project hosts a 2-tier application: an **App Service** (SLA = 99.95 %) front-end and an **Azure SQL Database Business Critical** (SLA = 99.995 %) backend. Compute (i) the composite SLA, (ii) the maximum yearly downtime, and (iii) suggest **two** architectural changes to improve the composite SLA to ≥ 99.99 %. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shared Responsibility Model in Azure**
> The shared responsibility model defines **what Azure manages** and **what the customer manages** at each cloud service layer.

**Step 1 — Define the model.**

Azure is responsible for the **security *of* the cloud** (the physical infrastructure, hosts, network, and the hypervisor). The customer is responsible for the **security *in* the cloud** (their data, their access keys, their application code, their OS patches in IaaS, and their identity configuration in PaaS/SaaS).

**Step 2 — Layered table (3 marks).**

| Layer | On-Premises | IaaS (Azure VM) | PaaS (App Service / SQL) | SaaS (Microsoft 365) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Data & Access Mgmt | Customer | Customer | Customer | Customer |
| Application | Customer | Customer | Customer | Azure |
| Runtime / Middleware | Customer | Customer | Azure | Azure |
| Operating System | Customer | Customer | Azure | Azure |
| Virtualisation | Customer | Azure | Azure | Azure |
| Servers & Storage | Customer | Azure | Azure | Azure |
| Networking | Customer | Azure | Azure | Azure |
| Physical DC | Customer | Azure | Azure | Azure |

**Step 3 — Key inferences (2 marks).**

- As you move from on-premises → IaaS → PaaS → SaaS, **customer responsibility decreases** and **operational burden shifts to Azure**.
- The customer **always** retains responsibility for **data classification, identity, and access control**, regardless of the service model.
- KTU students should note: *misconfigured NSGs, open storage accounts, leaked SAS tokens, and disabled MFA* are the most common security failures **on the customer's side**.

**Step 4 — Diagram.**

```
+--------------------------------------------+
|       CUSTOMER  RESPONSIBILITY  (top)     |
|  data | access | app code | identity       |
+--------------------------------------------+
       PaaS line   IaaS line
+--------------------------------------------+
|       AZURE   RESPONSIBILITY  (bottom)     |
| OS  |  virt  |  hosts  |  network  |  DC   |
+--------------------------------------------+
```

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Adopting PaaS or SaaS reduces operational cost and patching overhead but does **not** absolve the customer of identity, data, and access governance.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute composite series SLA (2 marks).**

$$
S_{\text{composite}} = S_{\text{app}} \times S_{\text{sql}} = 0.9995 \times 0.99995
$$

$$
S_{\text{composite}} = (1 - 0.0005)(1 - 0.00005) \approx 1 - 0.00055 + 0.000000025
$$

$$
S_{\text{composite}} \approx 0.99945 = 99.945\,\%
$$

**Step 2 — Yearly downtime (1 mark).**

$$
D_{\text{year}} = (1 - 0.99945) \times 525\,600 = 0.00055 \times 525\,600 = 289.08\;\text{minutes} \approx 4.82\;\text{hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Architectural changes to reach ≥ 99.99 % (4 marks).**

**Change 1 — Deploy App Service in *Availability Zones* (2 marks).** The default App Service SLA is 99.95 % because the instance is single-pinned. Deploying it across two Availability Zones raises its SLA to **99.99 %**. The new composite becomes:

$$
S_{\text{new}} = 0.9999 \times 0.99995 = 0.99985 = 99.985\,\%
$$

Yearly downtime:

$$
D_{\text{new}} = (1 - 0.99985) \times 525\,600 = 0.00015 \times 525\,600 = 78.84\;\text{minutes}
$$

That is ≈ **1.3 hours per year** — well within the 99.99 % target *for the single-region design*.

**Change 2 — Add *active-active multi-region* deployment using Azure Front Door (2 marks).** Replicate both App Service and Azure SQL into a **region pair** (e.g., Central India + South India). Route traffic through **Azure Front Door**, which gives the *parallel* formula per region. Effective global availability can reach **99.99 %+**, satisfying the requirement.

**Step 4 — Final answer statement (½ mark).**

> **Composite SLA = 99.945 %**, **downtime ≈ 4.82 hours/year** originally. After deploying App Service across AZs and adding multi-region active-active with Front Door, the composite SLA rises to **≈ 99.99 %+** with downtime under **53 minutes per year**.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Azure architecture** comprising the **Edge, Identity, Application, Data, and Management layers**. List the main Azure service in each layer. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare **Azure VM (IaaS)**, **Azure App Service (PaaS)**, **AKS (PaaS)**, and **Azure Functions (FaaS)** across: control level, scaling model, ideal use case, and pay model. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Draw the layered block diagram (3 marks).**

```
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  EDGE LAYER     Azure Front Door / CDN / App Gateway     |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  IDENTITY       Entra ID (Azure AD), RBAC, MFA, PIM      |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  APPLICATION    App Service | AKS | Container Apps | Fn  |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  DATA           Azure SQL | Cosmos DB | Redis | Blob      |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  MANAGEMENT      ARM | Azure Monitor | Advisor | Policy   |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
       (All running on Azure-managed physical fabric)
```

**Step 2 — Brief role of each layer (4 marks).**

- **Edge Layer** — Global entry-point: terminates TLS, performs WAF inspection, caches static content at the edge. Example: *Azure Front Door + Application Gateway + Azure CDN*.
- **Identity Layer** — Authentication, authorisation, single sign-on. Example: *Microsoft Entra ID* with OAuth 2.0 / OIDC tokens; *RBAC* roles Owner / Contributor / Reader.
- **Application Layer** — Where business logic runs. Example: *App Service* for web, *AKS* for containers, *Azure Functions* for event-driven code.
- **Data Layer** — Where state is persisted. Example: *Azure SQL* (relational), *Cosmos DB* (globally distributed NoSQL), *Blob Storage* (objects), *Azure Cache for Redis* (in-memory).
- **Management Layer** — Centralised control plane. Example: *Azure Resource Manager* (ARM) for declarative deployment, *Azure Monitor* for logs/metrics, *Azure Policy* for governance.

**Conclusion (½ mark):** Each layer is independently scalable and replaceable — a key tenet of the **microservices** philosophy later taught in this very course (PECBT745).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

| Criterion | **Azure VM (IaaS)** | **App Service (PaaS)** | **AKS (PaaS)** | **Azure Functions (FaaS)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Control level** | Highest — full OS, root, kernel modules | Low — code + config only | Medium — node config + cluster | Lowest — code only |
| **Scaling model** | Manual or VMSS rules | Built-in autoscale rules | Cluster autoscaler + HPA | Event-driven, instant scale-out |
| **Cold start** | None (always running) | None for paid tier | None | Yes (hundreds of ms to seconds) |
| **Ideal use case** | Lift-and-shift, legacy, custom drivers | Web apps, REST APIs, mobile BFFs | Containerised microservices, polyglot | Event handlers, schedulers, glue code |
| **Pay model** | Per-second (Linux) / per-hour (Win) of VM runtime | Per-App Service Plan tier | Per-Node + control plane fee | Per-execution: **GB-seconds** + **executions** |
| **Max execution time** | Indefinite | Indefinite | Indefinite | Default **5 min**, max **10 min** (Consumption) / unlimited (Premium) |
| **Management overhead** | High | Low | Medium | Very low |
| **Cost for bursty / sporadic traffic** | Poor (pay for idle VM) | Moderate | Moderate | **Excellent (pay only when running)** |

**Step 3 — Final recommendation (1 mark).**

> Choose **VM** for legacy and full-OS control. Choose **App Service** for standard web/REST workloads. Choose **AKS** for containerised microservices (this is the most important pick for the rest of *PECBT745*). Choose **Azure Functions** for event-driven, short-lived glue code where cost follows usage.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Azure**
>
> 1. **Confusing SLA with availability zone redundancy.** A single VM with Premium SSD gives 99.99 %, but this does **not** mean it survives a data-centre outage. You must explicitly say "across AZs" or "across regions" for true fault tolerance.
> 2. **Mixing up LRS, ZRS, GRS, RA-GRS.** GRS has **6** copies (3 + 3), not 4. RA-GRS allows **read** access to the secondary, not write.
> 3. **Treating Azure as "just VMs".** Azure is a platform of **200+** services. If your answer only mentions VMs and Blob Storage, you will lose at least 4 marks on a 14-mark question.
> 4. **Wrong SLA multiplication.** Series SLA is **product**, parallel is **1 − (1 − S₁)(1 − S₂)**. Reversing these is a common error.
> 5. **No mention of region pair / Azure Front Door** in DR answers. Just saying "use a backup" is insufficient; name the services.
> 6. **Confusing Azure AD (Entra ID) with Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS).** Azure AD is a cloud identity provider for SaaS/OAuth/OIDC; AD DS is the on-prem LDAP/Kerberos server. They are *related* but **not the same**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Azure (Module 1, PECBT745)**
>
> ✅ **Azure = Microsoft's public cloud** offering IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, and FaaS.
> ✅ **Three architectural pillars** — Regions, Availability Zones (AZs), and Region Pairs.
> ✅ **Compute options** — VM (IaaS) → App Service (PaaS Web) → AKS (PaaS Containers) → Functions (FaaS). Decision flows matter.
> ✅ **Storage quartet** — Blob (objects), File (SMB/NFS shares), Queue (messaging), Table (NoSQL). Each has a distinct access pattern.
> ✅ **Redundancy ladder** — LRS (3 in 1 DC) → ZRS (3 across AZs) → GRS (6 across regions) → RA-GRS (read access to secondary).
> ✅ **Networking essentials** — VNet, Subnet, NSG, App Gateway (L7+WAF), Load Balancer (L4), Front Door (global L7), VPN Gateway, ExpressRoute, Private Endpoint.
> ✅ **Identity** — Microsoft Entra ID (= Azure AD), RBAC roles (Owner, Contributor, Reader, UAA), OAuth 2.0 / OIDC / SAML.
> ✅ **SLAs to memorise** — Single VM (Premium SSD) 99.99 %; Availability Set 99.95 %; Azure SQL Business Critical 99.995 %; App Service default 99.95 %.
> ✅ **Composite SLA formulas** — Series: **product** of $S_i$. Parallel: **$1 - (1-S_1)(1-S_2)$**.
> ✅ **SLA-to-downtime** — $D = (1-S) \times 525\,600$ minutes per year.
> ✅ **Billing** — PAYG per-second, Reserved Instances (1-yr/3-yr discounts), Spot VMs (up to 90 % off). Inter-region outbound is charged; intra-region is free.
> ✅ **Management planes** — Portal, CLI, PowerShell, ARM templates, Bicep, Terraform. All REST calls go through **Azure Resource Manager (ARM)**.
> ✅ **Shared responsibility** — Azure secures *of* the cloud (physical, hosts, network, hypervisor); customer secures *in* the cloud (data, IAM, app, OS in IaaS).
> ✅ **Microservices relevance** — AKS + Service Mesh (Istio/Linkerd via AKS add-ons) + Cosmos DB + Service Bus + Entra ID form the canonical microservices stack on Azure.
> ✅ **KTU favourite exam phrasing** — *"Explain with diagram…", "Compare X and Y", "Compute the composite SLA", "Recommend a service for scenario Z."* Always respond with a diagram, a comparison table, a numeric answer, and a justified recommendation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Google
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT745 - CLOUD, MICROSERVICES AND APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Google

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Google Cloud Platform (GCP)** is a suite of public cloud computing services offered by Google. It provides a range of hosted services for compute, storage, machine learning, data analytics, and networking, running on the same infrastructure that Google uses internally for its end-user products such as Google Search, Gmail, Google Drive, and YouTube. GCP is hosted across **31 regions**, **100+ zones**, and **200+ network edge locations (PoPs)** globally, leveraging Google's private global fiber-optic network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECBT745 – Module 1):**
> *"Google Cloud Platform (GCP) is a comprehensive cloud services suite delivering IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS offerings built on Google's globally distributed, secure, and sustainable infrastructure. It provides on-demand compute (Compute Engine), serverless (Cloud Functions, Cloud Run), storage (Cloud Storage, Bigtable, Spanner), and AI/ML (Vertex AI) services."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "Google Cloud as a Public Utility Company"**

Imagine Google Cloud as a massive, global **electricity utility company**, but instead of distributing electricity, it distributes **computing power and data storage**:

| Utility Company | Google Cloud (GCP) |
|---|---|
| Power Plants | **Data Centers** (located in regions/zones) |
| Power Grid (wires) | **Google's Private Fiber Network** |
| Electricity Meter | **Resource Usage Metrics** (vCPU-hours, GB-months) |
| Electricity Bill | **GCP Billing & Pricing** (Pay-as-you-go) |
| Household Appliances | **Workloads/Applications** |
| Voltage Regulations (110V/220V) | **Service Tiers** (Standard, Premium, Shielded VM) |

Just as you don't build a power plant to switch on a light bulb, **you don't build a data center to host a web application** — you "plug in" to Google Cloud and consume exactly what you need.

---

## 1.3 Core Characteristics of Google Cloud

- **Global Scale**: Backed by **Google's private fiber-optic network** spanning continents.
- **Sustainability**: Carbon-neutral cloud since **2007**; aims for 24/7 carbon-free energy by **2030**.
- **Multi-tenancy**: Resources isolated per project via Identity and Access Management (IAM).
- **Open Source Friendly**: Strong support for **Kubernetes** (originally developed by Google), **TensorFlow**, **Go**, **Angular**.
- **Pricing Models**: Pay-as-you-go, Sustained Use Discounts, Committed Use Discounts, Spot VMs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Historical Milestones (Frequently asked in KTU exams):**
> - **2008**: Google App Engine launched (first GCP service — PaaS).
> - **2011**: Google Compute Engine (IaaS) launched.
> - **2012**: Google Cloud Storage (GCS) launched.
> - **2014**: GCP officially opened to general public.
> - **2015**: Google Cloud Machine Learning Engine launched.
> - **2018**: Anthos (hybrid/multi-cloud platform) launched.
> - **2019**: Google hired Thomas Kurian (ex-Oracle) as CEO of Google Cloud.
> - **2021**: Vertex AI unified ML platform launched.

---

## 1.4 The Google Global Infrastructure Hierarchy

GCP infrastructure is organized in a **4-tier hierarchy**:

$$\text{Physical Media (Cables/Optics)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Point of Presence (PoP)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Region} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Zone}$$

| Layer | Description | Count (Approx.) |
|---|---|---|
| **Region** | A specific geographical location (e.g., `us-central1` = Council Bluffs, Iowa) | **40+ regions** |
| **Zone** | An isolated location within a region; independent power, cooling, networking | **3+ per region** |
| **Edge Location / PoP** | Google's CDN nodes for low-latency content delivery | **200+** |
| **Network** | Google's private global fiber backbone | **Submarine + Terrestrial** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Regions & Zones Matter for Exams:**
> - A **Region** = Independent geographic area.
> - A **Zone** = Failure domain (fault-isolated).
> - Deploying across **multiple zones** in a region = High Availability.
> - Deploying across **multiple regions** = Disaster Recovery.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 GCP Core Service Categories

GCP services are organized into the following product families (high-yield for KTU):

### 2.1.1 Compute Services

| Service | Type | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Compute Engine** | IaaS | Virtual Machines (VMs) — equivalent to AWS EC2 |
| **Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE)** | PaaS/Container | Managed Kubernetes cluster |
| **App Engine** | PaaS | Deploy apps without managing infrastructure |
| **Cloud Functions** | FaaS (Serverless) | Event-driven functions (Node.js, Go, Python) |
| **Cloud Run** | Serverless Containers | Run stateless containers on demand |
| **Bare Metal Solution** | IaaS | Dedicated hardware for high-performance workloads |

### 2.1.2 Storage Services

| Service | Type | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Cloud Storage (GCS)** | Object Storage | Unstructured data (images, videos, backups) |
| **Persistent Disk** | Block Storage | Boot disks, database volumes |
| **Filestore** | File Storage (NFS) | Shared file systems |
| **Cloud SQL** | Managed RDBMS | MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server |
| **Cloud Spanner** | Distributed RDBMS | Global, strongly consistent transactions |
| **Firestore / Datastore** | NoSQL Document | Real-time, mobile, web apps |
| **Bigtable** | NoSQL Wide-Column | Time-series, IoT, analytics |

### 2.1.3 Networking Services

- **Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)**: Software-defined networking.
- **Cloud Load Balancing**: Global, anycast IP-based L4/L7 load balancer.
- **Cloud CDN**: Google's Content Delivery Network.
- **Cloud DNS**: 100% SLA managed DNS.
- **Cloud Armor**: DDoS protection & WAF.
- **Cloud Interconnect**: Dedicated connection between on-premises and GCP.

### 2.1.4 Big Data & AI/ML Services

- **BigQuery**: Serverless, petabyte-scale data warehouse.
- **Dataflow**: Stream and batch data processing (Apache Beam).
- **Dataproc**: Managed Hadoop/Spark.
- **Pub/Sub**: Real-time messaging (asynchronous).
- **Vertex AI**: Unified ML platform.
- **AutoML**: Train custom models without coding.

---

## 2.2 The GCP Resource Hierarchy (IAM Structure)

Google Cloud uses a **4-level resource hierarchy** for policy inheritance:

```
Organization (Root Node)
        |
    Folders (groups of projects, e.g., "Production", "Dev")
        |
    Projects (basic organization unit; isolated by default)
        |
    Resources (VMs, buckets, databases, etc.)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Point:** IAM policies are inherited **downward** in the hierarchy. An IAM policy on the **Organization** level applies to all folders, projects, and resources underneath. A project provides a **trust boundary** (default-deny between projects).

---

## 2.3 Pricing & Billing Models

| Model | Discount (Approx.) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **On-Demand (Pay-as-you-go)** | Base price | Short-term, unpredictable workloads |
| **Sustained Use Discounts (SUD)** | Up to **30%** auto-discount | Long-running VMs in a month |
| **Committed Use Discounts (CUD)** | Up to **57%** (1-yr) / **70%** (3-yr) | Predictable steady-state workloads |
| **Spot VMs** | Up to **91%** discount | Fault-tolerant, batch jobs |
| **Preemptible VMs** | Up to **80%** discount (deprecated → Spot) | Short-lived workloads |
| **Free Tier** | Always-free + $300 credit (90 days) | Learning, prototyping |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cost Calculations)

For exam questions involving **monthly cost calculation**, the following formulas are used:

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Total vCPU Cost** | $C_{cpu} = N_{cpu} \times P_{cpu} \times H_{month}$ | USD/month |
| **Total Memory Cost** | $C_{mem} = M_{GB} \times P_{mem} \times H_{month}$ | USD/month |
| **Total Storage Cost** | $C_{sto} = S_{GB} \times P_{sto} \times 30$ | USD/month |
| **Sustained Use Discount** | $C_{SUD} = C_{base} \times (1 - D_{SUD})$ | USD/month |
| **Egress Cost** | $C_{egress} = D_{out} \times P_{egress}$ | USD/month |
| **Total Monthly Bill** | $C_{total} = C_{cpu} + C_{mem} + C_{sto} + C_{egress}$ | USD/month |

Where:
- $N_{cpu}$ = Number of vCPUs
- $P_{cpu}$ = Price per vCPU-hour (USD)
- $H_{month}$ = Total hours used in a month
- $M_{GB}$ = Total RAM in GB
- $S_{GB}$ = Total disk size in GB
- $D_{out}$ = Outbound data in GB
- $D_{SUD}$ = Sustained use discount fraction (e.g., 0.30 for 30%)

> [!NOTE]
> **Egress is NEVER free** in GCP (unlike AWS which gives 100 GB/month free). First **1 GB/month** of egress to any destination is free in GCP. Cross-region egress is charged at higher rates.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

GCP powers engineering applications including:
- **Snap Inc.**: Uses GCP Bigtable for Snapchat's Snap Map and stories.
- **Spotify**: Migrated to GCP for batch analytics (BigQuery, Dataflow).
- **Twitter/X**: Uses GCP for analytics pipelines.
- **PayPal**: Uses GKE for fraud detection workloads.
- **GitHub Codespaces**: Backed by GCP for cloud-based dev environments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Monthly GCP Cost Calculation (14-Mark Exam Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Style Numerical Problem:**
> *"A startup deploys a web application on Google Cloud. The deployment uses **4 Compute Engine VMs (n2-standard-4)**, each running **24×7 for the entire month of April (30 days)**. Each VM has **4 vCPUs** and **16 GB RAM**. The persistent disk attached to each VM is **100 GB SSD (pd-ssd)**. The application serves **500 GB of outbound traffic (egress)** to users. Compute the total monthly bill assuming on-demand pricing in the `us-central1` region."*
>
> **Given (us-central1 on-demand prices):**
> - vCPU-hour = **$0.03365**
> - GB-RAM-hour = **$0.00457**
> - pd-SSD = **$0.170 per GB-month**
> - Egress (first 1 GB free) = **$0.12 per GB**

---

### **Step 1: Convert the month into hours**

A 30-day month has:

$$H_{month} = 30 \;\text{days} \times 24 \;\text{hours/day} = 720 \;\text{hours}$$

Since the VMs run **24×7**, $H_{month} = 720$ hours.

---

### **Step 2: Compute vCPU cost**

Total vCPUs across all 4 VMs:

$$N_{cpu}^{total} = 4 \;\text{VMs} \times 4 \;\text{vCPUs/VM} = 16 \;\text{vCPUs}$$

vCPU cost:

$$C_{cpu} = N_{cpu}^{total} \times P_{cpu} \times H_{month}$$

$$C_{cpu} = 16 \times \$0.03365 \times 720$$

$$C_{cpu} = \$387.65$$

---

### **Step 3: Compute Memory (RAM) cost**

Total RAM:

$$M_{GB}^{total} = 4 \;\text{VMs} \times 16 \;\text{GB/VM} = 64 \;\text{GB}$$

Memory cost:

$$C_{mem} = M_{GB}^{total} \times P_{mem} \times H_{month}$$

$$C_{mem} = 64 \times \$0.00457 \times 720$$

$$C_{mem} = \$210.59$$

---

### **Step 4: Compute Storage cost**

Total SSD storage:

$$S_{GB}^{total} = 4 \;\text{VMs} \times 100 \;\text{GB/VM} = 400 \;\text{GB}$$

Storage cost (per-month pricing, no multiplication by hours):

$$C_{sto} = S_{GB}^{total} \times P_{sto}$$

$$C_{sto} = 400 \times \$0.170$$

$$C_{sto} = \$68.00$$

---

### **Step 5: Compute Egress cost**

Subtract the **1 GB free tier**:

$$D_{out}^{billable} = 500 \;\text{GB} - 1 \;\text{GB} = 499 \;\text{GB}$$

Egress cost:

$$C_{egress} = D_{out}^{billable} \times P_{egress}$$

$$C_{egress} = 499 \times \$0.12$$

$$C_{egress} = \$59.88$$

---

### **Step 6: Compute the Total Monthly Bill**

$$C_{total} = C_{cpu} + C_{mem} + C_{sto} + C_{egress}$$

$$C_{total} = \$387.65 + \$210.59 + \$68.00 + \$59.88$$

$$C_{total} = \$726.12$$

---

### **Step 7: Apply Sustained Use Discount (Bonus – Examiner's Favorite)**

Since the VMs run the **entire month**, they qualify for **Sustained Use Discounts (SUD)**. For VMs running 100% of the month, the SUD is approximately **30%** off both vCPU and RAM.

Discounted compute cost:

$$C_{compute}^{SUD} = (C_{cpu} + C_{mem}) \times (1 - 0.30)$$

$$C_{compute}^{SUD} = (387.65 + 210.59) \times 0.70 = 598.24 \times 0.70 = \$418.77$$

**New total bill:**

$$C_{total}^{SUD} = \$418.77 + \$68.00 + \$59.88 = \$546.65$$

**Savings:** $\$726.12 - \$546.65 = \$179.47$ (≈ 24.7% total savings).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of GCP Compute Services

| Service | Abstraction Level | Cold Start | Max Runtime | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Compute Engine** | IaaS (VM) | Minutes | Unlimited | Lift-and-shift, custom OS |
| **GKE** | Containers | Minutes | Unlimited | Microservices, hybrid cloud |
| **App Engine (Standard)** | PaaS | Seconds | Unlimited | Web apps, REST APIs |
| **App Engine (Flexible)** | PaaS (Container) | Minutes | Unlimited | Custom runtimes |
| **Cloud Functions** | FaaS | 100 ms–1 s | 60 min (default 9 min) | Event-driven, lightweight |
| **Cloud Run** | Serverless Container | <1 s | 60 min | Stateless containers |

> [!NOTE]
> **2nd-Tier Analytical Formula — Availability (SLA):**

$$\text{Combined Availability} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - A_i)$$

**Example:** Two services with individual SLAs of 99.9% combined in a multi-zone deployment:

$$A_{combined} = 1 - (1 - 0.999) \times (1 - 0.999) = 1 - 0.000001 = 0.999999 = 99.9999\%$$

This equals "**six nines**" (≈ 31.5 seconds downtime per year).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
