# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Google Cloud Platform (GCP). List any four core services offered by GCP.**

**Model Answer:**

Google Cloud Platform (GCP) is a suite of public cloud computing services offered by Google, providing scalable and reliable infrastructure for compute, storage, networking, big data, and machine learning workloads.

**Four core services:**

1. **Compute Engine** – Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) for Virtual Machines.
2. **Cloud Storage** – Object storage for unstructured data.
3. **BigQuery** – Serverless, petabyte-scale data warehouse.
4. **Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE)** – Managed Kubernetes service for container orchestration.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for listing any four services with brief description.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the concept of a Region and a Zone in Google Cloud. Why are multi-zone deployments preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

- A **Region** is a specific independent geographical area where Google Cloud resources are hosted (e.g., `us-central1` in Iowa, USA). Each region contains at least three zones.
- A **Zone** is a deployment area within a region, with its own independent power, cooling, and networking infrastructure. Zones are isolated from each other to ensure fault tolerance.

**Why multi-zone deployments are preferred:**

1. **High Availability (HA):** If one zone fails, the application continues to run in another zone.
2. **Disaster Recovery:** Protects against localized failures.
3. **Latency Optimization:** Users are routed to the nearest healthy zone.
4. **Compliance:** Meets regulatory requirements for redundancy.

*(3 marks: 1 for definitions + 2 for reasons.)*

---

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with **internal choice**. Two sub-parts of **7 marks each** are typical.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Google Cloud resource hierarchy** with a neat diagram. How does IAM policy inheritance work in this hierarchy? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the **core compute services** of GCP — Compute Engine, GKE, App Engine, and Cloud Functions. Compare them on the basis of abstraction level and use case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Google Cloud Resource Hierarchy (4 Levels):**

1. **Organization Node** – The root node representing your company/domain. There is only one organization per Google Workspace / Cloud Identity domain.
2. **Folders** – Used to group projects (e.g., "Production", "Development", "Billing Departments").
3. **Projects** – The basic unit of organization. Each project is an isolated namespace with its own billing, IAM, and resources. Projects are the trust boundary — by default, no resource is shared across projects.
4. **Resources** – The actual services (VMs, buckets, databases, etc.).

**IAM Policy Inheritance (Top-Down):**

- IAM policies attached to a parent node are **inherited** by all child nodes.
- Example: A policy granting `roles/viewer` to a user at the **Organization** level automatically applies to all folders, projects, and resources beneath it.
- Child-level policies **override** parent policies through **least-privilege** enforcement.

```mermaid
graph TB
    ORG["Organization"]
    F1["Folder Production"]
    F2["Folder Development"]
    P1["Project prod-app"]
    P2["Project prod-db"]
    P3["Project dev-test"]
    ORG --> F1
    ORG --> F2
    F1 --> P1
    F1 --> P2
    F2 --> P3
```

*(7 marks: 3 for hierarchy + 2 for diagram + 2 for IAM inheritance explanation.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Core Compute Services in GCP:**

| Service | Type | Abstraction | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compute Engine** | IaaS | Lowest (full VM control) | Lift-and-shift, custom OS, high-performance workloads |
| **Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE)** | PaaS/Container | Mid (Kubernetes) | Containerized microservices, hybrid cloud |
| **App Engine (Standard/Flexible)** | PaaS | High (code-only) | Web apps, REST APIs, auto-scaling services |
| **Cloud Functions** | FaaS (Serverless) | Highest (function-only) | Event-driven, lightweight, triggered tasks |

**Detailed Comparison:**

1. **Compute Engine:** Provides raw VMs with full control over OS, kernel, and packages. Best for legacy applications.
2. **GKE:** Managed Kubernetes — Google's flagship container service. Supports auto-scaling, self-healing clusters.
3. **App Engine:** Fully managed PaaS; supports Java, Python, Node.js, Go, PHP, Ruby. Auto-scales to zero.
4. **Cloud Functions:** Event-driven serverless. Triggered by Pub/Sub, Cloud Storage events, HTTP. Maximum execution time is **9 minutes** (HTTP) or **60 minutes** (event-driven).

*(7 marks: 4 for explanations + 3 for comparison table.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** A startup deploys 5 Compute Engine VMs (n2-standard-8: 8 vCPUs, 32 GB RAM each), running 24×7 for 30 days, with 250 GB pd-ssd disk per VM, and 2 TB of egress traffic. Compute the total monthly bill with and without sustained use discounts. (Use the pricing given in Section 3.1.) **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **networking services** of GCP — VPC, Cloud Load Balancing, Cloud CDN, and Cloud Interconnect. Highlight the role of Google's private global network. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Given:**
- VMs = 5 × n2-standard-8 → 8 vCPUs, 32 GB RAM each.
- Runtime = 24×7 × 30 days = 720 hours.
- Disk = 250 GB pd-ssd per VM.
- Egress = 2 TB = 2048 GB.
- Prices: vCPU = $0.03365/hr, RAM = $0.00457/hr, SSD = $0.170/GB-month, Egress = $0.12/GB (first 1 GB free).

**Step 1: vCPU Cost**

$$N_{cpu} = 5 \times 8 = 40 \;\text{vCPUs}$$

$$C_{cpu} = 40 \times 0.03365 \times 720 = \$968.64$$

**Step 2: RAM Cost**

$$M_{GB} = 5 \times 32 = 160 \;\text{GB}$$

$$C_{mem} = 160 \times 0.00457 \times 720 = \$526.46$$

**Step 3: Storage Cost**

$$S_{GB} = 5 \times 250 = 1250 \;\text{GB}$$

$$C_{sto} = 1250 \times 0.170 = \$212.50$$

**Step 4: Egress Cost**

$$C_{egress} = (2048 - 1) \times 0.12 = 2047 \times 0.12 = \$245.64$$

**Step 5: Total Without SUD**

$$C_{total} = 968.64 + 526.46 + 212.50 + 245.64 = \$1{,}953.24$$

**Step 6: With 30% SUD (compute portion)**

$$C_{compute}^{SUD} = (968.64 + 526.46) \times 0.70 = 1049.57$$

$$C_{total}^{SUD} = 1049.57 + 212.50 + 245.64 = \$1{,}507.71$$

**Savings:** $\$1{,}953.24 - \$1{,}507.71 = \$445.53$ (≈ 22.8%).

*(7 marks: 1 per step, plus a final summary line.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**GCP Networking Services:**

1. **Virtual Private Cloud (VPC):**
   - A software-defined, logically isolated private network in GCP.
   - Supports **subnets (regional)**, **firewall rules**, **routes**, **shared VPC** across multiple projects.
   - Global by default — subnets can span zones within a region.

2. **Cloud Load Balancing:**
   - **Single global anycast IP** for all users worldwide.
   - Supports **L4 (Network Load Balancer)** and **L7 (HTTP(S) Load Balancer)**.
   - **Health checks** automatically route around failed backends.
   - Built-in **DDoS protection** via Google Front End (GFE).

3. **Cloud CDN (Content Delivery Network):**
   - Caches content at **200+ Edge PoPs** globally.
   - Reduces latency by serving content from the nearest PoP.
   - Integrated with Cloud Load Balancing — simply enable a checkbox.

4. **Cloud Interconnect:**
   - **Dedicated Connection:** Direct physical link (10 Gbps / 100 Gbps) between on-premises data center and Google.
   - **Partner Interconnect:** Through a supported service provider (50 Mbps – 50 Gbps).
   - Provides **lower latency, higher throughput, and reduced egress costs** compared to public internet.

**Role of Google's Private Global Network:**

- Google owns and operates one of the **largest private fiber-optic networks in the world** (≈ 2 million miles of fiber).
- All GCP traffic within Google’s network **never touches the public internet** — this is called **"Premium Tier"** networking.
- This results in **lower latency, higher security, and consistent performance** compared to AWS/Azure in some benchmarks.

*(7 marks: 5 for service descriptions + 2 for private network role.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Region with Zone** — Region is geographic; Zone is a fault-isolated location within a region.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1 GB free egress** — Always subtract 1 GB before computing egress cost.
> 3. **Multiplying storage cost by hours** — pd-ssd is charged **per GB-month**, not per hour. Many students incorrectly multiply by 720.
> 4. **SUD is AUTOMATIC, but only on Compute Engine vCPU and RAM** — It does NOT apply to disk or egress. Don't apply it blindly.
> 5. **GKE ≠ Compute Engine** — GKE is a managed Kubernetes service; CE provides raw VMs. Do not interchange them in comparisons.
> 6. **Confusing FaaS with PaaS** — Cloud Functions (FaaS) triggers on events; App Engine (PaaS) serves HTTP requests continuously.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 – Google Cloud Platform):**

- **GCP** = Google's public cloud suite, launched officially in **2008** (App Engine) / opened to public in **2014**.
- **Hierarchy:** Organization → Folders → Projects → Resources.
- **IAM** is **inherited top-down**.
- **Region** = Geographic area; **Zone** = Isolated failure domain within a region.
- **Global private network** = Differentiator; uses **Premium Tier** routing.
- **Compute services:** Compute Engine (IaaS), GKE (Container), App Engine (PaaS), Cloud Functions (FaaS), Cloud Run (Serverless Container).
- **Storage services:** Cloud Storage (Object), Persistent Disk (Block), Cloud SQL (RDBMS), Spanner (Global RDBMS), Bigtable (NoSQL Wide-Column).
- **Big Data:** BigQuery (DWH), Dataflow (Beam), Dataproc (Hadoop/Spark), Pub/Sub (messaging).
- **Pricing:** On-Demand, SUD (auto, up to 30%), CUD (up to 70%), Spot VMs (up to 91%).
- **Cost formulas** use vCPU-hours, GB-RAM-hours, GB-months for disk, and GB-egress.
- **Egress:** First 1 GB/month free in GCP; **NOT** free in general.
- **SUD applies** automatically to vCPU + RAM only (not disk, not egress).
- **Anthos** = GCP's hybrid/multi-cloud platform (based on Kubernetes).
- **Vertex AI** = Unified ML platform (2021).
- **Sustainability:** Carbon-neutral since **2007**; goal of 24/7 carbon-free by **2030**.

> **End of Module 1 – Google Cloud Platform Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Architectures - Monolithic & Distributed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT745 - CLOUD, MICROSERVICES AND APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Application Architectures - Monolithic & Distributed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Application Architectures - Monolithic & Distributed

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Monolithic Application Architecture

A **Monolithic Architecture** is a traditional, unified software design model where all the functional modules of an application (User Interface (UI), Business Logic, Data Access Layer (DAL), and Database integrations) are tightly coupled, packaged, and deployed together as a single, indivisible unit. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is formally defined as a "single-tiered, single-codebase software architectural pattern where the application is built and shipped as one unified artifact."

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight**
> The term "monolith" originates from the Greek word *monolithos* (single stone). In software engineering, it refers to a system where logical separations (like front-end, back-end, database) exist in code, but they are physically deployed as one executable file, one WAR/EAR package, or one container image.

#### Conceptual Analogy: The Restaurant Analogy

Imagine a **single chef running a small restaurant**. The chef takes orders, cooks the food, serves the customers, handles billing, and washes the dishes — all by themselves, in a single physical location. 

- If the chef gets sick, the **entire restaurant shuts down**.
- If the restaurant becomes famous and 500 people show up, the **single chef cannot scale** to handle the load.
- To add a new dish (a new feature), the chef has to **stop everything and learn it from scratch**.

This is exactly how a monolithic application behaves. All functionalities (UI, business logic, database calls) are baked into one codebase and run as a single process. One failure can crash the entire system, and scaling means duplicating the *entire application*, not just the busy part.

---

### 1.2 Distributed Application Architecture

A **Distributed Architecture** is a software design paradigm in which the application's components (services) are decoupled, independently deployable, and communicate with each other across a network using well-defined interfaces (typically lightweight protocols like HTTP/REST, gRPC, or message queues like RabbitMQ/Kafka). Each service is responsible for a single business capability and can be developed, deployed, and scaled independently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight**
> A distributed application is a system where components, located on networked computers, coordinate their actions by passing messages. The key shift from monolith to distributed is the **separation of concerns** at the *deployment* level, not just the *code* level.

#### Conceptual Analogy: The Large Restaurant Chain Analogy

Now imagine a **massive restaurant chain** like McDonald's. The chain has separate, specialized units:
- A **Call Center** team takes orders.
- A **Kitchen Team** prepares food.
- A **Delivery Team** handles dispatch.
- A **Billing Team** processes payments.
- Each team is in its own building, uses its own tools, and can be **hired, fired, or expanded** independently.

If the call center gets flooded with calls, you hire **more call center staff** — you don't hire more chefs. If a payment system goes down, the kitchen and delivery keep working. This is the essence of distributed architecture: **independent services that communicate over a network**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Trade-off Intuition**
> Monolith = Simplicity over Scalability. Distributed = Scalability over Simplicity. The KTU syllabus emphasizes that **distributed systems trade operational complexity for business agility**.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|---|---|
| **Tight Coupling** | A change in one module requires changes in another (monolith) |
| **Loose Coupling** | Services are independent; interface changes don't break consumers (distributed) |
| **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** | One component failure brings down the whole system (common in monolith) |
| **Horizontal Scalability** | Adding more machines to handle load (distributed-friendly) |
| **Vertical Scalability** | Adding more power (CPU/RAM) to one machine (monolith-friendly) |
| **Service Boundary** | Logical contract defining what a service does and what it exposes |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Monolithic Architecture

A monolithic application is typically organized into three primary internal layers, all sharing a **single runtime process** and a **single database schema**:

- **Presentation Layer (UI Tier):** Handles HTTP requests, renders HTML/JSON. Built using frameworks like Spring MVC, Django, or ASP.NET.
- **Business Logic Layer (Application Tier):** Contains the domain rules, validations, and workflow orchestration. This is the "brain" of the application.
- **Data Access Layer (Persistence Tier):** Communicates with a centralized database (e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle) using ORM tools like Hibernate or Entity Framework.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works for small teams:** The "one codebase, one process" model offers excellent **IDE support**, **easy debugging** (single stack trace), **straightforward testing**, and **simple deployment** (copy a single JAR/WAR file). For startups and small products, monoliths are often the *correct* choice.

#### The Operational 'Why' Behind Monolith Constraints

The monolith suffers from three structural diseases as it grows:

- **The "Big Ball of Mud" Effect:** Over time, modules lose clear boundaries. The "Order" code starts calling the "User" code which calls the "Payment" code — creating a web of dependencies that no single developer fully understands.
- **Deployment Lock-Step:** Even if a developer changes one line of code in the "Invoice" module, the *entire* application must be rebuilt, retested, and redeployed. This is the famous **"one-line change, two-week release"** problem.
- **Technology Lock-In:** Because everything is one process, you are bound to a single language/framework. You cannot write the recommendation engine in Python and the payment engine in Java — they share the same JVM/runtime.

---

### 2.2 Anatomy of a Distributed Architecture

In a distributed architecture, the three layers of the monolith are split into **independently deployable services**, each with its own:

- **Own codebase** (often polyglot: Java, Python, Go, Node.js)
- **Own database** (Database-per-Service pattern)
- **Own deployment pipeline** (CI/CD per service)
- **Own runtime** (Docker container, Kubernetes pod)

Services communicate through:

- **Synchronous protocols:** HTTP/REST, gRPC, GraphQL
- **Asynchronous protocols:** Message Queues (RabbitMQ, Kafka), Event Bus

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept**
> The two foundational patterns of distributed architecture are:
> 1. **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA):** Coarse-grained services, often sharing an Enterprise Service Bus (ESB), typically using SOAP/XML.
> 2. **Microservices Architecture:** Fine-grained services, communicating via lightweight protocols (REST/gRPC), following the *Single Responsibility Principle* at the service level. This is the **modern KTU-recommended** distributed pattern.

---

### 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Comparative Cheat Sheet

The following table is **examination-critical**. Memorize the distinctions for Part A and Part B questions.

| Aspect | Monolithic Architecture | Distributed Architecture |
|---|---|---|
| **Deployment Unit** | Single artifact (JAR/WAR/EAR) | Multiple independent artifacts (containers/bins) |
| **Codebase** | One shared repository (monorepo often) | Multiple repositories (polyrepo) or modular monorepo |
| **Database** | One shared database/schema | Database-per-service (decentralized data) |
| **Scalability** | **Vertical** (scale-up: bigger machine) | **Horizontal** (scale-out: more instances) |
| **Failure Impact** | **Total outage** (one process fails = system fails) | **Partial degradation** (circuit breakers, fallbacks) |
| **Communication** | In-memory function calls | Network calls (HTTP, gRPC, queues) |
| **Latency** | **Low** (no network hop) | **Higher** (network overhead: ~1–10 ms per call) |
| **Consistency Model** | Strong ACID transactions easy | **BASE / Eventual Consistency** (CAP theorem applies) |
| **Team Structure** | Single large team | **Conway's Law:** small, autonomous teams per service |
| **Technology Stack** | Uniform (one language) | **Polyglot** (Java, Python, Go, Node.js mixed) |
| **Testing** | Simple (in-process unit tests) | Complex (contract tests, integration tests, chaos tests) |
| **Best Suited For** | Small teams, MVP, single-domain apps | Large teams, high-scale, multi-domain platforms |
| **KTU Example** | Traditional banking core systems | Netflix, Amazon, Uber, Airbnb |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often claim monoliths are "outdated" or "bad." This is **wrong for KTU exams**. Monoliths are *not* an anti-pattern — they are the **pragmatic choice for small systems**. The correct answer is always **context-driven**: monolith for simplicity, distributed for scale.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The choice between monolithic and distributed is not academic — it directly impacts **business metrics**:

- **Time-to-Market:** Monoliths ship features faster initially (no inter-service contract design).
- **Cost:** Monoliths have lower infrastructure costs (1 server) vs. distributed (N servers + service mesh + observability stack).
- **Reliability:** Distributed systems can achieve **higher availability** (99.99%) through redundancy, but introduce new failure modes (network partitions, cascading failures).
- **Maintainability:** Beyond ~50,000–100,000 lines of code, monoliths suffer from **"merge hell"** and **exponential bug-fix cost**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Quantifying Monolith vs Distributed Performance: Amdahl's Law

A core analytical tool in distributed architecture is **Amdahl's Law**, which quantifies the theoretical speedup gained by parallelizing a system. This is highly relevant when comparing a single-process monolith (no parallelism) to a distributed system (parallel services).

**Amdahl's Law Formula:**

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}$$

Where:
- $S(N)$ = Theoretical speedup factor with $N$ parallel processing units
- $P$ = Proportion of the task that *can* be parallelized (expressed as a decimal, e.g., 0.8 for 80%)
- $N$ = Number of parallel processing units (e.g., 8 microservices)
- $(1 - P)$ = The strictly sequential portion (cannot be parallelized)

#### Step-by-Step Derivation of Amdahl's Law

**Step 1: Define the total execution time of a sequential (monolith) task.**

Let $T(1)$ be the time taken by a single processor to complete the task. By definition:

$$T(1) = 1 \text{ (normalized time unit)}$$

This is the baseline performance of a monolithic system running on one machine.

**Step 2: Partition the task into parallel and serial fractions.**

The task is split into two parts:
- A **parallelizable portion** of size $P$ (e.g., order processing, recommendation generation, image transcoding).
- A **strictly sequential portion** of size $(1 - P)$ (e.g., authentication, database commits, transaction coordination).

The original time equation becomes:

$$T(1) = (1 - P) + P = 1$$

This is an identity, confirming the baseline.

**Step 3: Apply parallelization to the $P$ portion across $N$ workers.**

In a distributed system, $N$ workers share the parallel portion. Each worker handles a fraction $\frac{P}{N}$ of the parallel load. The sequential portion $(1 - P)$ cannot be divided and must remain on a single coordinator.

The new execution time on $N$ parallel workers is:

$$T(N) = (1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}$$

**Step 4: Compute the speedup ratio $S(N)$.**

Speedup is defined as the ratio of sequential time to parallel time:

$$S(N) = \frac{T(1)}{T(N)} = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}$$

**Step 5: Analyze the asymptotic limit as $N \rightarrow \infty$.**

As the number of distributed workers grows indefinitely:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}$$

Since $\frac{P}{N} \to 0$ as $N \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{1 - P}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (KTU Exam Favorite):** Even with *infinite* distributed workers, the maximum speedup is bounded by $\frac{1}{1 - P}$. This proves that **the sequential portion is the bottleneck**. If 20% of your system cannot be parallelized ($P = 0.8$), the absolute maximum speedup — no matter how many microservices you spin up — is **5x**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Monolith vs Distributed

**Problem Statement:** A monolithic e-commerce application has an average request processing time of **1000 ms**. Analysis shows that 80% of the request can be parallelized across independent services (catalog, cart, payment, inventory). Compute the speedup when scaled to (a) 4 services and (b) 16 services.

**Given:**
- $T(1) = 1000$ ms
- $P = 0.80$ (80% parallelizable)
- $(1 - P) = 0.20$ (20% strictly serial, e.g., authentication + DB commit)

**Solution (a) — For $N = 4$ services:**

$$T(4) = (1 - P) + \frac{P}{N} = 0.20 + \frac{0.80}{4}$$

$$T(4) = 0.20 + 0.20 = 0.40 \text{ (normalized)}$$

$$T(4)_{\text{actual}} = 0.40 \times 1000 = 400 \text{ ms}$$

$$S(4) = \frac{1}{0.40} = 2.5\times \text{ speedup}$$

**Solution (b) — For $N = 16$ services:**

$$T(16) = 0.20 + \frac{0.80}{16}$$

$$T(16) = 0.20 + 0.05 = 0.25 \text{ (normalized)}$$

$$T(16)_{\text{actual}} = 0.25 \times 1000 = 250 \text{ ms}$$

$$S(16) = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4.0\times \text{ speedup}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Doubling the services from 4 to 16 (a 4x increase) only improved speedup from 2.5x to 4.0x (a 1.6x improvement). This is **Amdahl's Law in action** — diminishing returns as $N$ grows. This justifies why KTU 2024 syllabus warns: *"Do not over-distribute. Microservice proliferation is an anti-pattern."*

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use What

Use this decision framework, a high-yield KTU examination point, to architect a new application:

| System Characteristic | Recommended Architecture | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Team size $\leq 5$ developers, MVP/Prototype | **Monolith** | Coordination cost outweighs scalability need |
| Expected users $< 10,000$ | **Monolith** | Vertical scaling is cheaper than distributed overhead |
| Team size $\geq 20$, multiple BUs | **Distributed (Microservices)** | Conway's Law demands team autonomy |
| Expected users $> 1$ million, global | **Distributed** | Horizontal scaling across regions is mandatory |
| Regulatory isolation (PCI-DSS, HIPAA) | **Distributed** | Service boundaries enable data isolation |
| Tight ACID transactions required | **Monolith** | Distributed XA transactions are slow and complex |
| High deployment frequency (multiple/day) | **Distributed** | Independent deploys prevent "release train" blocking |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Monolithic Architecture — Block Diagram

The following diagram illustrates the **single-process, single-database** structure of a monolithic application. All modules share the same runtime and the same data store.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientA[Client Browser or Mobile App]
    loadbalA[Load Balancer]
    monolithA[Monolithic Application - Single Process]
    subgraph monoBlockA[Monolith Runtime]
        uiA[Presentation Layer - UI and View]
        bizA[Business Logic Layer - Domain Rules]
        dalA[Data Access Layer - ORM and Repos]
    end
    dbA[(Centralized Shared Database)]
    clientA --> loadbalA
    loadbalA --> monolithA
    monolithA --> uiA
    uiA --> bizA
    bizA --> dalA
    dalA --> dbA
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- The dashed outer box represents **one deployable unit** (one JAR/WAR file).
- The three internal modules (UI, Business, DAL) are **logically separated** but **physically fused** in one process.
- The shared database is a **single point of failure** and a **scaling bottleneck**.

---

### 4.2 Distributed (Microservices) Architecture — Block Diagram

The following diagram shows a **decoupled, polyglot, multi-database** distributed system. Each service is an independent process with its own data store.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientB[Client Browser or Mobile App]
    gatewayB[API Gateway - Single Entry Point]
    subgraph distributedBlock[Distributed Runtime - Independent Services]
        authB[Auth Service - Java]
        catalogB[Catalog Service - Python]
        cartB[Cart Service - NodeJS]
        paymentB[Payment Service - Go]
        orderB[Order Service - Java]
        notifyB[Notification Service - Python]
    end
    msgBusB[Message Bus - Kafka or RabbitMQ]
    dbAuth[(Auth DB - PostgreSQL)]
    dbCat[(Catalog DB - MongoDB)]
    dbCart[(Cart DB - Redis)]
    dbPay[(Payment DB - PostgreSQL)]
    dbOrder[(Order DB - MySQL)]
    clientB --> gatewayB
    gatewayB --> authB
    gatewayB --> catalogB
    gatewayB --> cartB
    gatewayB --> paymentB
    gatewayB --> orderB
    orderB -. Async Event .-> msgBusB
    msgBusB -.-> notifyB
    authB --> dbAuth
    catalogB --> dbCat
    cartB --> dbCart
    paymentB --> dbPay
    orderB --> dbOrder
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- The **API Gateway** acts as the single entry point, performing authentication, rate limiting, and request routing.
- Each service has its **own database** — the *Database-per-Service* pattern, a cornerstone of microservices.
- The **dotted lines** represent **asynchronous** communication (e.g., event publishing to a message bus), decoupling services in time.
- Solid lines represent **synchronous** HTTP/gRPC calls.

---

### 4.3 Monolith vs Distributed: Side-by-Side Visual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph leftSide[Monolithic Architecture]
        L1[UI]
        L2[Business Logic]
        L3[Data Access]
        L4[(Single Database)]
        L1 --- L2 --- L3 --- L4
    end
    subgraph rightSide[Distributed Architecture]
        R1[UI Service]
        R2[Order Service]
        R3[Payment Service]
        R4[(DB1)]
        R5[(DB2)]
        R6[(DB3)]
        R1 --- R2
        R2 --- R3
        R2 --- R4
        R3 --- R5
        R3 --- R6
    end
```

---

### 4.4 Failure Domain Comparison Diagram

This diagram contrasts the **blast radius** of a failure in each architecture — a frequently tested KTU concept.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph monoFailure[Monolith Failure Scenario]
        m1[Module X Crashes]
        m2[Entire Application Down]
        m3[All Users Affected]
        m1 --> m2 --> m3
    end
    subgraph distFailure[Distributed Failure Scenario]
        d1[Payment Service Crashes]
        d2[Circuit Breaker Triggers]
        d3[Orders Still Accepted - Payment Retried Later]
        d4[Only Checkout Users Affected]
        d1 --> d2 --> d3
        d2 --> d4
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Monolithic Architecture. List two advantages. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Monolithic Architecture** is a software design pattern in which the entire application — User Interface, Business Logic, and Data Access Layer — is built, packaged, and deployed as a single, indivisible unit sharing one runtime process and one database.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Simplicity of Development and Deployment:** A single codebase with a unified build pipeline makes onboarding, debugging, and shipping straightforward. *(1 mark)*
2. **Low Latency:** Internal modules communicate via in-memory function calls (microseconds), avoiding network overhead typical of distributed systems. *(1 mark)*
3. **Easier Testing:** End-to-end testing can be performed in a single process without network stubs. *(Bonus - 1 mark)*

---

#### Question 2: What is a Distributed Application Architecture? Mention one key challenge. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Distributed Application Architecture** is a system in which independent, loosely-coupled services are deployed across networked machines and coordinate via message passing (HTTP, gRPC, or message queues). Each service owns its data and can be scaled, updated, and failed independently. *(2 marks)*

**Key Challenge — Network Reliability:**
Distributed systems must handle partial failures, network partitions, message duplication, and latency variance — failures that do not exist in a single-process monolith. This violates the developer's mental model of "local function calls." *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Rule:** Part B Module 2 questions carry an **internal choice**. You must answer either **Question A** OR **Question B** in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Total = **14 marks**.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare Monolithic and Distributed (Microservices) architectures across **six key dimensions** (deployment, scalability, fault tolerance, consistency, technology stack, and team structure). Use a comparison table in your answer. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of Amdahl's Law, explain why a system with 90% parallelizable code ($P = 0.9$) achieves a maximum theoretical speedup of only 10x, regardless of how many additional services are added. Show all derivation steps. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Dimension | Monolithic | Distributed (Microservices) |
|---|---|---|
| **Deployment Unit** | Single artifact (JAR/WAR) | Multiple independent containers |
| **Scalability** | Vertical (scale-up via bigger machine) | Horizontal (scale-out via more instances) |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Low — one crash brings down all | High — circuit breakers isolate failures |
| **Data Consistency** | Strong ACID (single DB) | Eventual consistency (CAP theorem) |
| **Technology Stack** | Uniform (single language) | Polyglot (Java, Python, Go, etc.) |
| **Team Structure** | One large team, shared codebase | Small autonomous teams per service |

*For each row, the student is expected to provide a one-line justification. Award 1 mark per correctly contrasted dimension, 1 mark for table presentation. Total 7 marks.*

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $P = 0.9$, therefore $(1 - P) = 0.1$.

**Step 1:** Apply Amdahl's Law:

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}} = \frac{1}{0.1 + \frac{0.9}{N}}$$

**Step 2:** As $N \to \infty$, the term $\frac{0.9}{N} \to 0$:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{0.1 + 0} = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the maximum speedup is bounded at **10x**. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation (3 marks):**
The 10% sequential portion (e.g., authentication, distributed transaction coordination, logging) forms a hard ceiling. No matter how many microservice instances you spin up, these serial operations form a queue at a single bottleneck. This is why:
- Database write contention limits distributed throughput.
- Two-phase commit (2PC) for distributed transactions is notoriously slow.
- Critics of "naive microservices" cite this exact Amdahl bound to argue against over-decomposition.

**Step 5 — Architectural Implication (2 marks):**
To break this ceiling, you must **reduce the sequential fraction** — for example, by using:
- Asynchronous event-driven workflows (sagas) instead of synchronous 2PC.
- Sharding the database to reduce write contention.
- CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) to separate read and write paths.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three-layered structure of a Monolithic application** with a neat block diagram. Discuss why monoliths are not inherently "bad" and identify the specific conditions under which they should be preferred over distributed systems. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **CAP Theorem** in the context of distributed architectures. Explain how a distributed e-commerce system must make trade-offs between Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance, with a real-world scenario. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**The Three Layers (4 marks):**
1. **Presentation Layer:** Handles HTTP requests, renders UI (HTML/JSON). Built using Spring MVC, Django, or ASP.NET.
2. **Business Logic Layer:** Domain rules, validations, and workflow orchestration. The "brain" of the application.
3. **Data Access Layer:** Talks to the database using ORM (Hibernate, Entity Framework) or raw SQL.

*Each layer is awarded 1 mark. The fourth mark is for the block diagram showing their interconnections within a single process.*

**Why Monoliths Are Not "Bad" (3 marks):**
1. **Pragmatic for Small Systems:** A startup with 5 engineers does not need Netflix's architecture. Monoliths reduce coordination overhead.
2. **Easier Debugging:** Single stack trace, single log file, single profiler view.
3. **Lower Operational Cost:** One server, one database, no service mesh, no Kubernetes. Significantly cheaper for low-traffic apps.
4. **Stronger Consistency:** Single ACID-compliant database, no eventual consistency anomalies.

**Conditions Favoring Monoliths:**
- Team size $\leq 10$ developers
- Domain is small and well-understood
- Traffic is predictable and $\leq$ moderate scale
- Time-to-market is critical (startup MVP)

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**CAP Theorem Statement (2 marks):**
Proposed by Eric Brewer (1999), the CAP Theorem states that in any **distributed data store**, you can simultaneously guarantee **only two** of the following three properties:
- **C — Consistency:** Every read receives the most recent write or an error.
- **A — Availability:** Every request receives a non-error response (no guarantee of latest data).
- **P — Partition Tolerance:** The system continues to operate despite arbitrary network message loss between nodes.

Since network partitions (P) **must** be tolerated in any real distributed system, the real-world trade-off is between **CP** and **AP**.

**Real-World E-Commerce Scenario (5 marks):**

Consider an e-commerce platform with microservices: `Catalog`, `Cart`, `Order`, and `Payment`.

**Scenario A — Checkout Page (CP choice):**
- A network partition occurs between the `Order` and `Payment` services.
- The system chooses **Consistency over Availability** (CP).
- **Behavior:** The checkout button is disabled, an error "Service temporarily unavailable, please retry" is shown. The system refuses to place an order that may not be paid for, preventing data corruption (e.g., a paid order with no inventory reservation).
- **Why:** Financial integrity is non-negotiable. A duplicate charge is worse than a temporary outage.

**Scenario B — Product Browsing (AP choice):**
- A partition occurs between `Catalog` and the cache.
- The system chooses **Availability over Consistency** (AP).
- **Behavior:** The product page still loads, but stock counts may be slightly stale ("5 left" when only 3 are actually available). Users can still browse and add to cart.
- **Why:** Losing a browsing session is worse than showing slightly stale inventory. Eventual consistency is acceptable for catalog data.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Read Carefully):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing "CAP says pick 2 of 3 at design time." This is **WRONG**. Partition Tolerance is **mandatory** in any network-based system. The choice is always between **CP and AP** during a partition, not at design time.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing CAP with ACID. CAP is a *distributed consistency* theorem; ACID is a *database transaction* property.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting the diagram or example. The 7-mark question demands a **scenario with at least 2 services** interacting. A textual definition alone will not secure full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam):**

- **Monolith = Single Codebase + Single Process + Single Database + Single Deployment.** Easy to build, hard to scale. Ideal for MVPs and small teams.

- **Distributed = Multiple Services + Multiple Databases + Network Communication + Independent Deployment.** Hard to build, easy to scale. Ideal for large platforms and high-traffic systems.

- **Monolith Strengths:** Low latency (in-memory calls), strong ACID consistency, simple debugging, lower cost.

- **Distributed Strengths:** Independent scalability, fault isolation, technology heterogeneity, team autonomy (Conway's Law).

- **Monolith Weaknesses:** Single point of failure, technology lock-in, deployment lock-step, "big ball of mud" as code grows.

- **Distributed Weaknesses:** Network failures, eventual consistency, operational complexity, distributed transaction cost.

- **Amdahl's Law Formula (MUST MEMORIZE):** $S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}$. Maximum speedup as $N \to \infty$ is $\frac{1}{1 - P}$.

- **CAP Theorem (MUST MEMORIZE):** In a network partition, choose between **CP (Consistency + Partition tolerance)** or **AP (Availability + Partition tolerance)**. P is non-negotiable.

- **Key Real-World Examples to Quote in Exams:**
  - **Monolith:** Amazon (pre-2001), Shopify (Ruby on Rails monolith), Stack Overflow, Instagram (early).
  - **Distributed:** Netflix (cloud-native microservices), Uber, Airbnb, Amazon (post-2001 "API mandating" letter by Bezos).

- **Golden Rule:** "Start with a monolith. Decompose to microservices **only when pain points emerge**" — Martin Fowler's law of microservices. Always quote this in conclusion-style answers.

- **Exam Scoring Hack:** For 14-mark Part B questions, always include (1) a diagram, (2) a comparison table, and (3) a real-world example. Examiners reward visual and contextual answers generously.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microservice Fundamentals and Design Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT745 - CLOUD, MICROSERVICES AND APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Microservice Fundamentals and Design Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Microservices? (Formal KTU Definition)

**Microservices** — also called the **microservice architecture** — is an architectural style that structures an application as a collection of **small, autonomous, loosely coupled services**, each focused on a single business capability, communicating over lightweight protocols (typically **HTTP/REST** or messaging queues), and **independently deployable** by fully automated continuous delivery pipelines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard Board Term):**
> A *microservice* is a self-contained, independently deployable, single-purpose service that implements a bounded business capability, owns its own data store, and communicates with other services through well-defined APIs and lightweight mechanisms. Each microservice is a **mini-application** in itself, with its own hexagonal architecture (business logic + adapters + database).

### Key Industry Term — Origin
The term was first coined at a **software architecture workshop near Venice in 2011**, and popularized by **Martin Fowler & James Lewis (2014)** in their seminal paper *"Microservices: a definition of this new architectural term"*. In the KTU context, the canonical reference is the **Fowler–Lewis 9 Common Characteristics** which examiners frequently quote.

---

## 1.2 The 9 Defining Characteristics of Microservices (Fowler–Lewis Model)

| # | Characteristic | One-line Essence |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Componentization via Services** | Replaceable units organized as services |
| 2 | **Organized around Business Capability** | Cross-functional teams owning a capability end-to-end |
| 3 | **Products, not Projects** | "You build it, you run it" ownership |
| 4 | **Smart Endpoints & Dumb Pipes** | Business logic lives in services, not in ESB/middleware |
| 5 | **Decentralized Governance** | Freedom to pick the right tool per service |
| 6 | **Decentralized Data Management** | Each service owns its private database |
| 7 | **Infrastructure Automation** | CI/CD, containers, IaC (Terraform/CloudFormation) |
| 8 | **Design for Failure** | Processes die and are restarted; expect failures |
| 9 | **Evolutionary Design** | Replace, not rewrite; incremental change |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasizes **Characteristics 2, 4, 6, 7, and 8**. Examiners will ask you to explain *why* smart endpoints and dumb pipes beat traditional ESB-heavy Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA).

---

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant vs. The Central Kitchen"

> [!TIP]
> **Restaurant Analogy for First-Time Learners**
>
> Imagine two ways to run a large food company:
>
> **Monolith = One Giant Central Kitchen**
> A single massive kitchen prepares *every* dish. If the chef is sick, *all* orders stop. If you need to add a new dessert, you must renovate the *entire* kitchen. Scaling means building a *bigger* kitchen.
>
> **Microservices = Food Court with Many Small Stalls**
> Each stall (Order, Payment, Delivery, Desserts) is a **small independent unit** with its own chef, ingredients, and till. If the Dessert stall closes, the rest still runs. You add a new stall without disturbing others. You scale just the busy stall.
>
> **Key mappings:**
> - Stall = Microservice
> - Order ticket on counter = REST API call
> - Each stall's pantry = Private database
> - Food court manager = Service Registry & API Gateway

---

## 1.4 Microservice vs. Monolith vs. SOA (The Crucial KTU Comparison)

| Dimension | Monolith | SOA | Microservices |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unit of deploy | Single big unit | Multiple services | Many tiny services |
| Coupling | High (in-process) | Loosely coupled | Loosely + independently coupled |
| Data | Shared DB | Shared DB often | **Per-service private DB** |
| Communication | Function calls | **ESB (heavy)** | **HTTP/REST, gRPC, MQ (light)** |
| Tech stack | Single | Often single | **Polyglot** (polyglot persistence + polyglot programming) |
| Governance | Centralized | Centralized standards | Decentralized |
| Failure impact | Whole app | Partial | Partial + isolated via **circuit breakers** |
| Best for | Small apps, MVP | Enterprise integration | Cloud-native, scalable, evolving systems |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Microservice — Internal Hexagonal Structure

Each microservice is internally organized as a **hexagonal architecture** (Ports & Adapters) introduced by Alistair Cockburn. This is high-yield KTU content.

### Three concentric layers inside ONE microservice:

**Layer A — Business Logic (Core Domain)**
- Pure domain entities, aggregates, value objects
- No framework dependencies (no `@RestController`, no `@Entity` annotations leak here)
- Encapsulates the bounded context
- Contains the **Use Case** / **Application Service** that orchestrates the domain

**Layer B — Inbound Adapters (Driving Side)**
- REST controllers, gRPC service stubs, GraphQL resolvers, CLI handlers, Message listeners
- Translates external transport (JSON over HTTP, Protobuf) into domain method calls
- Performs input validation, DTO ↔ Entity mapping

**Layer C — Outbound Adapters (Driven Side)**
- Database repositories (JPA, JDBC, DynamoDB SDK, Mongo driver)
- HTTP clients to *other* microservices
- Message publishers (Kafka, RabbitMQ)
- Email/SMS gateway adapters

> [!NOTE]
> **Ports** = Java/Kotlin **interfaces** in the core
> **Adapters** = Concrete implementations of those interfaces in the outer rings
> This is precisely why swapping a MySQL database for MongoDB requires touching **only Layer C** — a hallmark of clean microservice design.

---

## 2.2 Granularity: The "Just Right" Microservice Size

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark question** territory. The granularity question has no magic number, but the **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)** at the service level gives a heuristic:

> **Rule of Thumb (Lewis-Fowler):** A microservice should be *as small as possible but as large as needed* to express one **bounded context** end-to-end. **Two-pizza team** ownership (Amazon's heuristic) is a useful proxy.

### 2.2.1 Domain-Driven Design (DDD) — Bounded Context

Eric Evans' DDD gives the **theoretically correct** decomposition rule:

- A **Bounded Context** = a boundary within which a domain model is **consistent and unambiguous**
- The word "Account" means different things in *Banking*, *Authentication*, and *Billing* contexts
- Each **bounded context → one microservice** (ideal)
- The shared kernel, customer-supplier, and anti-corruption layer (ACL) patterns handle inter-context communication

### 2.2.2 The "Nano-service Anti-pattern"

If a service is **smaller than its bounded context**, you get:

> [!WARNING]
> **Chatty Services / Distributed Monolith Anti-pattern**
> - 50 services for what should be 5
> - Network hop overhead dominates
> - Distributed transactions become unmanageable
> - Testing, tracing, and debugging nightmare
> - Frequently the result of misapplying SRP at the *class* level instead of the *service* level

### 2.2.3 The "Modular Monolith Alternative"

When a domain is **not well understood**, experts (Fowler, Sam Newman) recommend starting with a **modular monolith** — single deployment, but strict internal module boundaries enforced by package-private visibility and build-time checks. This is *not* an anti-pattern; it is a **deliberate evolutionary design strategy**.

---

## 2.3 Communication Patterns — The Lifeline of Microservices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Fundamental Styles of Inter-Service Communication**

| Style | Pattern | Tech Examples | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous** | Request/Response | REST over HTTP, gRPC, GraphQL | Real-time queries, immediate response needed |
| **Asynchronous** | Event-driven | Kafka, RabbitMQ, NATS, AWS SQS, SQS-SNS | State propagation, decoupled workflows |

### 2.3.1 Synchronous — REST vs. gRPC

**REST + JSON over HTTP**
- Human-readable, easy to debug (curl, Postman)
- Text-heavy (network bandwidth)
- HTTP/1.1 head-of-line blocking; HTTP/2 mitigates
- Loosely typed at runtime unless OpenAPI/JSON-Schema is enforced

**gRPC (Google Remote Procedure Call)**
- Uses **HTTP/2** + **Protocol Buffers (protobuf)** binary serialization
- 5–10× faster than REST/JSON for high-throughput inter-service calls
- Strongly typed contracts via `.proto` files
- Supports **bi-directional streaming**
- Generated stubs in 11+ languages
- Used heavily inside **service meshes** like Istio and Linkerd

### 2.3.2 Asynchronous — Event-Driven Architecture (EDA)

Two flavors of EDA:

**a) Event Notification (fire-and-forget)**
- Producer publishes "OrderPlaced" event
- Consumers (Inventory, Payment, Notification) react
- No return value; minimal coupling
- Risk: silent failure if no consumer-side monitoring

**b) Event Sourcing**
- The **state** of an entity is derived by replaying all events
- Events are the **source of truth**, not the current row in DB
- Enables **temporal queries**, **audit trail**, and **time-travel debugging**
- Needs **event store** (e.g., EventStoreDB, Kafka with compaction)
- Complex; tradeoff between audit and query complexity

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Hook:** Mention **Choreography vs. Orchestration** when discussing EDA.
> - **Choreography:** services react to events; no central brain (decentralized, scales well, harder to trace)
> - **Orchestration:** a central **Saga orchestrator** (or BPMN engine) tells each service what to do (centralized, easier to monitor, but coupled to orchestrator)

---

## 2.4 Data Management — The Hardest Problem in Microservices

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule 1 — Database-per-Service (Polyglot Persistence)**
> No two services share a database. Sharing a DB creates a *distributed monolith* in disguise.

> **Rule 2 — No Distributed Transactions (No 2PC)**
> Two-phase commit is **forbidden** in microservice design because of the **CAP Theorem** trade-off and latency. Replaced by the **Saga Pattern**.

### 2.4.1 The Saga Pattern (Stephen Marquez, 1987)

A saga is a sequence of **local transactions** $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n$ such that for each $T_i$, there exists a **compensating transaction** $C_i$ that semantically undoes $T_i$.

**Example: Order Placement Saga**
- $T_1$ = Create Order (Order Service)
- $T_2$ = Reserve Stock (Inventory Service)
- $T_3$ = Charge Payment (Payment Service)
- $T_4$ = Send Confirmation (Notification Service)

**Compensating actions:**
- $C_1$ = Cancel Order
- $C_2$ = Release Stock
- $C_3$ = Refund Payment
- $C_4$ = (no-op or apologize)

**Two implementation styles:**
- **Orchestrated Saga:** central coordinator invokes each step; easy to understand
- **Choreographed Saga:** services publish events; each service listens and reacts

### 2.4.2 CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 2000)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAP Theorem Statement**
> In any **distributed data store**, you can simultaneously guarantee at most **two** of the following three properties:
> - **C**onsistency — every read returns the most recent write
> - **A**vailability — every request receives a (non-error) response
> - **P**artition Tolerance — the system continues to operate despite arbitrary network message loss between nodes
>
> Since **P is mandatory** in any real distributed system, the choice is effectively **CP vs. AP**.

**Implication for microservices:** A service spanning multiple datastores will encounter partition; you must design either to **sacrifice availability for consistency** (e.g., banking ledger) or **sacrifice strong consistency for availability** (e.g., social media likes). This is precisely why microservice data design avoids 2PC.

### 2.4.3 CQRS — Command Query Responsibility Segregation

- **Write model (Command)** = normalized, event-sourced
- **Read model (Query)** = denormalized projections in optimized read stores (Elasticsearch, Redis, read replicas)
- Same event stream feeds both
- Trades storage and complexity for **massive read scalability** and **flexible querying**

### 2.4.4 API Composition vs. Event Sourcing vs. Hybrid

| Need | Best Fit |
|---|---|
| Cross-service aggregate query (e.g., "show me order + customer + shipment") | **API Composition** (call multiple services and join) |
| Audit trail, temporal queries | **Event Sourcing** |
| Read-heavy, query-diverse | **CQRS** |
| Mixed | Hybrid: command side event-sourced, query side CQRS projections |

---

## 2.5 Cross-Cutting Concerns (Non-Functional Requirements)

### 2.5.1 Service Discovery

Two flavors:
- **Client-side:** client queries a registry (Eureka, Consul) to find instances
- **Server-side:** client hits a load balancer (AWS ALB, NGINX, Envoy) that knows the instances

### 2.5.2 API Gateway Pattern

A single entry point that:
- Routes requests to the correct service
- Handles authentication, rate limiting, caching
- Aggregates multiple service responses (BFF — Backend for Frontend)
- Translates protocols (REST ↔ gRPC)

### 2.5.3 Circuit Breaker Pattern (Michael Nygard, *Release It!*)

> **Three States:**
> 1. **CLOSED** — normal traffic, requests pass through
> 2. **OPEN** — failures exceed threshold; all requests fail fast (no calls to the broken service)
> 3. **HALF-OPEN** — after a cooldown, allow a few trial requests; if they succeed, return to CLOSED

**Implementations:** Resilience4j (Java), Hystrix (legacy), Polly (.NET), Sentinel (Alibaba).

### 2.5.4 Observability — Logs, Metrics, Traces

- **Logs:** structured JSON (ELK/EFK stack)
- **Metrics:** Prometheus + Grafana (the **RED** method: Rate, Errors, Duration)
- **Traces:** OpenTelemetry, Jaeger, Zipkin (distributed tracing with **trace IDs propagated via W3C `traceparent` header**)

### 2.5.5 Service Mesh

A dedicated infrastructure layer (Istio, Linkerd, Consul Connect) that handles:
- mTLS encryption
- Retries, timeouts, circuit breaking
- Traffic shaping (canary, blue/green)
- Telemetry

Services communicate via **sidecar proxies** (Envoy) — your business code stays clean.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity (Heuristic 1) | $S \le T$ where $S$ = service size, $T$ = two-pizza team | Avoid nano-services |
| Granularity (Heuristic 2) | One service $\leftrightarrow$ one bounded context | DDD-aligned decomposition |
| Saga Compensation | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ with $\forall i, \exists C_i : C_i \circ T_i = \varepsilon$ | Replaces 2PC for distributed workflows |
| CAP trade-off | $\vert C \vert + \vert A \vert + \vert P \vert \le 2$ (at most 2 of 3) | Pick **CP** or **AP** for each datastore |
| Circuit Breaker thresholds | $F_{window} \ge N$ failures in $W$ seconds $\rightarrow$ OPEN | Failure isolation |
| Bulkhead | $W_s \le C_s$ — threads/connections per service bounded | Prevent one service exhausting thread pool |
| Hystrix default values | $W = 10s$, $N = 20$ failures, sleep window = $5s$ | Industry baseline |
| Service Mesh overhead | Latency overhead $\approx 0.5ms$–$3ms$ per sidecar hop | Sidecar cost trade-off |
| Conway's Law | $Architecture = f(Communication\ structure\ of\ org)$ | Team topology drives service boundaries |
| 12-Factor App | 12 rules for cloud-native apps | https://12factor.net |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "How to size a microservice?", answer using **bounded context + team topology + two-pizza team**, *not* "by lines of code" (a common student mistake).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix — Should I Use Microservices?

The KTU board frequently asks: *"Compare Monolith vs. Microservices and identify which to use when."* The following is the **exam-ready decision matrix** with derivations of when to pick which.

### 3.1.1 Decision Flowchart (Logical Derivation)

Step 1 — Start at the root. Ask the following questions in order:

**Q1: Is the domain well-understood, with stable, simple requirements?**
- **YES** → Use a **Modular Monolith** (or simple Monolith). Microservices add cost without value.
- **NO**  → Go to Q2.

**Q2: Is the team > ~50 engineers?**
- **NO** → **Modular Monolith** (Conway's law: small team = small architecture).
- **YES** → Go to Q3.

**Q3: Is independent deployability of components a hard requirement?**
- **NO** → **Modular Monolith with internal modular boundaries**.
- **YES** → Go to Q4.

**Q4: Are there heterogeneous workload profiles (e.g., CPU-heavy ML service vs. I/O-heavy API)?**
- **NO** → Modular Monolith with manual scaling.
- **YES** → **Microservices** (you can scale each service type independently).

**Q5: Is the deployment target a multi-cloud / hybrid cloud with strict fault isolation?**
- **NO** → Microservices on a single cloud.
- **YES** → **Microservices + Service Mesh + multi-region deployment**.

### 3.1.2 Cost-Benefit Trade-off Table

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Benefit}(M) &= \Delta \text{Speed} + \Delta \text{Resilience} + \Delta \text{Scalability} \\
&\quad - \left( \Delta \text{Complexity} + \Delta \text{NetworkRisk} + \Delta \text{OpsCost} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where each $\Delta$ is the marginal change when moving from a monolith to microservices. **Adopt microservices only if Net Benefit > 0.**

**Component analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \text{Complexity} &= C_{\text{distributed}} - C_{\text{in-process}} \\
&= \big(C_{\text{network}} + C_{\text{consistency}} + C_{\text{observability}}\big) - 0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \text{NetworkRisk} &= P_{\text{partition}} \times L_{\text{latency}} + P_{\text{cascading-fail}} \times D_{\text{blast-radius}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \text{Resilience} &= \big(1 - P_{\text{total-fail}}\big) - \big(1 - P_{\text{service-fail}}\big) \\
&= P_{\text{service-fail}} - P_{\text{total-fail}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Where $P_{\text{total-fail}} \le P_{\text{service-fail}}$ always (microservices are more resilient), so $\Delta \text{Resilience} \ge 0$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Saga Compensation Derivation

**Problem:** An e-commerce platform runs an order-placement saga:
- $T_1$ = Reserve stock (Inventory service)
- $T_2$ = Charge payment (Payment service)
- $T_3$ = Create shipment (Shipping service)

If $T_3$ fails after $T_1$ and $T_2$ succeed, derive the compensation sequence.

**Solution (Choreographed):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State}_0 &: \text{Idle} \\
\text{State}_1 &: T_1 \text{ OK} \rightarrow \text{StockReserved} \text{ event published} \\
\text{State}_2 &: T_2 \text{ OK} \rightarrow \text{PaymentCharged} \text{ event published} \\
\text{State}_3 &: T_3 \text{ FAILS} \rightarrow \text{ShipmentFailed} \text{ event published} \\
\text{State}_4 &: C_2 \text{ executes} \rightarrow \text{PaymentRefunded} \\
\text{State}_5 &: C_1 \text{ executes} \rightarrow \text{StockReleased} \\
\text{State}_6 &: \text{Final state: Compensated}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical property of compensating transactions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\forall i \in [1..n]: \quad T_i \circ C_i = \varepsilon \quad \text{(identity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: $C_i$ is **semantic** undo, not literal reversal. For example, refunding money is the *compensating* action for charging money — it is a new transaction, not a "delete the row."

**Idempotency requirement (essential!):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) = f(f(x)) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad f^{(n)}(x) = f(x)\ \ \forall n \ge 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> All saga participants **must** implement idempotency keys because of at-least-once delivery semantics in messaging.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — CAP Theorem Application

**Problem:** You are designing a **banking transaction system**. Must the system choose CP or AP? Justify with a formula.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{User-Visible Cost} &= \alpha \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{Inconsistent}} + \beta \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{Unavailable}}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $\alpha$ = cost of showing a stale balance
- $\beta$ = cost of being unable to transact

For a bank: $\alpha$ is moderate (annoying), $\beta$ is **catastrophic** (regulatory, customer trust loss). Hence $\beta \gg \alpha$.

To **minimize** user-visible cost, we must **avoid unavailability**, but we can tolerate brief inconsistency. So we choose **AP** for the *availability* domain *or* **CP** for the *consistency* domain depending on context.

> **KTU Board Answer:** For **monetary value transfer** (e.g., debit/credit), choose **CP** (e.g., Google Spanner, CockroachDB). For **balance display / read replicas**, choose **AP** with **eventual consistency**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Circuit Breaker State Derivation

**Given:** A circuit breaker with sliding window $W = 10$ seconds, failure threshold $N = 5$ failures, half-open trial count $K = 3$.

**Derive the conditions for state transitions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State} = \begin{cases}
\text{CLOSED} & \text{if } f_{W}(t) < N \text{ and no manual override} \\
\text{OPEN}   & \text{if } f_{W}(t) \ge N \\
\text{HALF\_OPEN} & \text{if state was OPEN for cooldown } \tau_{cooldown} \text{ and } t - t_{open} \ge \tau_{cooldown}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transition rule CLOSED → OPEN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=t-W}^{t} \mathbb{1}_{\text{fail}}(i) \ge N \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{State} \leftarrow \text{OPEN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transition rule OPEN → HALF-OPEN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(t - t_{\text{opened}}) \ge \tau_{\text{cooldown}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{State} \leftarrow \text{HALF\_OPEN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transition rule HALF-OPEN → CLOSED:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j \in \text{trials}} \mathbb{1}_{\text{success}}(j) \ge K \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{State} \leftarrow \text{CLOSED}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transition rule HALF-OPEN → OPEN (re-trip):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j \in \text{trials}} \mathbb{1}_{\text{fail}}(j) \ge 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{State} \leftarrow \text{OPEN},\ t_{\text{opened}} \leftarrow t
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **state machine** implementation behind Resilience4j, Hystrix, and Polly. Reproduce this in your exam for full marks.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix Summary — Mapping Trade-offs to Architecture Decisions

| Problem Symptom | Likely Cause | Recommended Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| Service takes too long to deploy | Monolithic growth | **Decompose by bounded context** |
| Cross-service joins slow | Chatty synchronous calls | **Replace with event-driven + CQRS** |
| One service failure cascades | No isolation | **Circuit Breaker + Bulkhead + Timeout** |
| Database contention spikes | Shared DB | **Database-per-service + event publishing** |
| Cannot trace request flow | Distributed, no tracing | **Distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry)** |
| Authentication repeated | Logic in every service | **API Gateway with central auth** |
| Too many cross-team changes | Wrong boundaries | **Realign with Conway's Law / Team Topologies** |
| High network traffic to one service | Hot service | **Cache (Redis) + Read Replicas + CQRS** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Microservice Architecture — High-Level System View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ClientA["WEB CLIENT"]
    ClientB["MOBILE CLIENT"]
    ClientC["THIRD PARTY API"]
    Gateway["API GATEWAY"]
    Auth["AUTH SERVICE"]
    Order["ORDER SERVICE"]
    Pay["PAYMENT SERVICE"]
    Inv["INVENTORY SERVICE"]
    Ship["SHIPPING SERVICE"]
    Notif["NOTIFICATION SERVICE"]
    DBOrder["ORDER DB"]
    DBPay["PAYMENT DB"]
    DBInv["INVENTORY DB"]
    DBShip["SHIPPING DB"]
    MQ["EVENT BUS KAFKA"]
    Registry["SERVICE REGISTRY CONSUL"]
    Trace["DISTRIBUTED TRACING JAEGER"]
    Monitor["METRICS PROMETHEUS GRAFANA"]

    ClientA --> Gateway
    ClientB --> Gateway
    ClientC --> Gateway
    Gateway --> Auth
    Gateway --> Order
    Gateway --> Pay
    Gateway --> Inv
    Gateway --> Ship
    Order --> MQ
    Pay --> MQ
    Inv --> MQ
    Ship --> MQ
    Notif --> MQ
    MQ --> Notif
    Order --> DBOrder
    Pay --> DBPay
    Inv --> DBInv
    Ship --> DBShip
    Gateway --> Registry
    Order --> Registry
    Pay --> Registry
    Inv --> Registry
    Ship --> Registry
    Order --> Trace
    Pay --> Trace
    Inv --> Trace
    Ship --> Trace
    Trace --> Monitor
```

## 4.2 Internal Hexagonal Architecture of a Single Microservice

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph External["External World"]
        Caller["REST CLIENT OR KAFKA CONSUMER"]
    end

    subgraph Inbound["INBOUND ADAPTERS DRIVING SIDE"]
        REST["REST CONTROLLER"]
        GRPC["GRPC SERVICE STUB"]
        MQList["MESSAGE LISTENER"]
    end

    subgraph Core["BUSINESS LOGIC CORE"]
        UC["USE CASE APPLICATION SERVICE"]
        Domain["DOMAIN ENTITIES AND AGGREGATES"]
        PortOut["OUTBOUND PORT INTERFACE"]
    end

    subgraph Outbound["OUTBOUND ADAPTERS DRIVEN SIDE"]
        Repo["DATABASE REPOSITORY"]
        HTTPOut["HTTP CLIENT TO OTHER SERVICES"]
        MQPub["EVENT PUBLISHER"]
    end

    subgraph Infra["INFRASTRUCTURE"]
        DB["PRIVATE DATABASE"]
        Kafka["KAFKA BROKER"]
        OtherSvc["OTHER MICROSERVICE"]
    end

    Caller --> REST
    Caller --> GRPC
    Caller --> MQList
    REST --> UC
    GRPC --> UC
    MQList --> UC
    UC --> Domain
    UC --> PortOut
    PortOut --> Repo
    PortOut --> HTTPOut
    PortOut --> MQPub
    Repo --> DB
    HTTPOut --> OtherSvc
    MQPub --> Kafka
```

## 4.3 Saga Pattern — Choreographed vs. Orchestrated

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Choreo["CHOREOGRAPHED SAGA EVENT DRIVEN"]
        S1C["T1 ORDER SERVICE PUBLISH ORDERPLACED"]
        S2C["T2 INVENTORY SERVICE LISTENS RESERVES STOCK"]
        S3C["T3 PAYMENT SERVICE LISTENS CHARGES"]
        S4C["T4 SHIPPING SERVICE LISTENS CREATES SHIPMENT"]
        S5C["EVENT BUS KAFKA CARRIES ALL EVENTS"]
        S1C --> S5C
        S5C --> S2C
        S2C --> S5C
        S5C --> S3C
        S3C --> S5C
        S5C --> S4C
    end

    subgraph Orch["ORCHESTRATED SAGA CENTRAL COORDINATOR"]
        Orch1["SAGA ORCHESTRATOR INITIATES"]
        Orch2["INVOKES T1 ORDER SERVICE VIA API"]
        Orch3["INVOKES T2 INVENTORY SERVICE"]
        Orch4["INVOKES T3 PAYMENT SERVICE"]
        Orch5["ON FAILURE CALLS COMPENSATING TRANSACTIONS"]
        Orch1 --> Orch2
        Orch2 --> Orch3
        Orch3 --> Orch4
        Orch4 -.fail.-> Orch5
    end
```

## 4.4 Circuit Breaker State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> OPEN: failure_count_greater_than_threshold
    OPEN --> HALFOPEN: cooldown_timer_expired
    HALFOPEN --> CLOSED: trial_requests_all_succeed
    HALFOPEN --> OPEN: any_trial_request_fails
    note right of CLOSED
        Normal operation
        Requests pass through
        Track failures in window
    end note
    note right of OPEN
        All requests fail fast
        No calls to broken service
        Timer counts cooldown
    end note
    note right of HALFOPEN
        Allow a few trial requests
        Decide based on outcome
    end note
```

## 4.5 Comparison Block Diagram — Monolith vs. Microservices

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Mono["MONOLITHIC APPLICATION"]
        UI1["UI LAYER"]
        BL1["BUSINESS LOGIC"]
        DAL1["DATA ACCESS LAYER"]
        DB1["SHARED SINGLE DATABASE"]
        UI1 --> BL1
        BL1 --> DAL1
        DAL1 --> DB1
    end

    subgraph Micro["MICROSERVICES APPLICATION"]
        UI2["UI LAYER"]
        GW2["API GATEWAY"]
        SvcA2["USER SERVICE"]
        SvcB2["ORDER SERVICE"]
        SvcC2["PRODUCT SERVICE"]
        SvcD2["PAYMENT SERVICE"]
        DBA2["USER DB"]
        DBB2["ORDER DB"]
        DBC2["PRODUCT DB"]
        DBD2["PAYMENT DB"]
        MQ2["KAFKA EVENT BUS"]
        UI2 --> GW2
        GW2 --> SvcA2
        GW2 --> SvcB2
        GW2 --> SvcC2
        GW2 --> SvcD2
        SvcA2 --> DBA2
        SvcB2 --> DBB2
        SvcC2 --> DBC2
        SvcD2 --> DBD2
        SvcA2 <--> MQ2
        SvcB2 <--> MQ2
        SvcC2 <--> MQ2
        SvcD2 <--> MQ2
    end
```
