- This is why stateless APIs are the foundation of cloud-native and serverless architectures.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module 2 — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B questions for a 14-mark module typically have **two sub-parts (a) and (b), each 7 marks**, OR one large question worth 14 marks. The format below mirrors the most common KTU pattern.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the 6 architectural constraints of RESTful APIs as defined by Roy Fielding. Why is each constraint important?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Design the complete RESTful API specification (with endpoints, methods, status codes, sample request/response) for a **"Course Enrollment System"** where students can view courses, enroll in courses, view their enrolled courses, and drop courses. Use proper versioning.

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — The 6 REST Constraints (7 Marks)

The 6 architectural constraints that define a *truly RESTful* API, as per Roy Fielding's PhD dissertation (2000), are:

**1. Client-Server Separation (1 Mark)**
- The user interface (client) and data storage (server) concerns are separated.
- They evolve independently — a mobile UI redesign does not affect the server.
- *Importance:* Improves portability of the client UI across platforms (web, mobile, IoT) and scalability of the server.

**2. Statelessness (1.5 Marks)**
- Each request from client to server must contain *all* information needed to understand and process it.
- The server does not store any client session state between requests.
- *Importance:* Enables horizontal scalability, simplifies server design, improves reliability (any server can serve any request).

**3. Cacheability (1 Mark)**
- Responses must explicitly indicate whether they are cacheable or not using headers like `Cache-Control` and `Expires`.
- *Importance:* Reduces client-server interactions, improves performance, reduces server load, and improves perceived user experience.

**4. Uniform Interface (1.5 Marks)**
- This is the central feature distinguishing REST from other styles. It has 4 sub-constraints:
  - **Resource Identification in Requests** — Each resource is uniquely identified by a URI.
  - **Resource Manipulation through Representations** — Client manipulates resources via representations (e.g., JSON), not directly.
  - **Self-Descriptive Messages** — Each message contains enough info to describe how to process it (e.g., `Content-Type: application/json`).
  - **HATEOAS (Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State)** — Responses include links to related resources, guiding the client.
- *Importance:* Decouples client and server, simplifies evolution, and enables independent evolution of the API.

**5. Layered System (1 Mark)**
- The architecture is composed of hierarchical layers (e.g., client → load balancer → gateway → microservice → database).
- The client cannot tell whether it is connected directly to the end server or an intermediary.
- *Importance:* Enables load balancing, caching, security (WAF), and legacy encapsulation without client awareness.

**6. Code-On-Demand (Optional) (1 Mark)**
- Servers can temporarily extend or customize client functionality by transferring executable code (e.g., JavaScript, applets).
- This is the *only optional* constraint.
- *Importance:* Reduces client-side feature pre-implementation and allows dynamic feature updates.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Course Enrollment API Design (7 Marks)

**Base URL:** `https://api.ktu.edu/v1`

**Resource Hierarchy:**

$$
\text{Students} \rightarrow \text{Enrollments} \rightarrow \text{Courses}
$$

**API Endpoint Specification Table (5 Marks):**

| # | HTTP Method | Endpoint | Purpose | Success Code | Error Codes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `GET` | `/v1/courses` | List all available courses | `200 OK` | `500` |
| 2 | `GET` | `/v1/courses/{courseId}` | Get details of one course | `200 OK` | `404`, `500` |
| 3 | `POST` | `/v1/students/{studentId}/enrollments` | Enroll student in a course | `201 Created` | `400`, `401`, `404`, `409` |
| 4 | `GET` | `/v1/students/{studentId}/enrollments` | List student's enrolled courses | `200 OK` | `401`, `404` |
| 5 | `GET` | `/v1/students/{studentId}/enrollments/{enrollmentId}` | Get one enrollment | `200 OK` | `404` |
| 6 | `DELETE` | `/v1/students/{studentId}/enrollments/{enrollmentId}` | Drop a course | `204 No Content` | `401`, `404` |

**Sample Request — Enroll in Course (1 Mark):**

```
POST /v1/students/KTU2021CS042/enrollments HTTP/1.1
Host: api.ktu.edu
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...
Content-Type: application/json
Accept: application/json

{
  "courseId": "CS745",
  "semester": 7
}
```

**Sample Response — Successful Enrollment (1 Mark):**

```
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
Location: /v1/students/KTU2021CS042/enrollments/789
Content-Type: application/json
Date: Mon, 14 Oct 2024 11:45:00 GMT

{
  "enrollmentId": 789,
  "studentId": "KTU2021CS042",
  "courseId": "CS745",
  "semester": 7,
  "enrolledOn": "2024-10-14T11:45:00Z",
  "status": "ACTIVE"
}
```

**Design Justification:**
- Used **path-based versioning** (`/v1/`) to allow future `/v2/` without breaking clients.
- Used **nouns (resources)** in URIs, never verbs.
- Used **nested resources** for hierarchical relationships (`/students/{id}/enrollments`).
- Used appropriate **status codes** (201 for create, 204 for delete, 404 for missing, 409 for duplicate enrollment).
- Used `Authorization: Bearer` header for stateless authentication.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast REST, SOAP, and GraphQL API styles. For each, give a real-world use case and explain why it is suitable.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the various components of an HTTP request and response with a labeled diagram. Describe the function of each of the 5 status code classes (1xx, 2xx, 3xx, 4xx, 5xx) with at least one example per class.

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — REST vs SOAP vs GraphQL (7 Marks)

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Feature | REST | SOAP | GraphQL |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Architectural style | Protocol | Query language + runtime |
| **Data Format** | JSON, XML, YAML, HTML | XML only | JSON only |
| **Transport** | HTTP/HTTPS | HTTP, SMTP, TCP, JMS | HTTP/HTTPS |
| **Contract** | Optional (OpenAPI 3.0) | Mandatory (WSDL) | Schema Definition Language (SDL) |
| **Endpoint Structure** | Multiple endpoints, one per resource | One endpoint, multiple operations | Single endpoint, queries/mutations |
| **Data Fetching** | May over-fetch or under-fetch | Fixed response structure | Client specifies exact fields |
| **Caching** | Native HTTP caching | Difficult | Complex (POST used) |
| **Learning Curve** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Tooling** | Excellent (Postman, Swagger) | Mature (SoapUI) | Good (Apollo, GraphiQL) |
| **Security** | HTTPS + OAuth/JWT | WS-Security, SAML, encryption | HTTPS + custom auth |
| **Performance** | Good | Slower (XML parsing) | Good (no over-fetching) |

**Real-World Use Cases (3 Marks):**

**REST Use Case: Public E-Commerce Product API (1 Mark)**
- Example: **Amazon Product Advertising API**, **Stripe Payment API**.
- *Why suitable:* REST's simplicity, JSON readability, HTTP caching, and massive tool ecosystem (Swagger, Postman) make it ideal for public-facing APIs consumed by thousands of third-party developers. Resources map naturally to products, orders, customers.

**SOAP Use Case: Banking Transaction Processing (1 Mark)**
- Example: **SWIFT financial messaging**, **SAP enterprise integration**.
- *Why suitable:* SOAP offers **WS-Security** (digital signatures, encryption), **ACID transaction guarantees**, and a formal **WSDL contract** that ensures strict type safety — critical for financial systems where data integrity and non-repudiation are non-negotiable.

**GraphQL Use Case: Mobile Social Media App (1 Mark)**
- Example: **GitHub GraphQL API v4**, **Facebook's mobile app backend**.
- *Why suitable:* Mobile devices benefit enormously from fetching *exactly* the data fields they need in a single request. A profile screen and a feed screen can hit the same `/graphql` endpoint with different queries, eliminating over-fetching and reducing mobile data usage by up to 80% compared to REST.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — HTTP Request/Response Components & Status Code Classes (7 Marks)

**HTTP Request Components (2.5 Marks):**

| Component | Location | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Request Line** | First line | Specifies method, URI, HTTP version | `GET /v1/students/42 HTTP/1.1` |
| **Request Headers** | After request line, key:value pairs | Metadata about request, client, auth | `Host: api.ktu.edu`, `Authorization: Bearer <token>` |
| **Blank Line (CRLF)** | Separator | Marks end of headers | `\r\n` |
| **Request Body** | After blank line (optional) | Payload for POST/PUT/PATCH | `{"name": "Anu"}` |

**HTTP Response Components (2.5 Marks):**

| Component | Location | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Status Line** | First line | HTTP version, status code, reason phrase | `HTTP/1.1 200 OK` |
| **Response Headers** | After status line | Metadata about response, caching, cookies | `Content-Type: application/json` |
| **Blank Line (CRLF)** | Separator | Marks end of headers | `\r\n` |
| **Response Body** | After blank line | The actual resource data | JSON/XML payload |

**The 5 Status Code Classes (2 Marks):**

| Class | Range | Category | Meaning | Example Code & Scenario |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1xx** | 100–199 | **Informational** | Request received, continuing process | `100 Continue` — Client should proceed to send body |
| **2xx** | 200–299 | **Success** | Request successfully received, understood, accepted | `200 OK` (successful GET), `201 Created` (after POST), `204 No Content` (after DELETE) |
| **3xx** | 300–399 | **Redirection** | Further action must be taken to complete the request | `301 Moved Permanently` (resource moved to new URI), `304 Not Modified` (use cached version) |
| **4xx** | 400–499 | **Client Error** | Request contains bad syntax or cannot be fulfilled | `400 Bad Request` (malformed JSON), `401 Unauthorized` (no auth), `403 Forbidden` (no permission), `404 Not Found` |
| **5xx** | 500–599 | **Server Error** | Server failed to fulfill an apparently valid request | `500 Internal Server Error` (unhandled exception), `503 Service Unavailable` (server overloaded) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic (Examiners' Observations):**
>
> 1. **❌ Using verbs in REST URIs** — Writing `POST /getStudent/42` instead of `GET /students/42`. This is an instant **2-mark deduction** under "Resource Identification."
>
> 2. **❌ Confusing PUT and PATCH** — PUT is for *full replacement* (send entire resource); PATCH is for *partial update* (send only changed fields). Mixing them up loses **1.5 marks**.
>
> 3. **❌ Wrong status code** — Returning `200 OK` after a successful `POST` (should be `201 Created`). Or returning `404` for a server error (should be `500`). Status codes are worth **1–2 marks** in nearly every API question.
>
> 4. **❌ Forgetting idempotency** — When asked "is GET idempotent?", many students answer "no" or are unsure. **Memorize:** `GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS` are idempotent; `POST, PATCH` are not.
>
> 5. **❌ Confusing authentication (401) and authorization (403)** — `401 Unauthorized` = "you didn't prove who you are"; `403 Forbidden` = "I know who you are, but you can't do this." KTU specifically tests this.
>
> 6. **❌ Listing 5 REST constraints instead of 6** — Students forget the optional "Code-On-Demand" constraint. Examiners expect all **6** in the answer.
>
> 7. **❌ Skipping the URL anatomy** — When asked to "design an API," students forget to break down the URL components (scheme, host, port, path, query) and version the API. This loses **1 mark** in design questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist. Read it 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **API** = Application Programming Interface — a contract for inter-software communication.
- ✅ **REST** = Representational State Transfer — the dominant cloud-native API style.
- ✅ **6 REST Constraints** = Client-Server, Stateless, Cacheable, Uniform Interface, Layered System, Code-On-Demand.
- ✅ **HTTP Methods to know cold:** `GET` (read), `POST` (create), `PUT` (replace), `PATCH` (partial update), `DELETE` (remove).
- ✅ **Idempotent methods:** GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS. **Non-idempotent:** POST, PATCH.
- ✅ **Status Code Class Rule:** $\lfloor \text{Code}/100 \rfloor$ → 1=Info, 2=Success, 3=Redirect, 4=Client Error, 5=Server Error.
- ✅ **Most-used codes:** 200, 201, 204, 400, 401, 403, 404, 409, 500, 503.
- ✅ **401 vs 403:** 401 = not authenticated, 403 = authenticated but not authorized.
- ✅ **REST URIs use nouns, not verbs.** Use plural collection names. Use path-based versioning (`/v1/`).
- ✅ **Statelessness = no server-side session.** Every request carries its own auth (e.g., JWT in `Authorization: Bearer` header).
- ✅ **Anatomy of HTTP request:** Request Line → Headers → Blank Line → Body (optional).
- ✅ **Anatomy of HTTP response:** Status Line → Headers → Blank Line → Body.
- ✅ **API Styles comparison:** REST (public web), SOAP (enterprise/banking), GraphQL (mobile/varied data), gRPC (internal microservices).
- ✅ **API Gateway** is the single entry point for routing, auth, rate limiting, and logging in cloud-native systems.
- ✅ **Caching** uses `Cache-Control` header; **Content Negotiation** uses `Accept` header.
- ✅ **Rate Limiting** returns `429 Too Many Requests` with a `Retry-After` header.
- ✅ **OpenAPI / Swagger** is the standard for documenting REST APIs.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microservice / API Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT745 - CLOUD, MICROSERVICES AND APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Microservice / API Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microservice / API Management** is the discipline of designing, deploying, governing, securing, monitoring, and orchestrating Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) and the corresponding microservices that expose them, ensuring reliable inter-service communication, scalability, and discoverability in a distributed cloud-native ecosystem.

### 1.1 Microservices — Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **microservice** is an architectural style that structures an application as a collection of small, autonomous, loosely coupled services, each:

- **Independently deployable** in its own process.
- **Organized around a single business capability** (Domain-Driven Design — DDD).
- **Owned by a small, cross-functional team**.
- Communicating via **lightweight mechanisms** (HTTP/REST, gRPC, message queues).
- **Polyglot** in nature — each service can use a different language, framework, or datastore.

> [!NOTE]
> **Domain-Driven Design (DDD)** — A software design approach where the system is modeled around the *business domain*, identifying **bounded contexts** that naturally translate into individual microservices.

### 1.2 API Management — Formal Definition

**API Management** encompasses the processes, tools, and policies required to design, publish, secure, monitor, and analyze APIs in a secure, scalable environment. It typically involves four pillars:

1. **API Gateway** — Entry point for all clients.
2. **Developer Portal** — Self-service documentation & onboarding.
3. **API Lifecycle Management** — Design → Publish → Deprecate.
4. **Analytics & Monitoring** — Traffic, latency, error rates.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Hall" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a city (your application):**
> - **Microservices** = Independent shops (a bakery, a pharmacy, a post office) — each handles one specific job.
> - **API** = The **doorway** of each shop, with a clear menu (endpoints) and rules of engagement (protocols).
> - **API Gateway** = The **City Hall** — a single, central reception desk that directs citizens (client requests) to the right shop.
> - **API Management Platform** = The **Municipal Authority** — issues permits (authentication), tracks footfall (monitoring), and enforces zoning laws (rate limits, throttling).

When citizens arrive, they do **not** run around the city finding the right shop. They go to **City Hall**, declare their need, and City Hall connects them to the right service. This is exactly how an API Gateway functions in a microservice architecture.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric / Constant | Standard Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **HTTP Status `200`** | Success | Request OK |
| **HTTP Status `4xx`** | Client Error | Bad request, unauthorized |
| **HTTP Status `5xx`** | Server Error | Service failure |
| **SLA Uptime** | **99.99%** (Four 9s) | Allowed downtime ≈ 52.6 min/year |
| **JWT Token Expiry** | **15 – 60 minutes** | Access token lifetime |
| **OAuth 2.0 Refresh Token** | **30 – 90 days** | Long-lived credential |
| **P99 Latency** | < 200 ms | 99th percentile response time |
| **Rate Limit (default)** | **100 req/min/user** | Throttle threshold |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Remember the **Four 9s SLA** ($99.99\%$) and its annual downtime — this is a frequently asked numerical question in the ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Core Components of API Management (Operational Breakdown)

- **API Gateway**:
  - **Why**: A single entry point hides internal service topology, enabling **service decoupling**.
  - **How**: Performs request routing, protocol translation (REST ↔ gRPC), authentication, rate limiting, and response aggregation.
  - **Examples**: Kong, AWS API Gateway, Apigee, NGINX, Spring Cloud Gateway, Zuul.

- **Service Discovery**:
  - **Why**: In a dynamic cloud environment, service instances are ephemeral (IP/port changes constantly).
  - **How**: A service registry (e.g., Eureka, Consul, etcd, Zookeeper) maintains a real-time catalog of available instances.
  - **Two Patterns**:
    1. **Client-Side Discovery** — Client queries the registry and chooses an instance.
    2. **Server-Side Discovery** — A load balancer (e.g., AWS ELB) queries the registry.

- **Load Balancing**:
  - **Why**: Distributes traffic to prevent any single instance from becoming a bottleneck.
  - **How**: Algorithms like **Round Robin, Least Connections, Weighted Response Time, IP Hash**.

- **Circuit Breaker Pattern**:
  - **Why**: Prevents cascading failures. If a downstream service is down, the circuit "opens" and returns a fallback instead of hanging.
  - **How**: States — **CLOSED → OPEN → HALF_OPEN**.
  - **Examples**: Hystrix (deprecated), Resilience4j, Istio.

- **Centralized Configuration**:
  - **Why**: Avoid hardcoding environment-specific values in each microservice.
  - **How**: Use **Spring Cloud Config Server, HashiCorp Consul KV, AWS Parameter Store**.

- **API Versioning**:
  - **Why**: APIs evolve; clients need stable contracts.
  - **How**: URI versioning (`/api/v1/users`), header versioning (`Accept: application/vnd.company.v2+json`).

- **Security (OAuth 2.0 + JWT)**:
  - **Why**: Stateless authentication and delegated authorization.
  - **How**: Resource Server validates signed **JWT (JSON Web Token)** for every request.

- **Rate Limiting & Throttling**:
  - **Why**: Protect services from abuse, DoS, and over-subscription.
  - **How**: Algorithms — **Token Bucket, Leaky Bucket, Fixed Window, Sliding Window**.

- **Observability** (The Three Pillars):
  - **Metrics** — Prometheus + Grafana
  - **Logs** — ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana)
  - **Traces** — Jaeger, Zipkin (Distributed Tracing)

### 2.2 Communication Patterns Between Microservices

| Pattern | Type | Use Case | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **REST (HTTP/JSON)** | Synchronous | CRUD operations, public APIs | Higher latency, blocking |
| **gRPC** | Synchronous | Internal high-performance comms | Protobuf required |
| **Message Queue (Kafka, RabbitMQ)** | Asynchronous | Event-driven, decoupling | Eventual consistency |
| **GraphQL** | Query-based | Aggregating data for mobile/web | Caching complexity |
| **WebSocket** | Bi-directional | Real-time chat, stock ticks | Stateful connection |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **SLA Downtime** | $\text{Downtime} = (1 - \text{SLA}) \times T$ | $T$ = time period (minutes/year) |
| 2 | **Availability (Series)** | $A_{\text{system}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | $A_i$ = availability of component $i$ |
| 3 | **Availability (Parallel)** | $A_{\text{sys}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - A_i)$ | Two components in parallel |
| 4 | **MTBF** | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Mean Time Between Failures |
| 5 | **MTTR** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Mean Time To Repair (hours) |
| 6 | **Throughput (Little's Law)** | $L = \lambda \times W$ | $L$ = requests in system, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $W$ = wait time |
| 7 | **Token Bucket Rate** | $r(t) = \min(C,\; \text{tokens}_{t-1} + B \cdot \Delta t)$ | $C$ = bucket size, $B$ = refill rate |
| 8 | **Latency Percentile** | $P_{99} = \text{99th percentile response time}$ | ms |
| 9 | **JWT Structure** | $\text{Header} . \text{Payload} . \text{Signature}$ | Base64URL encoded |
| 10 | **API Response Time** | $T_{\text{end-to-end}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ | Sum of all service hops |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LATEX RULE:** In markdown tables, never use the pipe symbol `|`. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead to avoid breaking table syntax. For example, write $A_{\text{system}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ (which uses `\prod`, not `|`).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Netflix** uses **Eureka** for service discovery, **Hystrix** for circuit breaking, and **Zuul** as the API gateway — handling billions of API calls per day.
- **Uber** moved from a monolithic to microservice architecture to support 1,000+ services across 2,000+ engineers.
- **Amazon** adopted the "two-pizza team" rule — each microservice owned by a team small enough to be fed by two pizzas.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Annual Downtime from SLA

**Problem:** A cloud provider guarantees **99.99%** availability. Calculate the maximum allowable downtime per year.

**Step 1 — State the total time in a year:**

$$
T_{\text{year}} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 \;\text{minutes} = 525{,}600 \;\text{minutes}
$$

**Step 2 — Express SLA as a fraction:**

$$
\text{SLA} = 99.99\% = 0.9999
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the unavailability (downtime fraction):**

$$
D_{\text{fraction}} = 1 - 0.9999 = 0.0001
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by total time:**

$$
D_{\text{year}} = D_{\text{fraction}} \times T_{\text{year}} = 0.0001 \times 525{,}600 = 52.56 \;\text{minutes/year}
$$

**Result:** The system can be down for a maximum of **52.56 minutes per year**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Explanation:** Each extra "9" in SLA reduces downtime by an order of magnitude:
> - 99% → 3.65 days/year
> - 99.9% → 8.77 hours/year
> - 99.99% → 52.6 minutes/year
> - 99.999% → 5.26 minutes/year

---

### 3.2 Derivation: System Availability (Series and Parallel)

**Scenario 1 — Services in Series (Call Chain)**

If a request passes through Service A → Service B → Service C, all three must be up for the request to succeed.

**Step 1 — Define individual availabilities:**

$$
A_A = 0.999,\quad A_B = 0.995,\quad A_C = 0.99
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the multiplicative rule for series:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{system}} &= A_A \times A_B \times A_C \\
&= 0.999 \times 0.995 \times 0.99 \\
&= 0.98405 \;\approx\; 98.41\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret:** Even if each service has high availability, the **compounded** availability drops significantly in a microservice chain. This is the **microservice availability penalty**.

**Scenario 2 — Services in Parallel (Redundancy)**

If Service A is replicated across 2 instances with availability $0.99$ each:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{system}} &= 1 - (1 - A_1) \times (1 - A_2) \\
&= 1 - (0.01) \times (0.01) \\
&= 1 - 0.0001 \\
&= 0.9999 \;=\; 99.99\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Insight:** Parallel redundancy **dramatically increases** availability.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Little's Law for Capacity Planning

**Problem:** A microservice processes requests at an average rate of $\lambda = 50$ requests/second. The average time a request spends in the system is $W = 0.4$ seconds. How many requests are in the system at any time?

**Step 1 — Recall Little's Law:**

$$
L = \lambda \times W
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 50 \times 0.4 \\
&= 20 \;\text{requests}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation:**

To prevent queue overflow, the system must have capacity for at least **20 concurrent in-flight requests**. This is critical for sizing thread pools, connection pools, and Kubernetes pod autoscaling thresholds.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What Pattern

| Scenario | Recommended Pattern | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Public-facing REST API for mobile app | **API Gateway + REST** | Simplicity, cacheability, tooling |
| High-throughput internal service-to-service | **gRPC** | Protobuf, HTTP/2 multiplexing, low overhead |
| Order placement → email notification, invoice | **Message Queue (Kafka)** | Asynchronous, decoupled, retry-safe |
| Aggregating user dashboard from 5 services | **API Gateway (BFF)** or **GraphQL** | Reduces chattiness, mobile-friendly |
| Real-time stock ticker | **WebSocket** | Server push, low latency |
| Payment processing with retries | **Circuit Breaker + Saga** | Failure isolation, compensation |
| Configuration propagation across 100 services | **Centralized Config Server** | Avoid redeploy, dynamic refresh |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Circuit Breaker State Machine

The **Circuit Breaker** has three states governed by a **failure threshold** $\theta$ and a **reset timeout** $\tau$.

$$
\text{State}_{t+1} =
\begin{cases}
\text{OPEN} & \text{if } f_t \geq \theta \\
\text{HALF\_OPEN} & \text{if } \text{State}_t = \text{OPEN} \;\text{and}\; t - t_{\text{opened}} \geq \tau \\
\text{CLOSED} & \text{if } \text{State}_t = \text{HALF\_OPEN} \;\text{and}\; \text{probe success} \\
\text{OPEN} & \text{if } \text{State}_t = \text{HALF\_OPEN} \;\text{and}\; \text{probe failure}
\end{cases}
$$

Where $f_t$ is the failure count in window $t$ and $t_{\text{opened}}$ is the timestamp when the circuit was opened.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Students often forget the **HALF_OPEN** state. Remember: after timeout, the breaker sends **one trial request** — success closes it, failure re-opens it.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Microservice Architecture with API Gateway

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client1[Mobile App Client]
    client2[Web Browser Client]
    client3[Third Party Partner]
    gw[API Gateway Layer]
    auth[Auth Service OAuth2 JWT]
    reg[Service Registry Eureka]
    cfg[Centralized Config Server]
    s1[User Microservice]
    s2[Order Microservice]
    s3[Payment Microservice]
    s4[Inventory Microservice]
    db1[(User DB)]
    db2[(Order DB)]
    db3[(Payment DB)]
    db4[(Inventory DB)]
    kfk[Kafka Message Broker]
    cb[Circuit Breaker Resilience4j]
    mon[Monitoring Prometheus Grafana]
    log[Centralized Logging ELK]
    trace[Distributed Tracing Jaeger]

    client1 --> gw
    client2 --> gw
    client3 --> gw
    gw --> auth
    gw --> s1
    gw --> s2
    gw --> s3
    gw --> s4
    gw --> cb
    s1 --> reg
    s2 --> reg
    s3 --> reg
    s4 --> reg
    cfg --> s1
    cfg --> s2
    cfg --> s3
    cfg --> s4
    s1 --> db1
    s2 --> db2
    s3 --> db3
    s4 --> db4
    s2 --> kfk
    s3 --> kfk
    s1 --> mon
    s2 --> mon
    s3 --> mon
    s4 --> mon
    s1 --> log
    s2 --> log
    s3 --> log
    s4 --> log
    s1 --> trace
    s2 --> trace
    s3 --> trace
    s4 --> trace
```

---

### 4.2 Circuit Breaker State Machine Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> OPEN : Failure threshold exceeded
    OPEN --> HALF_OPEN : Reset timeout elapsed
    HALF_OPEN --> CLOSED : Trial request succeeds
    HALF_OPEN --> OPEN : Trial request fails
    CLOSED --> CLOSED : Request succeeds
```

---

### 4.3 API Request Flow — End-to-End Journey

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant GW as API Gateway
    participant AUTH as Auth Service
    participant RL as Rate Limiter
    participant SVC as Target Microservice
    participant DB as Database
    participant MON as Monitoring

    C->>GW: 1 Send HTTP Request
    GW->>AUTH: 2 Validate JWT Token
    AUTH-->>GW: 3 Token Valid
    GW->>RL: 4 Check Rate Limit
    RL-->>GW: 5 Within Quota
    GW->>SVC: 6 Forward Request
    SVC->>DB: 7 Query Data
    DB-->>SVC: 8 Return Data
    SVC-->>GW: 9 Service Response
    GW->>MON: 10 Emit Metrics Logs Traces
    GW-->>C: 11 Return Final Response
```

---

### 4.4 OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant W as Web Client
    participant AS as Authorization Server
    participant RS as Resource Server API

    U->>W: 1 Login Request
    W->>AS: 2 Authorize Redirect Client ID
    AS-->>U: 3 Show Login Page
    U->>AS: 4 Submit Credentials
    AS-->>W: 5 Authorization Code
    W->>AS: 6 Exchange Code for Access Token
    AS-->>W: 7 JWT Access Token
    W->>RS: 8 API Call with Bearer Token
    RS->>RS: 9 Validate JWT Signature
    RS-->>W: 10 Protected Resource
```

---

### 4.5 Service Discovery Pattern (Client-Side)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    c[Client Service] --> r{Query Service Registry}
    r --> reg[Eureka Consul etcd]
    reg --> r
    r --> s1[Service Instance 1]
    r --> s2[Service Instance 2]
    r --> s3[Service Instance 3]
    c --> lb[Load Balancer Ribbon]
    lb --> s1
    lb --> s2
    lb --> s3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q1. Define API Gateway. List any four of its responsibilities.**

**Model Answer:**

An **API Gateway** is a single entry point that sits between external clients and a collection of internal microservices. It abstracts the internal architecture and provides cross-cutting concerns.

**Four responsibilities:**
1. **Request Routing** — Directs incoming requests to the appropriate microservice based on the URL path, headers, or query parameters.
2. **Authentication & Authorization** — Validates JWT/OAuth tokens before forwarding the request.
3. **Rate Limiting & Throttling** — Enforces per-client request quotas to protect downstream services.
4. **Response Aggregation** — Combines multiple microservice responses into a single payload (BFF pattern).
5. **Protocol Translation** — Converts REST to gRPC or vice versa.
6. **SSL Termination** — Decrypts HTTPS traffic at the gateway level.

> *(Any four well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q2. What is a Circuit Breaker pattern? Explain its three states.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Circuit Breaker pattern** is a resilience design pattern that prevents an application from repeatedly trying an operation that is likely to fail, allowing it to recover and avoid cascading failures in a distributed system.

**Three States:**

1. **CLOSED (Normal Operation):**
   - All requests pass through to the downstream service.
   - Failures are counted within a sliding time window.
   - If failure count exceeds a configured threshold, the circuit transitions to OPEN.

2. **OPEN (Failure Detected):**
   - Requests are **immediately rejected** without calling the downstream service.
   - A fallback response (e.g., cached data, default value, error message) is returned.
   - After a **reset timeout** (e.g., 30 seconds), the circuit transitions to HALF_OPEN.

3. **HALF_OPEN (Probing):**
   - A **limited number of trial requests** are allowed through.
   - If they succeed → circuit transitions back to **CLOSED**.
   - If they fail → circuit returns to **OPEN** and the timer restarts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Real-world libraries like **Resilience4j** (Java), **Polly** (.NET), and **Hystrix** (legacy Netflix) implement this pattern.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Microservices Architecture** in detail. Compare it with **Monolithic Architecture** using a comparative table. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Microservices Architecture** is a software design approach where a large application is decomposed into small, independent, loosely coupled services, each responsible for a single business capability and communicating over a network through lightweight protocols.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Single Responsibility** — One service = one bounded context.
- **Independent Deployment** — Each service has its own CI/CD pipeline.
- **Decentralized Data** — Each service owns its own database (no shared schema).
- **Polyglot** — Services can use different languages/frameworks.
- **Resilience** — Failure of one service does not crash the entire system.
- **Scalability** — Individual services can be scaled horizontally based on load.

**Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | Monolithic Architecture | Microservices Architecture |
|---|---|---|
| **Codebase** | Single, unified codebase | Multiple, independent codebases |
| **Deployment** | Single deployable unit (WAR/EAR) | Many deployable units (containers) |
| **Scaling** | Scale the entire application | Scale individual services |
| **Technology Stack** | Single language/framework | Polyglot (mixed stack) |
| **Failure Impact** | One bug can crash the whole system | Failures are isolated |
| **Development Speed** | Slows down as codebase grows | Parallel team development possible |
| **Data Management** | Shared relational database | Database per service |
| **Communication** | In-process function calls | HTTP/REST, gRPC, Message Queues |
| **Testing** | Easier end-to-end | Complex (contract testing needed) |
| **Suitability** | Small teams, simple apps | Large teams, complex domains |

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the role of **Service Discovery** and **Load Balancing** in a microservices ecosystem. Mention any two service discovery tools. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Service Discovery:**

In a microservices architecture, instances are dynamically created and destroyed (especially in containerized environments like Kubernetes). Their IP addresses and ports are **ephemeral** and cannot be hardcoded. **Service Discovery** solves this by:

1. **Registration** — Each service instance registers itself with a central **service registry** at startup, providing its network location and metadata (host, port, health status).
2. **Discovery (Lookup)** — Clients or routers query the registry to find healthy instances of a target service.
3. **Health Checking** — The registry periodically pings instances and removes unhealthy ones from the catalog.
4. **De-registration** — On graceful shutdown, instances deregister themselves.

**Two Patterns of Service Discovery:**

1. **Client-Side Discovery** — The client queries the registry, gets a list of instances, and uses a load-balancing algorithm (e.g., Ribbon) to pick one.
2. **Server-Side Discovery** — A load balancer (e.g., AWS ELB, NGINX) queries the registry on behalf of the client and routes traffic.

**Two Service Discovery Tools:**

1. **Netflix Eureka** — A REST-based service registry widely used in Spring Cloud. Supports client-side discovery and is AWS-region aware.
2. **HashiCorp Consul** — Provides service discovery, health checking, KV store, and multi-datacenter support. Uses gossip protocol for cluster membership.

*(Other valid tools: etcd, Zookeeper, Kubernetes DNS)*

**Role of Load Balancing:**

Load balancing distributes incoming client requests across multiple service instances to:
- **Maximize throughput** and minimize response time.
- **Prevent overloading** a single instance.
- **Provide fault tolerance** by removing failed instances from rotation.

**Common Algorithms:**
- **Round Robin** — Distributes sequentially.
- **Least Connections** — Routes to the instance with fewest active connections.
- **Weighted Response Time** — Favors faster instances.
- **IP Hash** — Uses client IP for sticky sessions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **client-side** and **server-side** discovery. Remember: in **client-side**, the client does the work; in **server-side**, a separate load balancer handles it. Writing both with the same example loses marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of an **API Gateway pattern**. List its advantages and disadvantages. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**API Gateway Pattern:**

The API Gateway is a server that acts as the **single entry point** for all client requests in a microservices architecture. It encapsulates the internal system architecture and provides API services tailored to each client (web, mobile, third-party).

**Working (Refer Diagram in Section 4.1):**

1. A **client** (mobile/web/partner) sends a request to the API Gateway.
2. The gateway performs **cross-cutting concerns**:
   - Authentication (OAuth 2.0 / JWT validation)
   - Rate limiting & throttling
   - SSL termination
   - Request logging
3. The gateway **routes** the request to the appropriate microservice using path-based, header-based, or host-based routing.
4. The microservice processes the request and returns a response.
5. The gateway may **aggregate** responses from multiple services.
6. The gateway **returns** the final response to the client and emits metrics/traces to monitoring tools.

**Advantages:**

1. **Decoupling** — Clients are insulated from internal service changes.
2. **Centralized Concerns** — Auth, logging, rate limiting handled once.
3. **Protocol Translation** — REST to gRPC, HTTP to WebSocket.
4. **Reduced Chattiness** — Aggregates multiple calls into one.
5. **Security** — Single point to enforce TLS, WAF rules, DDoS protection.
6. **Observability** — Unified metrics and tracing.

**Disadvantages:**

1. **Single Point of Failure** — If the gateway fails, the entire system is inaccessible. *(Mitigation: deploy multiple gateway instances behind a load balancer.)*
2. **Performance Bottleneck** — All traffic flows through it; can become a latency hotspot.
3. **Increased Complexity** — Additional component to deploy, monitor, and scale.
4. **Development Coupling** — Gateway updates require coordination with all teams.

---

**Q. (b)** What is **OAuth 2.0**? Explain the **Authorization Code Grant** flow with a sequence diagram. How does **JWT** differ from an opaque token? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**OAuth 2.0:**

OAuth 2.0 is an **authorization framework** (RFC 6749) that enables a third-party application to obtain **limited access** to a protected resource on behalf of a user, without exposing the user's credentials. It uses **access tokens** issued by an **Authorization Server**.

**Roles in OAuth 2.0:**
- **Resource Owner** — The user.
- **Client** — The application requesting access.
- **Authorization Server** — Issues tokens (e.g., Okta, Keycloak).
- **Resource Server** — The API hosting the protected data.

**Authorization Code Grant Flow (Refer Sequence Diagram in Section 4.4):**

1. **User Login** — User clicks "Login" on the client application.
2. **Authorization Request** — Client redirects the user to the Authorization Server with `client_id`, `redirect_uri`, `response_type=code`, and `scope`.
3. **User Authentication** — User submits credentials and grants permission.
4. **Authorization Code** — Authorization Server redirects back to the client with a short-lived **authorization code**.
5. **Token Exchange** — Client sends the code + `client_secret` to the token endpoint.
6. **Access Token** — Authorization Server returns a **JWT access token** and an optional **refresh token**.
7. **API Call** — Client calls the Resource Server with `Authorization: Bearer <JWT>`.
8. **Resource Access** — Resource Server validates the JWT signature and returns protected data.

**JWT vs Opaque Token:**

| Parameter | JWT (JSON Web Token) | Opaque Token |
|---|---|---|
| **Format** | Self-contained JSON (Header.Payload.Signature) | Random string, meaningless to client |
| **Validation** | Stateless — resource server verifies signature locally | Requires **introspection call** to Auth Server |
| **Performance** | Faster (no extra round-trip) | Slower (extra network call) |
| **Revocation** | Harder (requires blocklist or short expiry) | Easier (delete from DB) |
| **Size** | Larger (~200–800 bytes) | Smaller (~32–64 bytes) |
| **Use Case** | Distributed, stateless APIs | Centralized, revocable sessions |
| **Security** | Vulnerable if signing key leaks | Safe to leak (unusable without DB) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "OAuth 2.0 is an authentication protocol." This is **wrong** — it is an **authorization** framework. Authentication happens *within* the flow, but the protocol's purpose is delegated **authorization**. Losing 1 mark for this mix-up is common.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Remember for KTU ESE**

- ✅ **Microservice** = Single-responsibility, independently deployable, loosely coupled service.
- ✅ **API Gateway** = Single entry point handling routing, auth, rate limiting, aggregation.
- ✅ **Service Discovery** = Dynamic registration & lookup of service instances (Eureka, Consul).
- ✅ **Circuit Breaker** = Prevents cascading failures (CLOSED → OPEN → HALF_OPEN).
- ✅ **OAuth 2.0** = Authorization framework, NOT authentication.
- ✅ **JWT** = Stateless, self-contained token (Header.Payload.Signature, Base64URL).
- ✅ **Opaque Token** = Requires introspection; easier to revoke.
- ✅ **API Versioning** = URI-based (`/v1/`) or header-based (`Accept: vnd.api.v2+json`).
- ✅ **SLA Formula** = $\text{Downtime} = (1 - \text{SLA}) \times T_{\text{period}}$.
- ✅ **99.99% SLA** = 52.56 minutes/year downtime.
- ✅ **Little's Law** = $L = \lambda \times W$ (used for capacity planning).
- ✅ **Series Availability** = Product of individual availabilities (compounds downward).
- ✅ **Parallel Availability** = $1 - \prod (1 - A_i)$ (redundancy boosts availability).
- ✅ **REST** = Synchronous, HTTP/JSON, public APIs.
- ✅ **gRPC** = Synchronous, Protobuf, internal high-performance.
- ✅ **Kafka / RabbitMQ** = Asynchronous, event-driven, decoupled.
- ✅ **Three Pillars of Observability** = Metrics + Logs + Traces.
- ✅ **BFF (Backend for Frontend)** = Per-client gateway for mobile vs web.
- ✅ **Saga Pattern** = Manages distributed transactions via compensation.
- ✅ **Service Mesh (Istio, Linkerd)** = Offloads service-to-service concerns (mTLS, retries, telemetry) to a sidecar proxy.

> **End of Notes — Microservice / API Management (Module 2)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spring Boot Fundamentals and Design of Microservice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT745 - CLOUD, MICROSERVICES AND APPLICATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Spring Boot Fundamentals and Design of Microservice ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Spring Boot — Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!NOTE] > **Spring Boot** is an opinionated, convention-over-configuration extension of the Spring Framework that enables the rapid development of production-grade, stand-alone, executable Spring applications. It achieves this through three pillars: **auto-configuration**, **starter dependencies**, and **embedded servers**. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECBT745)**, Spring Boot is the de-facto standard framework for implementing Java-based **Microservices**. It eliminates boilerplate configuration through intelligent defaults, allowing developers to focus on business logic rather than infrastructure plumbing. ### 1.2 Microservice — Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Microservice Architecture** is a software architectural style that structures an application as a suite of small, independently deployable, loosely coupled services, each running in its own process and communicating through lightweight mechanisms (typically HTTP/REST or messaging queues). Each service is organized around a specific **business capability**, is **independently deployable**, and may be written in different programming languages and use different data storage technologies (Polyglot Persistence). ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are running a **large restaurant chain** (Monolithic Architecture). Everything happens in **one massive kitchen**: - One power outage → entire kitchen shuts down. - Adding a new dish → must retrain the whole team. - Scaling for a busy festival → you must build a *bigger* kitchen (vertical scaling). Now, **Spring Boot Microservices** is like converting that single kitchen into a **food truck fleet** (each truck is a microservice): - 🍔 The **Burger Truck** handles only burger orders. - 🍕 The **Pizza Truck** handles only pizza. - 🥗 The **Salad Truck** handles only salads. - 🚚 Each truck has its **own engine** (JVM/Process), **own refrigerator** (Database), and **own menu** (API contract). - ☎️ Trucks communicate via **walkie-talkies** (REST/gRPC) or **delivery orders** (Message Queues like Kafka/RabbitMQ). - ⚡ If the Pizza Truck breaks down, Burgers and Salads still sell! **Spring Boot** is the *ready-made truck chassis* — pre-assembled, with engine, GPS, and fuel system pre-wired. You just attach the body (business logic) and start driving. ### 1.4 Key Spring Boot Components (KTU 2024 High-Yield Glossary) | Term | Definition | KTU High-Yield Mark | |---|---|---| | **Auto-Configuration** | Mechanism that automatically configures Spring beans based on classpath JARs | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Starter Dependency** | Pre-packaged dependency descriptor (e.g., `spring-boot-starter-web`) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Embedded Server** | Servlet container bundled inside the JAR (Tomcat/Jetty/Undertow) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **SpringApplication** | Bootstrap class that launches the application context | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Actuator** | Production-ready module for health checks, metrics, and monitoring | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Spring Initializr** | Web-based project generator at `start.spring.io` | ⭐⭐⭐ | > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Metric:** A typical Spring Boot "fat JAR" is **15–50 MB** in size. The default embedded server is **Apache Tomcat 10.x** on port **8080**. Spring Boot applications start in **2–5 seconds** on modern hardware. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Spring Boot Architecture — The 3-Layer Intelligence Model Spring Boot operates on three intelligent layers that work in unison: #### Layer 1: Starter Dependencies (What do I need?) - Defined in `pom.xml` (Maven) or `build.gradle` (Gradle). - They are **NOT** libraries themselves — they are **meta-packages** that aggregate a curated set of transitive dependencies. $$Starter = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Library_i + Version_i + TransitiveDependencies_i)$$ **Example:** `spring-boot-starter-web` pulls in: - `spring-web` - `spring-webmvc` - `jackson-databind` (JSON) - `hibernate-validator` (Bean validation) - `tomcat-embed-core` (Embedded server) #### Layer 2: Auto-Configuration (How do I configure it?) - Triggered by `@SpringBootApplication` annotation. - Spring Boot scans the classpath and applies configuration only when matching classes are detected. - Uses the `@Conditional` API internally. > [!IMPORTANT] > The annotation `@SpringBootApplication` is a composite of: > 1. `@SpringBootConfiguration` — Marks the class as configuration source. > 2. `@EnableAutoConfiguration` — Enables the auto-configuration magic. > 3. `@ComponentScan` — Scans the current package for `@Component`, `@Service`, `@Repository`, `@Controller`. #### Layer 3: Embedded Server (How do I deploy it?) - Tomcat, Jetty, or Undertow is bundled inside the executable JAR. - The application is launched via `java -jar app.jar`. - No external **WAR deployment** to a separate server is required. ### 2.2 The 12-Factor App Principles Applied to Microservices (KTU Syllabus Mandate) | # | Factor | Microservice Application | |---|---|---| | I | **Codebase** | One codebase = one microservice, tracked in one Git repo | | II | **Dependencies** | Explicitly declared in `pom.xml` | | III | **Config** | Externalized via `application.yml` / Environment variables | | IV | **Backing Services** | DB, MQ, cache are attached resources, not part of code | | V | **Build, Release, Run** | Strict separation via CI/CD pipelines | | VI | **Processes** | Stateless processes, horizontal scaling | | VII | **Port Binding** | Self-contained embedded server on dynamic port | | VIII | **Concurrency** | Scale out via process replication | | IX | **Disposability** | Fast startup (Spring Boot's 2-second boot) and graceful shutdown | | X | **Dev/Prod Parity** | Same backing services in dev, staging, production | | XI | **Logs** | Treated as event streams to STDOUT, collected by ELK/Prometheus | | XII | **Admin Processes** | One-off tasks run as separate processes | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Metric | |---|---|---| | **Spring Boot Startup Time** | $T_{boot} \approx T_{classload} + T_{autoconfig} + T_{contextRefresh}$ | seconds | | **Microservice Coupling Score** | $C = \frac{\text{Number of inter-service calls}}{\text{Number of services}}$ | dimensionless | | **Scalability Metric** | $S = \frac{Throughput_{scaled}}{Throughput_{baseline}}$ | dimensionless ratio | | **Fault Tolerance** | $MTTF = \frac{Total\ Uptime}{Number\ of\ Failures}$ | hours | | **Fat JAR Size** | $Size_{jar} = \sum JAR_i + Embedded\ Server$ | MB (15–50 MB typical) | | **Service Decomposition** | $N_{services} = f(Bounded\ Contexts)$ | integer count | | **Resource Pool (HikariCP)** | $Pool_{max} = \frac{(Core\_Count \times 2) + Effective\_Spindle\_Count}{1.0}$ | connections | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Condition:** A microservice should NEVER exceed the **"Two-Pizza Rule"** (Amazon's heuristic): the entire team owning the service should be feedable with two pizzas. This typically implies **6–10 developers per service**. ### 2.4 Spring Boot Annotations — The KTU Must-Know List | Annotation | Purpose | Layer | |---|---|---| | `@SpringBootApplication` | Main entry point marker | Bootstrap | | `@RestController` | Marks class as REST API returning JSON | Web | | `@RequestMapping("/path")` | Maps HTTP requests to methods | Web | | `@GetMapping`, `@PostMapping` | HTTP verb-specific mappings | Web | | `@Autowired` | Dependency injection | DI | | `@Service` | Business logic component | DI | | `@Repository` | Data access component | DI | | `@Configuration` | Java-based configuration class | Config | | `@Bean` | Declares a Spring-managed bean | Config | | `@Value("${prop}")` | Injects property values | Config | | `@Profile("dev")` | Environment-specific bean | Config | | `@EnableDiscoveryClient` | Registers with Eureka/Consul | Microservice | | `@EnableCircuitBreaker` | Enables Hystrix/Resilience4j | Microservice | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Monolith to Microservice Decomposition (Strangler Fig Pattern) The **Strangler Fig Pattern** (Martin Fowler, 2004) is the canonical KTU-prescribed method to incrementally migrate a monolith to microservices. **Given:** - A monolithic application $M$ serving $N$ business functions. - We want to extract function $F_k$ into a separate microservice. **Step 1:** Identify the bounded context. $$B_k = \{F_k \mid F_k \in M, F_k\ has\ coherent\ business\ responsibility\}$$ **Step 2:** Create a façade (API Gateway) in front of the monolith. $$Request_{in} \rightarrow Gateway(G) \rightarrow \{ M\ or\ S_k \}$$ **Step 3:** Route requests for $F_k$ to the new microservice $S_k$ while all other requests continue to the monolith. $$\text{For each request } R: \quad G(R) = \begin{cases} S_k(R) & \text{if } R \mapsto F_k \\ M(R) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Step 4:** Verify functional parity via parallel run. $$\text{Parity} = \frac{\text{Matching Responses}}{\text{Total Requests}} \times 100\%$$ **Step 5:** Decommission the legacy module inside $M$ once parity reaches 100%. **Result:** The monolith shrinks over time, eventually being "strangled" by the new microservices. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Monolith vs Microservice | Attribute | Monolith | Microservice (Spring Boot) | |---|---|---| | **Deployment Unit** | Single WAR/JAR | Multiple independent JARs | | **Tech Stack** | Single language | Polyglot (Java, Python, Go) | | **Database** | Shared single DB | Database-per-service | | **Failure Impact** | Whole app down | Single service down | | **Scaling** | Vertical (bigger server) | Horizontal (more instances) | | **Startup Time** | 30–120 sec | 2–5 sec | | **Team Size** | Large team on one codebase | Small teams per service | | **Communication** | In-memory calls | HTTP/REST/gRPC + Message Queues | | **Testing** | Unit + Integration | + Contract + End-to-End | ### 3.3 Derivation: Auto-Configuration Decision Logic **Given:** A Spring Boot application with classpath `C` and the annotation `@EnableAutoConfiguration`. **Step 1:** Spring Boot loads `META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports` from each starter JAR. **Step 2:** For each auto-configuration class $A_i$, evaluate its `@Conditional` predicates. $$A_i\ applies = \bigwedge_{j=1}^{m} C_{ij}$$ Where each condition is one of: - `@ConditionalOnClass` — Class $X$ is on classpath - `@ConditionalOnBean` — Bean $X$ is already defined - `@ConditionalOnMissingBean` — Bean $X$ is NOT yet defined (allows user override) - `@ConditionalOnProperty` — Property $prop=value` is set - `@ConditionalOnWebApplication` — Application is web-enabled **Step 3:** Only auto-configurations whose predicates ALL evaluate to TRUE are applied. $$\text{Active Configs} = \{A_i \mid \forall j: C_{ij} = TRUE\}$$ **Step 4:** User-defined beans always take precedence over auto-configured beans (thanks to `@ConditionalOnMissingBean`). ### 3.4 Worked Example: Spring Boot REST Controller (KTU 14-Mark Pattern) ```java // File: ProductController.java @RestController @RequestMapping("/api/v1/products") public class ProductController { @Autowired private ProductService productService; @GetMapping("/{id}") public ResponseEntity getProduct(@PathVariable Long id) { Product p = productService.findById(id); return ResponseEntity.ok(p); } @PostMapping public ResponseEntity createProduct(@RequestBody @Valid Product p) { Product saved = productService.save(p); return new ResponseEntity<>(saved, HttpStatus.CREATED); } } ``` **Execution flow derivation:** 1. HTTP `GET /api/v1/products/42` arrives at embedded Tomcat on port **8080**. 2. `DispatcherServlet` (auto-configured by Spring Boot) routes the request. 3. `RequestMappingHandlerMapping` matches `/api/v1/products/{id}` to `getProduct(Long)`. 4. `@PathVariable` extracts `42` and binds it to parameter `id`. 5. `ProductService` is injected via `@Autowired` (constructor injection is preferred in modern Spring Boot 3.x). 6. `Jackson` (auto-configured) serializes the returned `Product` to JSON. 7. Response sent with HTTP **200 OK** and `Content-Type: application/json`. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Spring Boot Auto-Configuration Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: java -jar app.jar] --> B[Load META-INF/spring.factories] B --> C[SpringApplication.run] C --> D[Create ApplicationContext] D --> E[Component Scan: basePackage] E --> F[Auto-Configuration Engine] F --> G{Check ConditionalOnClass} G -- Present --> H{Check ConditionalOnMissingBean} G -- Absent --> X[Skip Config] H -- Missing --> I[Apply Default Bean] H -- Present --> J[Use User Bean] I --> K[Embedded Tomcat on Port 8080] J --> K K --> L[Application READY: /actuator/health] X --> M[Continue Scan] M --> K ``` ### 4.2 Microservice Architecture Topology ```mermaid flowchart LR Client[Client Browser or Mobile App] --> GW[API Gateway Spring Cloud Gateway] GW --> S1[Order Service Spring Boot] GW --> S2[Payment Service Spring Boot] GW --> S3[Inventory Service Spring Boot] GW --> S4[Notification Service Spring Boot] S1 --> DB1[(Orders DB PostgreSQL)] S2 --> DB2[(Payments DB MongoDB)] S3 --> DB3[(Inventory DB MySQL)] S4 --> MQ[Kafka Topic: notifications] S1 --> EU[Eureka Service Registry] S2 --> EU S3 --> EU S4 --> EU S1 --> CF[Hystrix Circuit Breaker] S2 --> CF S3 --> CF S4 --> CF CF --> S2 ``` ### 4.3 Spring Boot Request Lifecycle Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Client HTTP Request] --> B[Embedded Tomcat Connector] B --> C[DispatcherServlet] C --> D[HandlerMapping] D --> E[HandlerAdapter] E --> F[Controller Method Invoke] F --> G[Service Layer] G --> H[Repository Layer] H --> I[(Database)] I --> H H --> G G --> F F --> J[ResponseEntity built] J --> K[HttpMessageConverter Jackson JSON] K --> L[HTTP Response back to Client] ``` ### 4.4 Microservice Decomposition Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Identify Module in Monolith] --> Q1{Does it have independent
business capability?} Q1 -- No --> Stay[Keep in Monolith] Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Can team deploy
it independently?} Q2 -- No --> Refactor[Refactor for Loose Coupling] Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Shared database
or isolated?} Q3 -- Shared --> SplitDB[Extract Database Schema] Q3 -- Isolated --> CreateMS[Create Spring Boot Microservice] Refactor --> Q2 SplitDB --> CreateMS CreateMS --> Register[Register with Eureka and Add to API Gateway] Register --> End[Monitor via Actuator and Prometheus] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** > **Define Spring Boot. List any FOUR features of Spring Boot.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Spring Boot is an opinionated extension of the Spring Framework that simplifies the development of production-ready, stand-alone Spring applications by providing auto-configuration, starter dependencies, and embedded servers. **Four Features (2 Marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Auto-Configuration** — Automatically configures beans based on classpath. 2. **Starter Dependencies** — Curated dependency bundles to avoid version conflicts. 3. **Embedded Server** — Bundled Tomcat/Jetty enables JAR execution via `java -jar`. 4. **Spring Boot Actuator** — Production-ready endpoints for health, metrics, and audits. > **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2]** > **What is a Microservice? Mention THREE characteristics of Microservice Architecture.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** A microservice is a small, autonomous, independently deployable service that implements a single bounded business capability, communicating via lightweight protocols like HTTP/REST. **Three Characteristics (2 Marks — 0.67 each):** 1. **Single Responsibility** — One service = one bounded context (SRP). 2. **Decentralized Data Management** — Each service owns its own database (Database-per-Service). 3. **Independent Deployability** — Each service can be deployed, scaled, and updated without affecting others. --- ### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > **(a)** Explain the **Spring Boot Architecture** in detail with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of `@SpringBootApplication` annotation. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Design a **RESTful microservice** using Spring Boot for an **Order Management System**. Show the controller, service, and repository layers with proper annotations. Explain how the embedded server enables deployment. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Spring Boot Architecture (7 Marks):** Spring Boot Architecture consists of four layers: 1. **Spring Boot Layer (Top)** — Provides auto-configuration, starters, and actuator. 2. **Spring Framework Layer (Middle)** — Core features: DI, AOP, MVC, Data Access. 3. **Third-Party Integrations Layer** — Hibernate, Jackson, Logback, Tomcat. 4. **Java/JVM Layer (Bottom)** — The runtime platform. **Role of `@SpringBootApplication` (2 Marks):** It is a composite annotation equivalent to: - `@SpringBootConfiguration` — Declares the class as a configuration source. - `@EnableAutoConfiguration` — Activates the auto-configuration mechanism. - `@ComponentScan` — Scans the current package and sub-packages for Spring components. > **Diagram Mention:** Draw a layered stack diagram showing Java/JVM at the bottom, Spring Core, Spring Boot, and Application at the top, with arrows showing embedded server (Tomcat) and auto-config. **Part (b) — Order Management Microservice Design (7 Marks):** ```java // (1) Entity (1 Mark) @Entity @Table(name = "orders") public class Order { @Id @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY) private Long id; private String productName; private Double amount; private String status; // getters and setters } // (2) Repository (1 Mark) @Repository public interface OrderRepository extends JpaRepository { List findByStatus(String status); } // (3) Service (1 Mark) @Service public class OrderService { @Autowired private OrderRepository repo; public Order placeOrder(Order o) { o.setStatus("PLACED"); return repo.save(o); } public List getAllOrders() { return repo.findAll(); } } // (4) Controller (2 Marks) @RestController @RequestMapping("/api/orders") public class OrderController { @Autowired private OrderService service; @PostMapping public ResponseEntity create(@RequestBody Order o) { return new ResponseEntity<>(service.placeOrder(o), HttpStatus.CREATED); } @GetMapping public List list() { return service.getAllOrders(); } } // (5) Main Class (1 Mark) @SpringBootApplication public class OrderServiceApplication { public static void main(String[] args) { SpringApplication.run(OrderServiceApplication.class, args); } } ``` **Embedded Server Deployment (1 Mark):** The project is packaged as a **fat JAR** (via the `spring-boot-maven-plugin`). Running `mvn clean package` produces `order-service-1.0.jar` containing the compiled classes, dependencies, and an **embedded Tomcat server**. The command `java -jar order-service-1.0.jar` starts Tomcat on port **8080** internally — no external application server (Tomcat/WildFly) installation is required. --- > **Q3 (B). [Alternative Choice for KTU Pattern]** > **(a)** Discuss the **key design principles** of Microservice Architecture. Compare **Monolithic vs Microservice** architectures in a tabular form. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Explain the **Strangler Fig Pattern** for migrating a monolithic application to microservices. List the **challenges** of microservice architecture. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Key Design Principles (7 Marks):** The **9 Key Principles** (3 Marks): 1. **Single Responsibility** — One service = one bounded business context. 2. **Loose Coupling** — Services know as little as possible about each other. 3. **High Cohesion** — Related functionality grouped together. 4. **Autonomy** — Independently deployable, testable, scalable. 5. **API-First Design** — Contracts defined before implementation (OpenAPI/Swagger). 6. **Database per Service** — No shared databases. 7. **Statelessness** — No client session in server memory. 8. **Resilience** — Circuit breakers, retries, fallbacks. 9. **Observability** — Centralized logging, tracing, metrics. **Comparison Table (4 Marks):** | Attribute | Monolithic | Microservice | |---|---|---| | Deployment | Single unit | Independent units | | Scaling | Vertical | Horizontal | | Fault Tolerance | Low | High | | Tech Stack | Single | Polyglot | | Team Coordination | High | Low (cross-functional teams) | | Startup Time | 30+ sec | 2–5 sec | | Data Consistency | ACID | BASE / Eventual | | Cost | Low initial | High initial | **Part (b) — Strangler Fig Pattern & Challenges (7 Marks):** **Strangler Fig Pattern (4 Marks):** 1. Place an **API Gateway** in front of the monolith. 2. Identify a bounded context to extract. 3. Build the new Spring Boot microservice alongside the monolith. 4. Route new traffic to the microservice; legacy traffic to monolith. 5. Repeat incrementally until the monolith is "strangled" away. **Challenges (3 Marks — 1 each):** 1. **Distributed Complexity** — Network latency, partial failures, serialization overhead. 2. **Data Consistency** — Distributed transactions are hard; need Saga or Event Sourcing. 3. **Operational Overhead** — Need Docker, Kubernetes, CI/CD, service mesh, observability tooling. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Missing `@SpringBootApplication` decomposition** — Examiners expect you to write out the three sub-annotations (`@SpringBootConfiguration`, `@EnableAutoConfiguration`, `@ComponentScan`). Writing only the macro annotation loses **2 of 7 marks**. > 2. **No diagram in long answers** — A 14-mark question without a block diagram typically loses **2–3 marks**. Always include a labelled architecture diagram. > 3. **Confusing Spring Boot with Spring Framework** — Spring is a *framework*; Spring Boot is an *opinionated tool to build Spring apps fast*. Examiners explicitly check this distinction. > 4. **Writing monolith advantages in microservice questions** — Bias your comparison. Always emphasize WHY microservices are preferred (independence, scalability, fault isolation). > 5. **Forgetting to mention embedded server** — A frequent omission. The phrase "embedded Tomcat" is a **mandatory keyword** in KTU Spring Boot answers. > 6. **Skipping the `java -jar` deployment explanation** — Worth **1 mark** in Part B; students often miss it. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** - ✅ **Spring Boot = Spring Framework + Auto-Configuration + Starters + Embedded Server**. - ✅ The three pillars are: **Auto-Configuration, Starter Dependencies, Embedded Server**. - ✅ `@SpringBootApplication` = `@SpringBootConfiguration` + `@EnableAutoConfiguration` + `@ComponentScan`. - ✅ Default embedded server = **Tomcat** on port **8080**; alternatives are Jetty and Undertow. - ✅ Default packaging = **executable fat JAR** (15–50 MB). - ✅ Microservice = small, autonomous, single-responsibility, loosely-coupled service. - ✅ Microservice communication = **REST/HTTP, gRPC, or Message Queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ)**. - ✅ The **Strangler Fig Pattern** is the KTU-prescribed migration strategy. - ✅ **Two-Pizza Rule** = team size for a microservice should be 6–10 developers. - ✅ **12-Factor App** principles are the foundation of cloud-native microservice design. - ✅ Key annotations: `@RestController`, `@GetMapping`, `@PostMapping`, `@Autowired`, `@Service`, `@Repository`, `@Configuration`, `@Bean`. - ✅ **Database per Service** — never share a database across microservices. - ✅ **API Gateway** is the single entry point for all client requests. - ✅ **Service Registry** (Eureka/Consul) is required for service discovery. - ✅ **Circuit Breaker** (Hystrix/Resilience4j) is mandatory for fault tolerance. - ✅ Spring Boot startup time = **2–5 seconds** (vs 30–120 sec for Spring monoliths). - ✅ Actuator endpoints: `/actuator/health`, `/actuator/metrics`, `/actuator/info`. - ✅ **Spring Initializr** (`start.spring.io`) is the official project generator. - ✅ Spring Boot 3.x requires **Java 17+** (KTU 2024 syllabus uses Java 17/21). ---

topics.title: API Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT745 - CLOUD, MICROSERVICES AND APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** API Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are API Tools?

An **API Tool** is a software application, framework, or platform that enables developers, testers, architects, and DevOps engineers to **design, build, document, test, mock, monitor, debug, and secure** Application Programming Interfaces throughout their entire lifecycle. In the context of cloud-native microservices architecture, API tools form the foundational layer that supports the **API-First Design** philosophy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *API Tools are specialized software utilities and platforms that facilitate the complete API lifecycle management — encompassing API design, prototyping, documentation, testing, mocking, versioning, security, deployment, and monitoring — to ensure interoperability, scalability, and reliability of distributed cloud-based microservices.*

### 1.2 Why API Tools are Critical in Microservices

In a microservices architecture, hundreds of independent services communicate exclusively through APIs. Without standardized tools, this becomes chaotic. API tools provide:

- **Standardization** — Uniform specification via **OpenAPI 3.0** or **AsyncAPI**.
- **Automation** — CI/CD integration for automated testing and deployment.
- **Observability** — Real-time monitoring, logging, and tracing.
- **Collaboration** — Shared contract between frontend, backend, and QA teams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):**
> API tools are essential for the **contract-first development** approach, where the API specification is authored *before* the implementation. This ensures loose coupling, parallel development, and reduced integration defects in cloud-deployed microservices.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Menu

> [!TIP]
> Imagine a **restaurant kitchen** (the microservice / backend). A **waiter** (the API) takes your order (request) and returns your food (response).
>
> - The **Menu** = API Documentation (Swagger / OpenAPI).
> - The **Order Pad** = API Request Builder (Postman / Insomnia).
> - The **Food Tester** = API Testing (SoapUI / JMeter).
> - The **Practice Setup** = API Mocking (Mockoon / WireMock).
> - The **Kitchen CCTV** = API Monitoring (Runscope / Apigee).
>
> Just as a restaurant cannot operate without a menu, testing, and quality control, microservices cannot function reliably without a well-engineered API toolchain.

### 1.4 Classification of API Tools

API tools can be classified based on their functional role in the **API lifecycle**:

| # | Category | Primary Function | Industry Standard Tools |
|---|----------|------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **API Design / Specification** | Authoring API contracts | Swagger Editor, Stoplight, Postman |
| 2 | **API Documentation** | Auto-generated, interactive docs | Swagger UI, Redoc, ReadMe |
| 3 | **API Testing** | Functional, load, security testing | Postman, SoapUI, JMeter, REST Assured |
| 4 | **API Mocking** | Simulating APIs before implementation | Mockoon, WireMock, Postman Mock Server |
| 5 | **API Monitoring & Analytics** | Uptime, latency, SLA tracking | Runscope, Uptime, Apigee, New Relic |
| 6 | **API Gateways** | Routing, throttling, authentication | Kong, Apigee, AWS API Gateway, NGINX |
| 7 | **API Versioning & CI/CD** | Lifecycle automation | Postman, GitHub Actions, Jenkins plugins |

### 1.5 Architectural Position of API Tools

> [!IMPORTANT]
> API tools operate at the **Contract Layer** of the microservices stack, situated between the **Client Layer** (web/mobile apps) and the **Service Layer** (business logic). They enforce the **API-as-a-Contract** principle, which is a core tenet of cloud-native development under the **Twelve-Factor App** methodology.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The API Lifecycle and Corresponding Tool Mapping

The API lifecycle is a **closed-loop, iterative process**. Each phase has dedicated tooling. Below is the structured logical breakdown of the lifecycle, the tools used, and the rationale.

### Phase 1 — **API Design (Contract-First)**

- **Step 1:** Author the OpenAPI 3.0 (formerly Swagger) specification in YAML/JSON.
- **Step 2:** Validate the specification against the OpenAPI schema.
- **Step 3:** Generate mock servers automatically from the spec.
- **Step 4:** Share the spec with frontend and backend teams for parallel development.

**Why?** Designing the contract first prevents ambiguity, enforces standardization, and enables **consumer-driven contract testing (CDCT)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **OpenAPI Specification (OAS)** is a vendor-neutral, language-agnostic standard maintained by the **Linux Foundation's OpenAPI Initiative (OAI)**.

### Phase 2 — **API Documentation**

- **Step 1:** Auto-render the OpenAPI YAML into interactive HTML using **Swagger UI** or **Redoc**.
- **Step 2:** Embed code samples in multiple languages (curl, JavaScript, Python, Java).
- **Step 3:** Host documentation on internal developer portals (e.g., Backstage, ReadMe).

**Why?** Well-documented APIs reduce onboarding time and external developer support tickets by up to **40%** (industry benchmark).

### Phase 3 — **API Testing**

- **Step 1:** **Unit Testing** — Test individual endpoints in isolation using Postman/Newman or REST Assured.
- **Step 2:** **Integration Testing** — Verify inter-service API contracts using Pact (CDCT).
- **Step 3:** **Load/Performance Testing** — Simulate concurrent traffic with **Apache JMeter** or **k6**.
- **Step 4:** **Security Testing** — Run **OWASP API Security Top 10** checks using tools like **Burp Suite** or **Astra**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **OWASP API Security Top 10 (2023)** identifies the most critical API vulnerabilities: *Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA), Broken Authentication, Broken Object Property Level Authorization, Unrestricted Resource Consumption, Broken Function Level Authorization, Server-Side Request Forgery, Security Misconfiguration, Lack of Protection from Automated Threats, Improper Asset Management, and Unsafe Consumption of APIs.*

### Phase 4 — **API Mocking**

- **Step 1:** Spin up a mock server from the OpenAPI spec.
- **Step 2:** Define example responses, status codes, and headers.
- **Step 3:** Frontend teams consume the mock while backend is under development.

**Why?** Decouples teams, accelerates development, and enables **shift-left testing**.

### Phase 5 — **API Deployment & Versioning**

- **Step 1:** Containerize the API service (Docker).
- **Step 2:** Deploy to a cloud platform (Kubernetes, AWS ECS, Azure AKS).
- **Step 3:** Register the API in an **API Gateway** (Kong, Apigee).
- **Step 4:** Version the API using **URI versioning** (`/api/v1/users`) or **header-based versioning**.

### Phase 6 — **API Monitoring & Observability**

- **Step 1:** Collect metrics — latency (p50, p95, p99), error rate, throughput, saturation.
- **Step 2:** Centralize logs using **ELK Stack** (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) or **Loki**.
- **Step 3:** Trace requests across services with **OpenTelemetry** + **Jaeger/Zipkin**.
- **Step 4:** Set up alerts on SLO violations using **Prometheus + Alertmanager**.

### Phase 7 — **API Deprecation & Retirement**

- **Step 1:** Issue deprecation notices (e.g., `Sunset` HTTP header per RFC 8594).
- **Step 2:** Provide migration guides and maintain a **deprecation timeline** (typically 6–12 months).
- **Step 3:** Monitor usage of deprecated endpoints and notify consumers.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Table — Key Standards, Formats & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX RULE:** The vertical pipe `|` is replaced with `\vert` inside table cells to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

| Concept | Standard / Formula / Specification | Industry Value / Unit |
|---------|------------------------------------|------------------------|
| API Specification Format | **OpenAPI 3.0 / 3.1** | YAML or JSON |
| Async API Spec | **AsyncAPI 2.6** | For event-driven APIs |
| API Style | REST, GraphQL, gRPC, WebSocket | N/A |
| HTTP Status Code (Success) | **200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content** | RFC 7231 |
| HTTP Status Code (Client Error) | **400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 429 Too Many Requests** | RFC 7231 / RFC 6585 |
| HTTP Status Code (Server Error) | **500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable** | RFC 7231 |
| Versioning Strategy | URI Path, Query Param, Header, Media Type | N/A |
| Auth Standard | **OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, JWT (RFC 7519)** | RFC 6749 |
| Rate Limiting | Token bucket, Leaky bucket, Fixed window | Requests/second |
| Latency Metric | p50, p95, p99 | Milliseconds (ms) |
| Availability SLA | $SLA = \frac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| Uptime Tier | 99.9% (Three 9s), 99.99% (Four 9s) | Hours/year downtime |
| Throughput | $T = \frac{N_{req}}{t}$ | Requests per second (RPS) |
| Error Rate | $E_{rate} = \frac{N_{err}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR) | $MTTR = \frac{\sum T_{down}}{N_{incidents}}$ | Minutes/Hours |
| Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) | $MTBF = \frac{\sum T_{up}}{N_{incidents}}$ | Hours/Days |
| API Gateway Pattern | Single Entry Point, Cross-Cutting Concerns | N/A |
| Circuit Breaker States | CLOSED, OPEN, HALF-OPEN | Hystrix, Resilience4j |
| Idempotency Key | UUID in `Idempotency-Key` header | For safe retries |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

API tools power nearly every digital service you use:

- **Stripe** uses OpenAPI specs to publish its 100+ payment APIs to merchants worldwide.
- **Netflix** employs a sophisticated API gateway (Zuul) and contract testing (Pact) across 700+ microservices.
- **Amazon** internal services communicate via gRPC APIs for high-throughput, low-latency inter-service calls.
- **GitHub** provides auto-generated, interactive REST API documentation via OpenAPI, serving 100M+ developers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrices & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: SLA Availability and Downtime Calculation

Cloud providers advertise **uptime SLAs** like 99.9%, 99.95%, 99.99%. Let us mathematically derive the maximum allowable downtime per year for each tier.

**Step 1 — Define the basic availability formula:**

$$A = \frac{Uptime}{Total\ Time}$$

**Step 2 — Total minutes in a year:**

$$\text{Total} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600\ \text{minutes}$$

**Step 3 — Express maximum allowable downtime:**

$$D_{max} = Total \times (1 - A)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate for the standard SLA tiers:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{99.9\%} &= 525{,}600 \times (1 - 0.999) = 525{,}600 \times 0.001 = 525.6\ \text{minutes/year} \\
D_{99.95\%} &= 525{,}600 \times (1 - 0.9995) = 525{,}600 \times 0.0005 = 262.8\ \text{minutes/year} \\
D_{99.99\%} &= 525{,}600 \times (1 - 0.9999) = 525{,}600 \times 0.0001 = 52.56\ \text{minutes/year} \\
