# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 3 Marks]**

### Q1. Define a container image and explain its layered architecture.

**Model Answer:**

A **container image** is a read-only, portable, executable software package that contains the application code, runtime, libraries, dependencies, configuration files, and a minimal OS userland needed to run the application consistently across environments.

It is built as a **stack of read-only layers**, where each layer corresponds to a Dockerfile instruction. Layers are stacked using a **Union File System (UnionFS / OverlayFS)**. When a container is launched, a thin **writable container layer** is added on top of the read-only image stack; all runtime modifications (file creation, edits) are captured in this ephemeral layer using **copy-on-write semantics**.

**Advantages of the layered model:**
- **Layer caching** accelerates rebuilds
- **Efficient distribution** — only delta layers traverse the network
- **Storage deduplication** — multiple images share common base layers
- **Auditability** — `docker history` shows the provenance of each layer

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 3 Marks]**

### Q2. List and briefly explain any three Dockerfile best practices.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Use Minimal Official Base Images:** Prefer `node:20-alpine` or `python:3.12-slim` over full distributions like `node:20` to reduce image size and attack surface. Pin to a specific version tag for reproducibility.

2. **Leverage Multi-Stage Builds:** Split the Dockerfile into a `builder` stage (with compilers, dev headers) and a `runtime` stage (with only the compiled artifact). This can shrink images by **60–80%** and removes build-time tools from the production attack surface.

3. **Order Instructions for Cache Efficiency:** Copy frequently changing files (source code) *after* rarely changing ones (dependency manifests). This maximizes Docker's layer cache hit rate, accelerating CI builds from minutes to seconds.

*(Additional valid practices: Run as non-root via `USER`, use `.dockerignore`, pin tags by digest, externalize secrets via build args/environment variables, combine RUN commands with `&&` to reduce layer count.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks — Choice A]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Design a complete containerized solution for a **"Library Management System"** microservice. The system must expose a REST API for managing books and borrowers, use a relational database, and be deployable locally via Docker Compose. Specify the design of the Dockerfile with proper instructions and explain each choice. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the layered architecture of a Docker image. How does layer caching improve build performance? Provide an illustrative Dockerfile with at least **six** instructions and identify which layer benefits most from caching during iterative development. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution for Q.A (a) — Library Management Container Solution Design (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Requirements Decomposition:**

| Component | Technology | Port | External Config |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| Library API | Python 3.12 + Flask | `5000` | `DB_URL`, `SECRET_KEY` |
| Database | PostgreSQL 16 | `5432` | `POSTGRES_USER`, `POSTGRES_PASSWORD` |
| Adminer (DB UI) | Adminer 4 | `8080` | — |

**Step 2 — Base Image Selection:** `python:3.12-slim` — balance between size, C-extension compatibility, and debuggability.

**Step 3 — Authored Dockerfile (with justification):**

```dockerfile
# Multi-stage build for minimal production image
FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder

WORKDIR /app

# Cache dependencies layer separately
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --user -r requirements.txt

# Runtime stage
FROM python:3.12-slim

# Security: non-root user
RUN useradd --create-home --uid 1001 libraryuser
WORKDIR /app

# Copy only installed packages from builder
COPY --from=builder /root/.local /home/libraryuser/.local
COPY --chown=libraryuser:libraryuser . .

USER libraryuser
ENV PATH=/home/libraryuser/.local/bin:$PATH
EXPOSE 5000

HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s \
    CMD python -c "import urllib.request; urllib.request.urlopen('http://localhost:5000/health')" || exit 1

ENTRYPOINT ["gunicorn", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:5000", "app:app"]
```

**Step 4 — Docker Compose Stack (`docker-compose.yml`):**

```yaml
version: "3.9"
services:
  library-api:
    build: .
    image: library-api:1.0.0
    ports: ["5000:5000"]
    environment:
      - DB_URL=postgresql://libuser:libpass@postgres:5432/librarydb
      - SECRET_KEY=${SECRET_KEY}
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
    networks: [libnet]

  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    volumes: [pg_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data]
    environment:
      - POSTGRES_USER=libuser
      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=libpass
      - POSTGRES_DB=librarydb
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U libuser -d librarydb"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
    networks: [libnet]

  adminer:
    image: adminer:4
    ports: ["8080:8080"]
    networks: [libnet]

volumes:
  pg_data:
networks:
  libnet:
```

**Design Justification Summary (5 bullet points for marks):**
- Multi-stage build → final image < **200 MB** vs **900+ MB** naive approach
- Non-root `libraryuser` → least-privilege execution
- Named volume `pg_data` → data survives container removal
- Health checks + `condition: service_healthy` → correct dependency ordering
- User-defined network `libnet` → automatic DNS-based service discovery

---

#### Solution for Q.A (b) — Layered Image Architecture & Cache Efficiency (7 Marks)

**Layered Architecture Explanation (4 marks):**

A Docker image is a **stack of read-only layers** assembled via a **Union File System**. Each Dockerfile instruction (`FROM`, `RUN`, `COPY`, `ADD`) produces a new layer, which is a *diff* against the layer below it. Layers are identified by a **content-addressable SHA-256 hash**; if the input and instructions are unchanged, the hash is unchanged, and Docker **reuses the cached layer** instead of re-executing the instruction.

When a container is instantiated, Docker adds a **thin writable layer** on top of this read-only stack. All runtime changes (file creates, writes, deletes) are recorded in this topmost layer via **copy-on-write (CoW) semantics** — the underlying layers are never modified.

**Illustrative Dockerfile (3 marks):**

```dockerfile
FROM ubuntu:24.04                      # Layer 1 — base OS (rarely changes)
LABEL maintainer="ktu@library.app"    # Layer 2 — metadata (rarely changes)
RUN apt-get update && \
    apt-get install -y python3 python3-pip && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*        # Layer 3 — system dependencies (rarely changes)
WORKDIR /app                           # Layer 4 — working dir (rarely changes)
COPY requirements.txt .                # Layer 5 — dependency manifest (occasionally changes)
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt  # Layer 6 — installed packages (occasionally changes)
COPY . .                               # Layer 7 — application source code (changes on every commit)
EXPOSE 5000                            # Layer 8 — metadata (rarely changes)
CMD ["python3", "app.py"]              # Layer 9 — entrypoint (rarely changes)
```

**Which layer benefits most from caching?**

**Layer 6 (the `pip install` step)** benefits most. When a developer edits a Python source file in Layer 7, Docker's builder:

- Detects that Layers 1–6 have unchanged hashes
- **Reuses them from the local cache** (no re-execution of `apt-get`, no re-download of pip wheels)
- Re-builds only Layer 7 onward

This is why **ordering instructions from least-frequent to most-frequent change** is the single most impactful Dockerfile optimization. In typical iterative development, this reduces rebuild time from **2–4 minutes to 1–3 seconds**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks — Choice B]**

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat architecture diagram, explain the **end-to-end workflow of designing and developing a containerized microservice solution**, from requirement analysis to observability. List the **five layers of the container stack** and state the responsibility of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Docker Compose** and **Kubernetes** as container orchestration tools. In which scenarios is each appropriate? Describe how the same Library Management application would be re-architected as a Kubernetes Deployment, Service, and ConfigMap. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution for Q.B (a) — End-to-End Container Solution Workflow (7 Marks)

**The Five Layers of the Container Stack (5 marks):**

| Layer | Name | Responsibility |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Hardware / Infrastructure** | Provides raw compute, storage, network (bare metal, IaaS VMs) |
| 2 | **Host Operating System** | Shares the Linux kernel; enforces namespace/cgroup isolation |
| 3 | **Container Engine** | Translates user commands into kernel primitives (Docker, containerd) |
| 4 | **Image & Registry** | Stores immutable, versioned, content-addressable build artifacts |
| 5 | **Application & Orchestration** | Runs the microservice workload; manages scheduling, healing, scaling |

**End-to-End Workflow Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Requirement Analysis
        ↓
Base Image Selection
        ↓
Dockerfile Authoring
        ↓
Multi-Stage Build
        ↓
Image Build + Tagging
        ↓
Security Scan (Trivy)
        ↓
Local Container Validation
        ↓
Registry Push (ECR / Hub)
        ↓
Orchestration Manifest (Compose / K8s)
        ↓
Configuration Management (12-Factor)
        ↓
CI/CD Pipeline Automation
        ↓
Observability (Logs, Metrics, Traces)
        ↓
Continuous Feedback → Step 1
```

Each stage produces a verifiable artifact (Dockerfile, image manifest, deployment YAML, dashboard) that feeds the next stage. The loop closure between observability and requirements embodies the **continuous improvement** principle of DevOps.

---

#### Solution for Q.B (b) — Compose vs Kubernetes (7 Marks)

**Comparative Analysis (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Docker Compose | Kubernetes |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Target Scope** | Single host, dev/test, edge | Multi-host, enterprise, cloud |
| **Self-Healing** | Limited (`restart: always`) | Probes, auto-restart, auto-rescheduling |
| **Auto-Scaling** | Manual `docker compose up --scale` | HPA, VPA, Cluster Autoscaler |
| **Service Discovery** | DNS in user-defined network | CoreDNS + Service abstraction + Ingress |
| **Rolling Updates** | Manual | Native RollingUpdate / Blue-Green / Canary |
| **Learning Curve** | Low — single YAML file | Steep — many CRDs and APIs |
| **KTU Use Case** | Module 4 Labs, demos | Production / cloud-native module |

**Kubernetes Re-Architecture of Library App (4 marks):**

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: library-config
data:
  DB_HOST: postgres
  DB_PORT: "5432"
  DB_NAME: librarydb
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: library-secrets
type: Opaque
stringData:
  DB_USER: libuser
  DB_PASSWORD: libpass
  SECRET_KEY: super-secret-value
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: library-api
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels: { app: library-api }
  template:
    metadata:
      labels: { app: library-api }
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: library-api
          image: library-api:1.0.0
          ports: [{ containerPort: 5000 }]
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef: { name: library-config }
            - secretRef:    { name: library-secrets }
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet: { path: /health, port: 5000 }
            periodSeconds: 10
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet: { path: /health, port: 5000 }
            periodSeconds: 30
          resources:
            requests: { cpu: "100m", memory: "128Mi" }
            limits:   { cpu: "500m", memory: "512Mi" }
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: library-api-svc
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector: { app: library-api }
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 5000
```

**Scenarios:**

- **Use Docker Compose** for: local development, integration testing on a single host, small-scale deployments (< 10 services), edge/IoT devices.
- **Use Kubernetes** for: production-grade multi-host clusters, zero-downtime rolling updates, multi-region high availability, complex microservice topologies, autoscaling workloads, and organizations practicing GitOps.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in container design questions:**

1. **Forgetting to justify the base image choice** — Examiners expect a *reasoned* trade-off between size, security, and compatibility. Writing only `FROM node` without justification costs **1 mark**.

2. **Confusing `CMD` and `ENTRYPOINT`** — `CMD` provides *default arguments* and is easily overridden; `ENTRYPOINT` defines the *main executable* of the container. Mixing them up is a common 2-mark deduction.

3. **Ignoring security best practices** — Running as `root` (`USER` not set), hardcoding secrets in the Dockerfile, and using the `:latest` tag are all flagged. Always set a non-root `USER`, externalize secrets via `ENV` or build args, and pin to a version tag.

4. **Not mentioning multi-stage builds** — A naive single-stage Dockerfile is acceptable but suboptimal. Examiners explicitly reward the use of **multi-stage** builds for image size optimization (2–3 marks).

5. **Skipping the layer caching rationale** — When asked about build optimization, students often give the rule but not the *why*. Always explain that layer hashes enable Docker to skip re-execution of unchanged instructions.

6. **Confusing `EXPOSE` with `-p` flag** — `EXPOSE` is *declarative metadata* only; the actual port publishing requires the `-p` flag in `docker run` or the `ports:` mapping in Compose.

7. **Treating containers as VMs** — Writing `RUN apt-get install openssh-server` to "SSH into the container" is a classic anti-pattern. Modern best practice is `docker exec` for debugging.

8. **No mention of health checks and dependency ordering** — A 14-mark design question without `HEALTHCHECK` and `depends_on: condition: service_healthy` is considered incomplete in 2024 scheme evaluation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This Before Walking Into the Exam Hall**

- [x] **Container** = application + dependencies packaged into a single portable unit, isolated via **namespaces** and **cgroups**, sharing the host kernel.
- [x] **Container Image** = a read-only, layered, content-addressable filesystem template built from a Dockerfile.
- [x] **UnionFS / OverlayFS** is the technology that merges multiple read-only layers into a single unified view at runtime.
- [x] **Dockerfile** is a declarative, line-by-line build recipe consisting of ~15 instructions; the **most important** are `FROM`, `WORKDIR`, `COPY`, `RUN`, `USER`, `EXPOSE`, `ENTRYPOINT`, `CMD`.
- [x] **Multi-stage builds** drastically reduce image size and attack surface by separating builder artifacts from runtime dependencies.
- [x] **Layer caching** is enabled by content-hashed layers; ordering instructions from least-frequent to most-frequent change is the #1 optimization.
- [x] **Container vs VM** — containers share the host kernel and start in <1s; VMs run a full guest OS and take 30–60s. Containers are **50–500 MB**, VMs are **5–20 GB**.
- [x] **Docker Compose** is for single-host, multi-container local development; **Kubernetes** is for multi-host, production-grade, self-healing, autoscaling deployments.
- [x] **12-Factor App compliance** mandates externalized configuration via environment variables, one-process-per-container, and stateless design.
- [x] **Container security triad** — minimal base image + non-root user + vulnerability scanning (Trivy/Snyk) at every CI/CD build.
- [x] **Production observability** is achieved via structured logs to STDOUT, Prometheus metrics on `/metrics`, and OpenTelemetry traces.
- [x] **12-Step Container Solution Workflow** — Requirements → Base Image → Dockerfile → Multi-Stage → Build/Tag → Scan → Validate → Push → Orchestrate → Configure → Automate → Observe.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Memorize Mnemonic — "D-FREE-LUSH"** for the 8 most-tested Dockerfile best practices:
>
> **D**istroless/minimal base · **F**ew layers (combine `RUN` with `&&`) · **R**ootless (`USER` non-root) · **E**xternalize secrets · **E**xplicit version tags · **L**east-frequent-first ordering · **U**se `.dockerignore` · **S**ingle responsibility · **H**ealthchecks defined

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Containerization of Application and Deployment Using Kubernetes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT745 - CLOUD, MICROSERVICES AND APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Containerization of Application and Deployment Using Kubernetes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Containerization? (Foundation Recap)

**Containerization** is a lightweight form of operating-system-level virtualization that packages an application together with its dependencies (libraries, binaries, configuration files, runtime) into a single, portable, executable unit called a **container**. Unlike traditional Virtual Machines (VMs) that virtualize the hardware stack and require a full guest OS, containers share the host OS kernel and virtualize only the application layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** *Containerization is a OS-level virtualization methodology that enables deployment of distributed applications in portable, isolated, lightweight execution environments called containers, which share the host operating system kernel while maintaining process and filesystem isolation through Linux features such as cgroups and namespaces.*

### Conceptual Analogy: "The Shipping Container Revolution"

Imagine you are a global manufacturer shipping goods across the world. Before standardized shipping containers, every truck, ship, and train had to be custom-loaded — boxes, barrels, sacks of different shapes. Loading and unloading was chaotic, slow, and error-prone.

- A **physical shipping container** standardized the size → any crane in any port can handle it.
- A **software container** standardizes the application package → any host running a container runtime (Docker, containerd, CRI-O) can run it.

| Concept | Physical World Analogy | Software World Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Goods | Application code & binaries | Executable + dependencies |
| Shipping Container | Standardized steel box | Docker / OCI Image |
| Crane / Port | Loading infrastructure | Container Runtime (containerd) |
| Truck / Ship | Mode of transport | Host OS / Kernel |
| Cargo Manifest | Inventory list | Dockerfile / Image Layers |

## 1.2 What is Kubernetes (K8s)?

**Kubernetes** (Greek for "helmsman" — abbreviated as **K8s** because there are 8 letters between K and s) is an open-source, production-grade **container orchestration platform** originally developed by Google (based on their internal **Borg** system) and now maintained by the **Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF)**. It automates the deployment, scaling, load balancing, self-healing, and rollouts/rollbacks of containerized applications across a cluster of machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Kubernetes is the de-facto industry standard for container orchestration. The current stable version track is v1.29+ as of 2024. KTU examiners focus on **architecture, core objects (Pod, Deployment, Service, ReplicaSet), YAML manifest syntax, and kubectl commands**.

### Conceptual Analogy: "The Air Traffic Control Tower"

Think of Kubernetes as the **Air Traffic Control (ATC)** of a major international airport:

- **Planes** = Your containerized application pods (taking off, landing, taxiing).
- **Pilots** = The applications inside the pods, following their flight plan.
- **ATC Tower** = The Kubernetes Control Plane (deciding which pod lands on which node).
- **Runways & Gates** = Worker Nodes (the actual physical/virtual machines).
- **Ground Staff** = Kubelet, kube-proxy, container runtime (handling local operations).
- **Air Traffic Rules** = Scheduler, ReplicaSet, Network Policies (ensuring safe operations).
- **Flight Schedules** = YAML manifests (Declarative configuration).

Without ATC, every plane would self-organize → collisions, missed landings, chaos. Kubernetes brings **declarative, automated coordination** to container fleets.

## 1.3 Why Kubernetes? The Problem It Solves

| Challenge Without K8s | How Kubernetes Solves It |
|---|---|
| Manual container restart on crash | **Self-healing** — ReplicaSet auto-restarts failed pods |
| Manual load distribution | **Service abstraction** — built-in L4/L7 load balancing |
| Manual scaling during traffic spikes | **HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler)** scales based on CPU/memory/custom metrics |
| Inconsistent environments (dev vs prod) | **Immutable container images** + declarative YAML = environment parity |
| Downtime during updates | **Rolling updates** with zero downtime; instant **rollback** |
| Secret leakage in code | **Secret objects** with etcd encryption at rest |
| Manual network configuration | **CNI plugins** (Flannel, Calico) provide pod-to-pod networking |

## 1.4 Key Terminology Cheat-Sheet

- **Cluster** — A set of worker machines (nodes) that run containerized applications managed by the control plane.
- **Node** — A single machine (VM or physical) in a cluster. Two types: **Control Plane (Master) Node** and **Worker Node**.
- **Pod** — The smallest deployable unit in K8s; an **ephermeral wrapper** around one or more tightly-coupled containers sharing network and storage.
- **Control Plane** — The "brain" of K8s; makes global decisions (scheduling, scaling, event response).
- **Declarative Configuration** — You specify **WHAT** you want (e.g., "3 replicas of nginx"); Kubernetes figures out **HOW** to achieve it.
- **kubectl** — The official command-line interface (CLI) to talk to the Kubernetes API server.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** In KTU exams, students often confuse **Deployment vs ReplicaSet vs Pod**. Always remember the **management hierarchy**:
> **Deployment** (manages rollout strategy) → **ReplicaSet** (maintains pod count) → **Pod** (runs the actual container).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Kubernetes High-Level Architecture

A Kubernetes cluster follows a **master-worker (control-plane / data-plane)** architecture:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              KUBERNETES CLUSTER                          │
│                                                          │
│  ┌────────────────────────┐  ┌────────────────────────┐ │
│  │   CONTROL PLANE NODE   │  │   WORKER NODE(S)       │ │
│  │   (Master / Brain)     │  │   (Data Plane)         │ │
│  │                        │  │                        │ │
│  │  • kube-apiserver      │  │  • kubelet             │ │
│  │  • etcd (DB)           │  │  • kube-proxy          │ │
│  │  • kube-scheduler      │  │  • container runtime   │ │
│  │  • controller-manager  │  │  • Pods (containers)   │ │
│  │  • cloud-controller    │  │                        │ │
│  └────────────────────────┘  └────────────────────────┘ │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## 2.2 Control Plane Components (Detailed)

### (a) kube-apiserver
- **The Front Door** — All internal and external components talk to each other ONLY through the API server.
- Exposes the **Kubernetes HTTP/REST API** (default port **6443**, secured via TLS + RBAC).
- Acts as the **gatekeeper** validating and mutating every request before persisting it to etcd.

### (b) etcd
- A **distributed, consistent, key-value store** (uses **Raft consensus algorithm**).
- The **single source of truth** — stores ALL cluster state: nodes, pods, configs, secrets, RBAC rules.
- Production tip: always maintain **regular etcd backups** (3-2-1 rule).

### (c) kube-scheduler
- Watches for **unscheduled Pods** (those with `spec.nodeName` empty).
- Decides **WHICH node** a new pod should run on based on a two-step filter:
  1. **Filtering (Predicates)** — removes nodes that don't meet hard requirements (CPU, memory, nodeSelector, taints/tolerations, affinity rules, port availability).
  2. **Scoring (Priorities)** — ranks remaining nodes (e.g., least-loaded, balanced distribution) and picks the **highest-scoring** node.

### (d) kube-controller-manager
- Runs **controllers** (control loops) that continuously reconcile actual state vs desired state.
- Key built-in controllers:
  - **ReplicaSet controller** → ensures `replicas` count is met
  - **Deployment controller** → manages rollout/rollback
  - **Node controller** → monitors node health, evicts pods from unreachable nodes
  - **Service Account & Token controller** → manages RBAC
  - **Job controller** → runs one-off tasks to completion

### (e) cloud-controller-manager (CCM)
- Bridges K8s with the underlying cloud provider's API (AWS, Azure, GCP).
- Manages cloud-specific resources: **LoadBalancers** (creating ELB/ALB), **Routes**, **Persistent Volumes** backed by cloud disks (EBS, Azure Disk, GCE PD).

## 2.3 Worker Node Components

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **kubelet** | Agent running on every node; registers node with apiserver; ensures containers in pods are running and healthy; executes pod lifecycle hooks. |
| **kube-proxy** | Maintains network rules (iptables / IPVS) on the node to enable **Service-to-Pod** communication and load balancing. |
| **Container Runtime** | Software responsible for running containers. K8s supports any runtime implementing the **CRI (Container Runtime Interface)**: **containerd** (default since 1.24+), **CRI-O**, **Docker Engine** (via `cri-dockerd` shim). |

## 2.4 Core Kubernetes Objects (The KTU High-Yield List)

### 2.4.1 Pod
- Smallest unit. Can contain **1 or more** tightly-coupled containers (e.g., main app + sidecar log shipper).
- All containers in a pod share:
  - Same **network namespace** → share IP, localhost communication.
  - Same **storage volumes**.
  - Same **lifecycle** (start, stop together).
- Pods are **ephemeral** — they can die and be replaced; never rely on a pod's IP.

### 2.4.2 ReplicaSet (RS)
- Ensures a **stable set of replica Pods** is running at any given time.
- Uses **labels & selectors** to identify its pods.
- Superseded by Deployments for production use, but still the underlying mechanism.

### 2.4.3 Deployment
- A **higher-level abstraction** over ReplicaSet.
- Manages:
  - **Rolling updates** (default strategy) — gradually replaces old pods with new ones.
  - **Rollback** — `kubectl rollout undo`.
  - **Pause / Resume** rollouts.
  - **Revision history** (default: keeps last 10 revisions).

### 2.4.4 Service
- Provides a **stable virtual IP (ClusterIP)** and DNS name for a set of pods (whose IPs change).
- **Service types:**
  - `ClusterIP` (default) — internal cluster access only.
  - `NodePort` — exposes service on each node's static port (30000–32767).
  - `LoadBalancer` — provisions a cloud load balancer (ELB/ALB).
  - `ExternalName` — CNAME proxy to an external service.
- **Service Discovery Modes:**
  - **iptables mode** (legacy, default) — kube-proxy writes iptables rules; kube-proxy does L4 DNAT.
  - **IPVS mode** — better performance for >1000 services.

### 2.4.5 Namespace
- A **logical partition** for cluster resources (multi-tenancy, dev/staging/prod separation).
- Default namespaces: `default`, `kube-system`, `kube-public`, `kube-node-lease`.
- DNS pattern: `<service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local`.

### 2.4.6 ConfigMap & Secret
- **ConfigMap** — stores non-confidential configuration as key-value pairs (env vars, file mounts).
- **Secret** — stores sensitive data (passwords, tokens, TLS certs). Base64-encoded by default; can be encrypted at rest in etcd.

### 2.4.7 Volume & PersistentVolume (PV) / PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC)
- **Volume** — directory accessible to containers in a pod (lives with pod).
- **PV** — cluster-wide storage resource (admin-provisioned or dynamically provisioned).
- **PVC** — user's request for storage; binds to a PV matching its requirements (size, access mode).

### 2.4.8 Probe (Health Check)
- **Liveness Probe** — "Is the container alive?" If fails → kubelet **restarts** the container.
- **Readiness Probe** — "Is the container ready to serve traffic?" If fails → pod removed from Service endpoints (no traffic sent). **Does NOT restart.**
- **Startup Probe** — "Has the app finished slow startup?" Disables liveness/readiness checks until it succeeds.
- **Probe handlers**: `exec` (shell command), `httpGet` (HTTP endpoint), `tcpSocket` (TCP port open), `gRPC` (K8s 1.24+).

## 2.5 Deployment Strategies

| Strategy | Description | Downtime | Risk | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rolling Update** *(Default)* | Gradually replaces old pods with new ones, in batches (`maxSurge`, `maxUnavailable`). | **Zero** | Low | Most production apps |
| **Recreate** | Terminates ALL old pods, then creates new ones. | **Yes** | High | Stateful apps where old+new can't coexist (e.g., DB schema change) |
| **Blue-Green** | Run two parallel environments; switch traffic via Service selector swap. | **Zero** (with care) | Low (instant rollback) | Critical services |
| **Canary** | Routes a small % (e.g., 5%) of traffic to new version; gradually increases. | **Zero** | Very Low | Risk-averse rollouts, A/B testing |

## 2.6 Scaling in Kubernetes

- **Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)** — scales **replicas** based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics.
- **Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA)** — adjusts **resource requests/limits** of containers.
- **Cluster Autoscaler (CA)** — adds/removes **nodes** when pods can't be scheduled.

## 2.7 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet Table

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a CS/Cloud topic, the "formula sheet" takes the form of **Kubectl command mappings and manifest field semantics** rather than mathematical equations.

| Concept | Key Field / Command | Default Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Replica count | `spec.replicas` | `1` | Desired pod count |
| Rolling update batch | `spec.strategy.rollingUpdate.maxSurge` | `25%` | Max pods ABOVE desired count during update |
| Rolling update downtime | `spec.strategy.rollingUpdate.maxUnavailable` | `25%` | Max pods allowed UNAVAILABLE during update |
| Liveness check | `livenessProbe.initialDelaySeconds` | `0` | Wait before first probe |
| Liveness check | `livenessProbe.periodSeconds` | `10` | Probe frequency (seconds) |
| Liveness check | `livenessProbe.failureThreshold` | `3` | Restart after N consecutive failures |
| Service port | `spec.ports[].port` / `targetPort` | — | Internal / container port |
| NodePort range | — | `30000–32767` | Range of NodePort service ports |
| Resource request | `resources.requests.cpu` | — | Guaranteed CPU (e.g., `100m` = 0.1 vCPU) |
| Resource limit | `resources.limits.memory` | — | Hard memory cap (OOMKilled if exceeded) |
| `kubectl get` | `kubectl get pods -A` | — | List pods in all namespaces |
| `kubectl apply` | `kubectl apply -f file.yaml` | — | Declarative create/update |
| `kubectl scale` | `kubectl scale deploy/web --replicas=5` | — | Imperative scaling |
| `kubectl rollout` | `kubectl rollout undo deploy/web` | — | Roll back to previous revision |
| `kubectl logs` | `kubectl logs pod-name -c container` | — | Stream container logs |
| `kubectl exec` | `kubectl exec -it pod -- bash` | — | Open shell inside container |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Kubernetes is used in:
- **Microservices orchestration** — Netflix runs thousands of K8s pods to stream to 200M+ users.
- **CI/CD pipelines** — Jenkins, ArgoCD, Tekton run as K8s workloads.
- **AI/ML workloads** — Kubeflow runs distributed ML training jobs on K8s.
- **Edge computing** — K3s (lightweight K8s) runs on IoT/edge devices.
- **5G telecom** — 5G core network functions are containerized and orchestrated by K8s (per CNCF Telecom User Group).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Manifest Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Imperative to Declarative (Conceptual Workflow)

**Step 1 — The User Intent (Imperative form):**
A developer wants *"3 instances of nginx running, load balanced, restartable on crash, with config from a file."*

**Step 2 — Translate to Kubernetes Objects (Declarative mapping):**

| User Intent | Kubernetes Object | Key Field |
|---|---|---|
| "3 instances" | `Deployment` → `ReplicaSet` | `spec.replicas: 3` |
| "nginx running" | `Pod` template → `Container` | `image: nginx:1.27` |
| "load balanced" | `Service` (type: LoadBalancer/NodePort) | `selector: app: web` |
| "restartable" | `livenessProbe` | `httpGet / path: /` |
| "config from file" | `ConfigMap` mounted as volume | `volumes.configMap.name` |

**Step 3 — Final state convergence:**
After `kubectl apply`, the **Deployment Controller** observes the ReplicaSet has 0 actual pods but 3 desired → it creates 3 Pods. The **Scheduler** places them on nodes. The **Service** watches Pods with `app: web` label and adds their IPs to its endpoint set. The **kube-proxy** updates iptables so incoming traffic is DNAT'd to a healthy pod.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Many Part B 14-mark questions ask you to *"Design a K8s manifest for a given application scenario."* The above 3-step mapping is your exam template.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Walkthrough: A Production-Ready Kubernetes Manifest

Below is a **fully commented, KTU-exam-grade** Deployment manifest. Every line is explained for a 14-mark solution.

```yaml
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# 1) CONFIGMAP — application configuration
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: web-config
  namespace: production
data:
  app.properties: |
    server.port=8080
    cache.enabled=true
    log.level=INFO
```

**Line-by-line rationale:**
- `apiVersion: v1` — ConfigMap lives in the core API group (no `/apps` or `/batch` prefix).
- `kind: ConfigMap` — The object type.
- `metadata.name` — Must be unique within the namespace.
- `data` — Holds key-value pairs; `app.properties` is the filename mounted inside the container.

```yaml
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# 2) SECRET — sensitive credentials
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-credentials
type: Opaque
stringData:
  username: admin
  password: S3cureP@ssw0rd!     # auto base64-encoded by K8s
```

**Why `stringData` instead of `data`?** `stringData` accepts plain text and lets K8s perform the base64 encoding. `data` requires you to provide the pre-encoded value.

```yaml
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# 3) DEPLOYMENT — the workload
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-server
  labels:
    app: web
spec:
  replicas: 3
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1              # add 1 new pod at a time
      maxUnavailable: 0        # NEVER take down a pod during update
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web
        image: myregistry.io/web:1.2.0
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        env:
        - name: DB_USER
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: db-credentials
              key: username
        - name: DB_PASS
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: db-credentials
              key: password
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config-volume
          mountPath: /etc/app/config
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 100m
            memory: 128Mi
          limits:
            cpu: 500m
            memory: 512Mi
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 10
          failureThreshold: 3
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /ready
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 5
      volumes:
      - name: config-volume
        configMap:
          name: web-config
```

**Analytical breakdown of the `Deployment.spec`:**

| Field | Value | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| `replicas: 3` | Desired state | 3 instances for HA; lose 1, still 2 serving |
| `maxSurge: 1` | Surge policy | Limits resource spike during update |
| `maxUnavailable: 0` | Zero-downtime policy | "Never go below desired count" |
| `imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent` | Image fetch | Use cached image if present; pull if newer |
| `resources.requests` | Guaranteed floor | Scheduler uses this to bin-pack pods |
| `resources.limits` | Hard ceiling | Prevents noisy-neighbor; container OOMKilled if exceeded |
| `livenessProbe` | Restart on fail | Catches deadlocks / hangs |
| `readinessProbe` | Traffic gating | Avoids sending requests to a still-starting pod |
| `volumeMounts` | ConfigMap mount | Hot-reloadable config (depending on app) |

```yaml
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# 4) SERVICE — stable network endpoint
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web-service
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: web
  ports:
  - protocol: TCP
    port: 80              # exposed port (ClusterIP)
    targetPort: 8080      # container port
```

**Endpoint computation flow:**

$$\text{Service Endpoints} = \{\, \text{Pod IP} \mid \text{Pod labels match } \text{selector} \,\}$$

This is automatically maintained by the **Endpoint controller** in the kube-controller-manager. When a pod dies, its IP is removed from the endpoints within seconds → traffic seamlessly shifts.

## 3.3 Derivation: Rolling Update Math

Suppose you have **N = 3** replicas, `maxSurge = 1`, `maxUnavailable = 0`.

**Round 1 (t=0s):**
- New ReplicaSet (RS-new) created. RS-new.replicas = `min(maxSurge, replicas) = 1`.
- Old ReplicaSet (RS-old) still has 3 pods (since `maxUnavailable = 0` means we can't scale it down yet... but wait, we need to "make room").

**Revised calculation with the standard K8s rolling-update algorithm:**

$$\text{RS-new replicas} = \min\left(\text{maxSurge},\ \text{total desired} - \text{RS-old that won't go away}\right)$$

More precisely, K8s performs a **discrete iteration**:

For each step `i = 1, 2, ..., N`:

$$R_{\text{new}}^{(i)} = \min\!\Big(\,i,\ R_{\text{new}}^{(i-1)} + \text{maxSurge}\,\Big)$$

$$R_{\text{old}}^{(i)} = N - R_{\text{new}}^{(i)} \quad \text{subject to } R_{\text{old}}^{(i)} \geq N - \text{maxUnavailable}$$

| Step i | RS-new (new version) | RS-old (old version) | Total Pods | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 3 | 3 | Initial |
| 1 | 1 | 2 | 3 | Surge: 1 new pod, kill 1 old pod |
| 2 | 2 | 1 | 3 | Surge: 1 new pod, kill 1 old pod |
| 3 | 3 | 0 | 3 | Final state, 0 old pods |

**Total update duration:**

$$T_{\text{total}} = N \times \big(\,\text{probe period} \times \text{failureThreshold} + \text{pod startup time}\,\big)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination tip:** When `maxSurge: 1` and `maxUnavailable: 0`, K8s creates a new pod first, waits for it to become Ready, and only THEN terminates an old pod. This guarantees zero downtime.

## 3.4 The Reconciliation Loop (Why "Self-Healing" Works)

This is the **core "magic" of Kubernetes** — every controller runs this loop:

```text
loop {
    desired = readFromAPIServer()       // from spec in YAML
    actual  = readFromAPIServer()       // from status
    if actual != desired {
        performAction(actual → desired)
    }
    sleep(poll_interval)
}
```

**Convergence guarantee:** K8s uses a **level-triggered** (not edge-triggered) reconciliation model → if you delete one pod out of three, the system observes `actual=2 ≠ desired=3` and creates a new pod. This is **idempotent** and **eventually consistent**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for KTU essays:** Kubernetes does NOT tell components what to do; it tells them **what the desired state is** and lets independent control loops converge. This is fundamentally different from imperative orchestration (e.g., Ansible, Chef) where steps are scripted sequentially.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right K8s Object

| If you need to... | Use this K8s Object | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Run a stateless web app with replicas | **Deployment** | Manages RS, supports rollouts/rollbacks |
| Run a single one-off script | **Job** | Guarantees N completions, then exits |
| Run a long-lived daemon (log shipper) | **DaemonSet** | One pod per node |
| Run stateful app with stable identity | **StatefulSet** | Stable network ID, persistent storage per replica |
| Schedule a recurring task (cron) | **CronJob** | Creates Jobs on a cron schedule |
| Store key-value config | **ConfigMap** | Plain, non-sensitive data |
| Store credentials | **Secret** | Base64 + etcd encryption |
| Expose app to external traffic | **Service (LoadBalancer / NodePort / Ingress)** | Stable virtual IP / L7 routing |
| Persist data beyond pod lifetime | **PersistentVolume + PVC** | Decouples storage lifecycle from pod |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Kubernetes Cluster Architecture (Master-Worker Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CP["CONTROL PLANE NODE (Master)"]
        API["kube-apiserver<br>REST API :6443"]
        ETCD["etcd<br>Distributed KV Store"]
        SCH["kube-scheduler<br>Pod placement logic"]
        CM["kube-controller-manager<br>ReplicaSet / Deployment / Node controllers"]
        CCM["cloud-controller-manager<br>Cloud API bridge"]
    end

    subgraph WN1["WORKER NODE 1"]
        KL1["kubelet<br>Node agent"]
        KP1["kube-proxy<br>Network rules"]
        CR1["containerd<br>Container runtime"]
        P1A["Pod A<br>app=nginx"]
        P1B["Pod B<br>app=redis"]
    end

    subgraph WN2["WORKER NODE 2"]
        KL2["kubelet<br>Node agent"]
        KP2["kube-proxy<br>Network rules"]
        CR2["containerd<br>Container runtime"]
        P2A["Pod C<br>app=nginx"]
        P2B["Pod D<br>app=worker"]
    end

    USER["User / kubectl CLI"] -->|HTTPS| API
    API <-->|read/write| ETCD
    API --> SCH
    API --> CM
    API --> CCM
    SCH -->|assigns pods| KL1
    SCH -->|assigns pods| KL2
    CM -->|monitors| KL1
    CM -->|monitors| KL2
    KL1 --> CR1
    KL2 --> CR2
    CR1 --> P1A
    CR1 --> P1B
    CR2 --> P2A
    CR2 --> P2B
    KP1 -.->|iptables/IPVS| P1A
    KP1 -.->|iptables/IPVS| P1B
    KP2 -.->|iptables/IPVS| P2A
    KP2 -.->|iptables/IPVS| P2B
```

## 4.2 Pod Lifecycle and Scheduling Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["kubectl apply -f deploy.yaml"] --> B["apiserver validates<br>stores in etcd"]
    B --> C["Deployment controller<br>creates ReplicaSet"]
    C --> D["ReplicaSet controller<br>creates Pod manifest"]
    D --> E["Scheduler picks<br>best-fit Node"]
    E --> F["kubelet on Node<br>pulls image"]
    F --> G["Container Runtime<br>starts container"]
    G --> H{"Readiness probe<br>passes?"}
    H -->|No| I["Pod NotReady<br>no traffic"]
    H -->|Yes| J["Pod Ready<br>added to Service endpoints"]
    J --> K["kube-proxy updates<br>iptables rules"]
    K --> L["Traffic flows<br>to Pod IP"]
    I -->|probe retries| H
```

## 4.3 Deployment Object Hierarchy and Update Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    USER2["User runs:<br>kubectl set image deploy/web web=nginx:1.27"]
    USER2 --> DCTL["Deployment Controller<br>detects spec change"]
    DCTL --> RSNEW["Create new ReplicaSet<br>(revision 2)"]
    DCTL --> RSCALE["Scale up new RS<br>by maxSurge"]
    RSCALE --> WAIT["Wait for new pods<br>Ready (readinessProbe)"]
    WAIT --> RSOLD["Scale down old RS<br>by maxUnavailable"]
    RSOLD --> CHECK{"Old RS.replicas == 0?"}
    CHECK -->|No| RSCALE
    CHECK -->|Yes| DONE["Rollout complete<br>store in revision history"]
    DONE --> HIST["kubectl rollout history<br>shows v1, v2, v3..."]
```

## 4.4 Service-to-Pod Networking Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EXT["External Client"] -->|HTTP :80| LB["Cloud LoadBalancer"]
    LB -->|kube-proxy DNAT| CIP["Service ClusterIP<br>10.96.45.12:80"]
    CIP -->|iptables rule| P3A["Pod A<br>10.0.1.5:8080"]
    CIP -->|iptables rule| P3B["Pod B<br>10.0.2.7:8080"]
    CIP -->|iptables rule| P3C["Pod C<br>10.0.3.9:8080"]
    subgraph LBL["Selected by Service selector: app=web"]
        P3A
        P3B
        P3C
    end
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Architecture: Containerization vs Virtualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VM["TRADITIONAL VIRTUAL MACHINES"]
        A1A["App A"] --> GA["Guest OS A"]
        A1B["App B"] --> GB["Guest OS B"]
        A1C["App C"] --> GC["Guest OS C"]
        GA --> HV["Hypervisor"]
        GB --> HV
        GC --> HV
        HV --> HOSTOS1["Host OS Kernel"]
        HOSTOS1 --> HW1["Physical Hardware"]
    end

    subgraph CONT["CONTAINERS (Kubernetes)"]
        A2A["App A"] --> RTA["Container Runtime (containerd)"]
        A2B["App B"] --> RTA
        A2C["App C"] --> RTA
        RTA --> HOSTOS2["Shared Host OS Kernel<br>(namespaces + cgroups)"]
        HOSTOS2 --> HW2["Physical Hardware"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway visible in the diagram:** Containers eliminate the Guest OS layer, resulting in **~10–100x faster startup** (seconds vs minutes), **~10x smaller image size** (MB vs GB), and **near-native performance** since there's no hypervisor overhead.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Docker container and a Kubernetes Pod. Why is a Pod considered the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Docker container** is a single runtime instance of a Docker image — a lightweight, standalone executable package containing an application and its dependencies, isolated using Linux namespaces and cgroups.

A **Kubernetes Pod** is a higher-level abstraction that **wraps one or more tightly-coupled containers** that share:
1. The same **network namespace** (same IP, same `localhost`).
2. The same **storage volumes**.
3. The same **lifecycle** (started, stopped, restarted together).

A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in K8s because K8s does NOT deploy individual containers — it always deploys **pods**, which then host the containers. This design allows the sidecar pattern (e.g., main app + log shipper in one pod).

| Feature | Docker Container | Kubernetes Pod |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Standalone runtime unit | Wraps one or more containers |
| Network | Own IP | Shared IP across all containers |
| Scheduling | Manual / via Docker Compose | Automated by K8s scheduler |
| Lifecycle | Container-level | Pod-level (all containers in pod) |
| Purpose | Run an app | Group co-located containers |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the Kubernetes scheduler. List any four factors it considers when placing a Pod on a node.**

**Model Answer:**

The **kube-scheduler** is a control-plane component that watches for newly-created Pods with no assigned `nodeName` and decides **which worker node** they should run on. It implements a two-step process:

1. **Filtering (Predicates):** Eliminates nodes that cannot run the pod.
2. **Scoring (Priorities):** Ranks remaining nodes and picks the highest-scoring one.

**Four scheduling factors:**

| Factor | Description |
|---|---|
| **Resource requests** | CPU and memory `requests` must fit within the node's available capacity. |
| **NodeSelector / Node Affinity** | Constraints based on node labels (e.g., schedule only on `disktype=ssd` nodes). |
| **Taints and Tolerations** | Nodes can "repel" pods unless the pod explicitly tolerates the taint (e.g., dedicate GPU nodes). |
| **Pod Affinity / Anti-Affinity** | Co-locate pods (affinity) or spread them apart (anti-affinity) for HA. |
| **Topology Spread Constraints** | Distribute pods evenly across zones/regions. |
| **Port availability** | If pod requests `hostPort`, node must have that port free. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose 3–4 marks on a 14-mark question by:
> (a) **Writing YAML without indentation/structure** — even 1-space mistake makes the manifest invalid; always paste clean YAML.
> (b) **Confusing `selector` with `labels`** — the `selector` of a Service/Deployment must match the `template.metadata.labels` of the Pod template, NOT the Deployment's own labels.
> (c) **Forgetting to explain the reconciliation loop** — K8s's "self-healing" comes from controllers continuously reconciling `actual vs desired` state.
> (d) **Not explaining WHY a particular field is set** — don't just write `replicas: 3`; explain "for high availability with N+1 redundancy."

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Kubernetes cluster. Differentiate between the **Control Plane** and the **Worker Node** components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a complete Kubernetes manifest YAML to deploy a stateless Node.js web application called `cart-service` with the following requirements: **(7 marks)**
- 4 replicas with rolling update strategy (`maxSurge: 2`, `maxUnavailable: 1`).
- Container image `myregistry.io/cart:2.1.0`, port `3000`.
- Environment variable `MONGO_URL` sourced from a Secret named `mongo-creds` (key `connection-string`).
- A ConfigMap named `cart-config` mounted at `/etc/cart/config`.
- Liveness probe on `/health` and readiness probe on `/ready`.
- CPU request `200m`, memory request `256Mi`; CPU limit `1000m`, memory limit `512Mi`.
- Expose the app externally using a `LoadBalancer` Service on port 80.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Architecture Explanation:**

*(Refer to Section 4.1 for the full architecture diagram. The answer should cover:)*

**Control Plane Components:**
- `kube-apiserver` — Front door; exposes REST API; only entry point to etcd.
- `etcd` — Distributed consistent key-value store; cluster's source of truth.
- `kube-scheduler` — Watches unscheduled pods; assigns them to nodes based on resources, affinity, taints.
- `kube-controller-manager` — Runs reconciliation loops (Deployment, ReplicaSet, Node, ServiceAccount controllers).
- `cloud-controller-manager` — Integrates with cloud provider APIs (AWS, Azure, GCP) for LoadBalancers and Volumes.

**Worker Node Components:**
- `kubelet` — Node agent; receives pod specs from apiserver; tells container runtime to start containers; reports node/pod status.
- `kube-proxy` — Maintains iptables/IPVS rules for Service-to-Pod networking.
- **Container Runtime** — Pulls images, starts/stops containers (containerd, CRI-O).

| Aspect | Control Plane | Worker Node |
|---|---|---|
| **Role** | Cluster-wide decisions, scheduling, state storage | Runs actual workloads (pods) |
| **Components** | apiserver, etcd, scheduler, controller-manager | kubelet, kube-proxy, container runtime |
| **Data stored** | Yes (etcd) | No (stateless wrt cluster state) |
| **Failure impact** | Cluster-wide | Only workloads on that node |
| **Count in production** | Typically 3 (HA) or 1 (dev) | Many (scale with workload) |

**(b) Complete Manifest Design:**

```yaml
# 1) ConfigMap for application configuration
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: cart-config
data:
  app.properties: |
    cache.ttl=300
    rate.limit=100
---
# 2) Secret (assumed already created; keys referenced via secretKeyRef)
# apiVersion: v1
# kind: Secret
# metadata:
#   name: mongo-creds
# type: Opaque
# data:
#   connection-string: <base64-encoded-mongo-url>
---
# 3) Deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: cart-service
  labels:
    app: cart
spec:
  replicas: 4
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 2
      maxUnavailable: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: cart
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: cart
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: cart
        image: myregistry.io/cart:2.1.0
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
        ports:
        - containerPort: 3000
        env:
        - name: MONGO_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: mongo-creds
              key: connection-string
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config-volume
          mountPath: /etc/cart/config
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 200m
            memory: 256Mi
          limits:
            cpu: 1000m
            memory: 512Mi
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 3000
          initialDelaySeconds: 20
          periodSeconds: 10
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /ready
            port: 3000
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 5
      volumes:
      - name: config-volume
        configMap:
          name: cart-config
---
# 4) Service
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: cart-service
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: cart
  ports:
  - protocol: TCP
    port: 80
    targetPort: 3000
```

**Apply sequence:**

```bash
kubectl apply -f cart-manifest.yaml
kubectl get pods -l app=cart -o wide
kubectl get svc cart-service
kubectl rollout status deployment/cart-service
```

**Why this works (reconciliation logic):**
- Deployment controller sees `replicas: 4` → creates ReplicaSet with 4 pod templates.
- Scheduler places 4 pods across worker nodes based on resource requests (`200m CPU, 256Mi RAM`).
- kube-proxy adds the 4 pod IPs to Service endpoints once they pass readiness probe.
- Cloud-controller-manager provisions the cloud LoadBalancer; its backend points to the nodes' NodePort.
- For zero-downtime updates: when you change image to `2.1.1`, K8s creates new RS, scales it up by 2 (maxSurge=2), waits for readiness, then scales down old RS by 1 (maxUnavailable=1) per iteration.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **declarative configuration** in Kubernetes. How does the **reconciliation loop** enable self-healing? Illustrate with a scenario where one out of three pods crashes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the following Kubernetes deployment strategies with suitable diagrams: **Rolling Update**, **Recreate**, **Blue-Green**, and **Canary**. For a production banking application, which strategy would you recommend and why? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Declarative Configuration & Self-Healing:**

**Declarative Configuration** means the user specifies **WHAT** the desired end-state should be (e.g., "3 replicas of `web` running"), NOT the step-by-step procedure to achieve it. The user submits this desired state as a YAML/JSON file via `kubectl apply`.

Kubernetes stores this in etcd. **Controllers** (e.g., ReplicaSet controller) continuously observe the cluster's **actual state** and take action to converge it toward the **desired state**.

**The Reconciliation Loop (Pseudo-code):**

```text
while (true) {
  desired = getReplicasFromSpec()      // 3
  actual  = getRunningPodCount()      // 3, then 2 after crash
  diff    = desired - actual          // 0, then 1 after crash
  if (diff > 0) {
    createNewPod(diff)                // reconciliation action
  }
}
```

**Self-Healing Scenario: One of three pods crashes**

| Time | Event | Desired | Actual | Action by K8s |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| t=0 | 3 pods running | 3 | 3 | None (steady state) |
| t=30s | `pod-2` container OOMKilled | 3 | 2 | kubelet reports status; RS controller observes gap |
| t=30.5s | RS controller creates new pod spec | 3 | 2 | New pod scheduled on a healthy node |
| t=35s | New pod's container starts; readiness probe runs | 3 | 2 | Awaiting readiness |
| t=40s | Readiness probe succeeds | 3 | 3 | Pod added to Service endpoints; traffic resumes balanced |

**Key Self-Healing Properties:**
- **Crash restart** — kubelet restarts crashed containers inside the same pod.
- **Pod replacement** — if node is unreachable for `pod-eviction-timeout` (default 5 min), pod is rescheduled elsewhere.
- **Health-based restart** — failed liveness probes trigger container restarts.
- **Traffic gating** — failed readiness probes remove the pod from Service endpoints.

**(b) Deployment Strategies Comparison:**

| Strategy | Process | Downtime | Resource Cost | Rollback | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rolling Update** *(K8s default)* | Sequentially replaces old pods with new ones. At any time, both versions coexist. | **None** (if `maxUnavailable: 0`) | ~1.5x during update | `kubectl rollout undo` | Most stateless apps |
| **Recreate** | Kills all old pods, then creates new ones. | **Yes** — full downtime window | 1x (no overlap) | Re-apply old manifest | Stateful apps where two versions can't coexist (DB migrations) |
| **Blue-Green** | Two full environments (Blue=current, Green=new). Switch service selector to flip traffic atomically. | **None** (instant switch) | 2x (full duplicate) | Flip selector back to Blue | Critical apps needing instant rollback |
| **Canary** | Routes small % (e.g., 5%) of traffic to new version. Gradually ramps up. | **None** | ~1.1x (small extra) | Reduce new-version replica count | Risk-averse rollouts, A/B testing, ML model deployment |

**Visual: Rolling Update over 3 iterations:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T0["t=0"]
        A1["v1"] & A2["v1"] & A3["v1"]
    end
    subgraph T1["t=step1"]
        B1["v1"] & B2["v1"] & B3["v2 (new)"]
    end
    subgraph T2["t=step2"]
        C1["v1"] & C2["v2 (new)"] & C3["v2 (new)"]
    end
    subgraph T3["t=end"]
        D1["v2 (new)"] & D2["v2 (new)"] & D3["v2 (new)"]
    end
    T0 --> T1 --> T2 --> T3
```

**Recommendation for Production Banking Application: Canary Deployment (or Blue-Green for critical patches)**

**Justification:**
- **Zero downtime** is non-negotiable in banking (regulatory + financial loss concerns).
- **Canary** allows a small % of real users to validate the new version's correctness (especially important for transaction processing).
- If the canary shows elevated error rates (e.g., monitored via Prometheus + alerting), the rollout is aborted with minimal user impact.
- **Blue-Green** is preferred for **database schema changes** or **security patches** where instant rollback is critical.

For a **typical micro-service in a banking app**, KTU expects the answer:
**"Canary deployment with progressive traffic shifting (5% → 25% → 50% → 100%) and automated rollback on error rate spike > 0.5%."**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for the night before the KTU exam:**

- **Kubernetes = K8s** — container orchestration platform, originated from Google's Borg, donated to CNCF.
- **Cluster has 2 planes:** **Control Plane** (decision-makers) and **Data/Worker Plane** (where pods run).
- **Control Plane components (mnemonic: AESC²):** **A**piserver, **E**tcd, **S**cheduler, **C**ontroller-manager, **C**loud-controller-manager.
- **Worker Node components (mnemonic: KPC):** **K**ubelet, **K**ube-proxy, **C**ontainer Runtime (containerd).
- **Pod = smallest deployable unit**, not container. Pods are ephemeral; never hard-code pod IPs.
- **Object hierarchy:** Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pod. **Deployment manages rollouts; ReplicaSet maintains replica count.**
- **Service types:** ClusterIP (internal), NodePort (each node 30000–32767), LoadBalancer (cloud LB), ExternalName (CNAME).
- **Probes:** Liveness = restart on fail. Readiness = remove from traffic on fail. Startup = disables others until app starts.
- **Rolling update defaults:** `maxSurge: 25%`, `maxUnavailable: 25%`. For zero downtime, set `maxUnavailable: 0`.
- **Declarative > Imperative** — always prefer `kubectl apply -f` over `kubectl create/run`.
- **Labels and Selectors** are the **glue** — Deployment's selector must match Pod template's labels.
- **Resources:** `requests` = guaranteed floor (scheduling). `limits` = hard ceiling (cgroup enforced).
- **CPU units:** `1000m` = `1 vCPU`. **Memory:** `Mi` = Mebibytes (binary), `M` = Megabytes (decimal).
- **ConfigMap** for non-sensitive config; **Secret** for sensitive (Base64; not encrypted by default — enable encryption at rest in etcd).
- **Self-healing = Reconciliation loop** = `desired_state - actual_state → take action`.
- **kubectl must-know commands:** `get`, `describe`, `logs`, `exec`, `apply`, `delete`, `scale`, `rollout`, `port-forward`, `top`.
- **Production patterns:** Always set resource requests, liveness/readiness probes, replica count ≥ 2, use Namespaces, use Ingress for L7 routing.
- **Blue-Green** = 2x resources, instant rollback. **Canary** = small % traffic ramp-up, lowest risk. **Recreate** = downtime acceptable. **Rolling** = K8s default, balanced trade-off.

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-minute exam traps to avoid:**
> 1. Confusing `kubectl apply` (declarative, idempotent) with `kubectl create` (imperative, one-shot).
> 2. Writing `matchLabels` in Deployment's `selector` that does NOT match the `template.metadata.labels` → K8s will reject the manifest with a validation error.
> 3. Forgetting the **`---`** YAML document separator between multiple resources in one file.
> 4. Saying "Kubernetes manages containers" — WRONG. **It manages Pods**, which manage containers.
> 5. Mixing up **liveness** (restart) and **readiness** (traffic gating) probes — losing 2 marks easily.

**End of Module 4 Notes — Containerization of Application and Deployment Using Kubernetes.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Security and Monitoring Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT745 - CLOUD, MICROSERVICES AND APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cloud Security and Monitoring Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Cloud Security?

**Cloud Security** is the discipline of protecting cloud-based systems, data, applications, and infrastructure from internal and external threats through the combined deployment of policies, controls, procedures, and technologies. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, cloud security is treated as a **shared responsibility** between the Cloud Service Provider (CSP) and the customer, covering data confidentiality, integrity, availability, and regulatory compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Cloud Security is the set of policies, technologies, applications, and controls utilized to protect virtualized IP, data, applications, services, and the associated infrastructure of cloud computing.* — KTU PECBT745 Module 4 Reference Notes.

## 1.2 What is Cloud Monitoring?

**Cloud Monitoring** is the continuous process of collecting, analyzing, and acting upon operational telemetry (metrics, logs, and traces) from cloud resources to ensure performance, availability, security, and cost optimization. It is the operational nervous system of any cloud-native deployment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of Observability** (Mandatory KTU Term): **Metrics** (numerical data over time), **Logs** (discrete event records), and **Traces** (request paths across services). These three pillars are explicitly mentioned in the KTU 2024 PECBT745 syllabus and are high-frequency in examinations.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy: The "Bank Vault + Control Room" Model

Imagine a large bank vault:

- The **vault door, biometric locks, and concrete walls** = **Cloud Security** (preventive and protective controls).
- The **CCTV cameras, motion sensors, and the security guard's monitoring room with multiple screens** = **Cloud Monitoring** (detective and reactive controls).

Just as a bank cannot rely *only* on locks (security) or *only* on cameras (monitoring), a cloud system requires both — **prevention** (firewalls, encryption, IAM) and **detection** (anomaly detection, log analysis, alerting). This analogy maps directly to the industry's **Defense-in-Depth (DiD)** strategy.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **CSP** | Cloud Service Provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) | High-weight term in Module 4 |
| **IAM** | Identity and Access Management | Mandatory topic |
| **CIA Triad** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability | Foundation concept |
| **DDoS** | Distributed Denial of Service | Frequently asked |
| **WAF** | Web Application Firewall | Linked to security tools |
| **SIEM** | Security Information and Event Management | Industry-standard |
| **RTO / RPO** | Recovery Time / Recovery Point Objective | Disaster recovery focus |
| **MTBF / MTTR** | Mean Time Between/To Recovery | Availability math |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** When asked to "explain cloud security," always begin with the **Shared Responsibility Model** — it is the single most-asked opening in KTU university papers for this module.

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme

This topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO4** of PECBT745: *"Apply cloud-native security and observability tools to design resilient microservices architectures."* It is worth approximately **15-20% of the total marks** in the End Semester Examination (ESE) and carries equal weight in internal assessments.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Shared Responsibility Model (Foundation Concept)

The **Shared Responsibility Model** divides security duties between the CSP and the customer. Misunderstanding this model is the **#1 cause of cloud breaches** (per Gartner 2024 industry reports).

**CSP Responsibilities (Security OF the Cloud):**
- Physical data center security
- Hardware and hypervisor security
- Network infrastructure
- Managed service patching (e.g., RDS, Lambda runtime)

**Customer Responsibilities (Security IN the Cloud):**
- Identity and access management (IAM)
- Data encryption (at rest and in transit)
- Operating system patching (for IaaS)
- Network traffic configuration (security groups, NACLs)
- Application-level security

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** In **IaaS** (EC2, Azure VM), the customer manages *more* (OS, middleware, runtime). In **PaaS** (RDS, App Service), the CSP manages *more*. In **SaaS** (Office 365, Gmail), the customer typically only manages data and access.

## 2.2 The CIA Triad in Cloud Context

| Principle | Cloud Implementation | KTU Tool Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | AES-256 encryption, TLS 1.3, KMS | AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault |
| **Integrity** | SHA-256 hashing, digital signatures, S3 Object Lock | AWS S3 Versioning + Macie |
| **Availability** | Multi-AZ deployment, Auto Scaling, CDN | CloudFront, Azure CDN |

## 2.3 Core Cloud Security Layers (Defense-in-Depth)

1. **Perimeter Layer:** WAF, DDoS protection (AWS Shield, Azure DDoS Protection).
2. **Network Layer:** VPCs, Security Groups, Network ACLs, VPN, PrivateLink.
3. **Identity Layer:** IAM roles, MFA, SSO (SAML 2.0, OIDC), AWS Cognito.
4. **Data Layer:** Encryption (KMS, HSM), Data Loss Prevention (DLP), tokenization.
5. **Application Layer:** Secure SDLC, SAST/DAST scanning, API gateways with throttling.
6. **Monitoring Layer:** SIEM, anomaly detection, threat intelligence feeds.

## 2.4 Cloud Monitoring Architecture (Standard 4-Stage Pipeline)

$$\text{Monitoring Pipeline} = \text{Collection} \rightarrow \text{Storage} \rightarrow \text{Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Action}$$

- **Collection:** Agents (CloudWatch Agent, Prometheus Node Exporter), APIs, log streams.
- **Storage:** Time-series databases (TSDB), object stores (S3 for logs), cold archives.
- **Analysis:** Rule engines, ML-based anomaly detection, dashboards.
- **Action:** Alerts (PagerDuty, SNS, Email), automated remediation (Lambda, Azure Functions).

## 2.5 High-Yield Formula Sheet (KTU Examination Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are high-probability exam topics. The availability and risk formulas are the two most frequently tested numericals in the KTU 2024 ESE.**

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **System Availability** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) |
| **Annual Downtime** | $D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ | Minutes per year |
| **SLA Availability %** | $\text{SLA} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{\text{Allowed Downtime}}{\text{Period}}\right)$ | E.g., 99.9% (Three-Nines) |
| **Risk Score** | $R = T \times V \times I$ | Threat × Vulnerability × Impact |
| **Recovery Point Objective** | $\text{RPO} = \text{Maximum acceptable data loss}$ | Time (minutes/hours) |
| **Recovery Time Objective** | $\text{RTO} = \text{Maximum acceptable downtime}$ | Time (minutes/hours) |
| **Shannon Entropy (Encryption Strength)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Bits of information |
| **Mean Time To Detect (Security)** | $\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Detection Times}}{\text{Number of Incidents}}$ | Hours (industry avg: 204 hrs) |

### 2.5.1 Key Boundary Conditions (Must Memorize)

- **99% (Two-Nines):** ~3.65 days/year downtime
- **99.9% (Three-Nines):** ~8.77 hours/year downtime
- **99.99% (Four-Nines):** ~52.6 minutes/year downtime
- **99.999% (Five-Nines):** ~5.26 minutes/year downtime

### 2.5.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic abstractions. **Microsoft Azure's SLA** for a single VM is **99.9%**, but deploying across **Availability Zones** raises it to **99.99%** — a direct commercial application of the availability formula. Banks like **JPMorgan Chase** use **RPO = 0** (synchronous replication) and **RTO < 15 minutes** for their trading platforms.

## 2.6 Major Cloud-Native Security & Monitoring Tools (Mandatory for KTU)

### Security Tools
- **AWS Security Hub:** Centralized security posture management.
- **Azure Defender (formerly Security Center):** Threat protection across Azure workloads.
- **Google Security Command Center (SCC):** GCP-native vulnerability management.
- **HashiCorp Vault:** Secrets management (cross-cloud).
- **CloudTrail / Azure Activity Log:** Audit and compliance logging.

### Monitoring & Observability Tools
- **AWS CloudWatch:** Metrics, logs, alarms, dashboards (AWS-native).
- **Azure Monitor + Application Insights:** End-to-end APM for Azure.
- **Google Cloud Operations Suite (formerly Stackdriver):** GCP's unified observability.
- **Prometheus + Grafana:** Open-source industry standard (CNCF graduated).
- **ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana):** Centralized logging.
- **Jaeger / Zipkin:** Distributed tracing.
- **Datadog / New Relic / Dynatrace:** Third-party SaaS observability platforms.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** KTU 2024 papers frequently ask for a **comparative study** of AWS CloudWatch vs. Azure Monitor vs. Google Cloud Operations. Always include at least one comparison table in your answer.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Calculating Annual Downtime from SLA

**Problem Statement:** A cloud provider advertises an SLA of **99.95%** for a managed database service. Calculate the maximum allowed annual downtime in minutes.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the input.**
The SLA is given as a percentage. We must convert this to a decimal form for the formula.

$$\text{Availability Ratio} = \frac{99.95}{100} = 0.9995$$

**Step 2 — Recall the total minutes in a year.**
The standard non-leap year contains **365 days**. The conversion factor is:

$$T_{\text{year}} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the availability formula.**
The annual downtime is the complement of availability, scaled to the total time period.

$$D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - A) \times T_{\text{year}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 525{,}600$$

**Step 5 — Compute the unavailability ratio.**

$$1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005$$

**Step 6 — Perform the final multiplication.**

$$D_{\text{annual}} = 0.0005 \times 525{,}600$$

$$D_{\text{annual}} = 262.8 \text{ minutes per year}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to hours for human-readability (optional but recommended for KTU).**

$$D_{\text{annual in hours}} = \frac{262.8}{60} \approx 4.38 \text{ hours per year}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** A 99.95% SLA permits approximately **262.8 minutes (≈ 4.38 hours)** of downtime per year. This is the boundary between Three-Nines and Four-Nines availability.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Quantitative Risk Assessment for a Cloud Deployment

**Problem Statement:** A microservice hosted on AWS EC2 has been identified with the following risk profile:
- Threat Likelihood (T) = 0.4 (40% chance per year)
- Vulnerability Score (V) = 0.7 (medium-high exposure)
- Impact (I) = ₹10,00,000 (business loss if exploited)

Calculate the **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Define the Risk Equation.**
The standard risk scoring formula in cloud security frameworks (NIST, ISO 27001) is:

$$R = T \times V \times I$$

**Step 2 — Identify each input value with units.**

$$T = 0.4 \text{ (dimensionless probability)}$$

$$V = 0.7 \text{ (dimensionless score, range 0-1)}$$

$$I = 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ (currency units, INR)}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply T and V (intermediate step).**

$$T \times V = 0.4 \times 0.7 = 0.28$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the result by Impact (I).**

$$R = 0.28 \times 10{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Compute the final product.**

$$R = 2{,}80{,}000 \text{ INR per year}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result for management.**

The organization is expected to lose approximately **₹2,80,000 annually** due to this specific risk. If a security control costs less than this ALE to implement and maintain, it is **financially justified**.

> [!NOTE]
> **This derivation is a common KTU numerical problem.** The 14-mark questions often include a scenario where you must calculate risk, recommend a control, and justify the cost-benefit.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Comparative Tool Analysis Matrix

**Problem Statement:** Compare AWS CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, and Google Cloud Operations across 6 critical KTU-defined parameters.

| Parameter | AWS CloudWatch | Azure Monitor | Google Cloud Operations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Metrics Storage** | 15 months (detailed: 1-min) | 93 days (Azure Metrics) | 6 weeks (basic) |
| **Log Ingestion Limit** | No hard cap (pay-per-GB) | 500 MB/s per workspace | 50 MB/s per project |
| **Native Tracing** | AWS X-Ray (separate) | Application Insights (built-in) | Cloud Trace (built-in) |
| **Alerting Channels** | SNS, Email, Lambda, Auto Scaling | Action Groups, ITSM webhooks | Pub/Sub, Email, Webhook |
| **Pricing Model** | Pay-per-metric, pay-per-GB-log | Pay-per-GB-ingested, per-metric | Pay-per-GB-ingested |
| **Open Standards** | Limited (custom format) | Prometheus-compatible (scrape API) | Prometheus-compatible (native) |

### Analytical Insights for KTU Answers

- **Prometheus Compatibility:** Azure Monitor and Google Cloud Operations are **natively compatible** with Prometheus (an open-source CNCF tool). AWS CloudWatch requires the **CloudWatch Agent with Prometheus support** or migration to **Amazon Managed Service for Prometheus (AMP)**.
- **Cost Optimization:** AWS CloudWatch is often the **most expensive** at scale due to its per-metric pricing. Google Cloud Operations is generally the **cheapest** for high-volume log ingestion.
- **Best-of-Breed:** Most enterprises (Netflix, Uber, Airbnb) use a **multi-tool strategy** — e.g., Prometheus + Grafana for metrics, ELK for logs, Jaeger for traces.

## 3.4 Analytical Framework: The SLI/SLO/SLA Hierarchy

This is a **mandatory KTU concept** for cloud-native monitoring.

**Step 1 — Define SLI (Service Level Indicator).**
SLI is a **quantitative measure** of a service's behavior.

$$\text{SLI} = \frac{\text{Good Events}}{\text{Total Events}} \times 100\%$$

**Example:** For a REST API, an SLI could be:

$$\text{SLI}_{\text{latency}} = \frac{\text{Requests} < 200\text{ms}}{\text{Total Requests}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Define SLO (Service Level Objective).**
SLO is the **target value** for an SLI over a time window.

$$\text{SLO} = \text{Target}(\text{SLI}) \text{ over window } W$$

**Example:** "99.9% of API requests will have latency < 200ms over a rolling 30-day window."

**Step 3 — Define SLA (Service Level Agreement).**
SLA is the **contractual commitment** with consequences (usually financial credits) if the SLO is breached.

$$\text{SLA} = \text{SLO} + \text{Penalty Clause}$$

> [!TIP]
> **The hierarchy is:** SLI (the measurement) → SLO (the target) → SLA (the contract). Always explain in this order in KTU answers.

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Alert Flow and Incident Response Sequence

This is a **sequence diagram** showing how a CloudWatch alarm triggers an end-to-end incident response.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant EC2Instance as EC2 Instance
    participant CWAgent as CloudWatch Agent
    participant CWMetrics as CloudWatch Metrics
    participant CWAlarm as CloudWatch Alarm
    participant SNSTopic as SNS Topic
    participant LambdaFn as Lambda Function
    participant OpsTeam as DevOps Engineer
    participant PD as PagerDuty
    
    EC2Instance->>CWAgent: Emits CPU Utilization Metric
    CWAgent->>CWMetrics: Pushes Metric Data Every 1 Minute
    CWMetrics->>CWAlarm: Evaluates Threshold Rule
    Note over CWAlarm: CPU greater than 80 percent for 5 minutes
    CWAlarm->>SNSTopic: Triggers Alarm State ALARM
    SNSTopic->>LambdaFn: Invokes Auto Remediation Function
    SNSTopic->>PD: Sends PagerDuty Incident
    PD->>OpsTeam: Pages On Call Engineer
    LambdaFn->>EC2Instance: Executes Restart API Call
    EC2Instance->>CWAgent: Resumes Normal CPU Levels
    CWAgent->>CWMetrics: New Metric Data Below Threshold
    CWMetrics->>CWAlarm: Evaluates and Returns OK State
    CWAlarm->>SNSTopic: Resolves Alarm
    OpsTeam->>PD: Acknowledges and Closes Incident
```

**Reading the sequence:** This shows the **closed-loop automation** possible in cloud monitoring — the system can self-heal via Lambda before a human even acknowledges the page.
