# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Part A questions in KTU 2024 ESE are short-answer, definition-based, or one-line derivations. Each carries 3 marks and expects 80-120 words.**

### Question A1: Define the Shared Responsibility Model in Cloud Computing. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]

**Model Answer:**

The **Shared Responsibility Model** is a security framework that defines the division of security obligations between the **Cloud Service Provider (CSP)** and the **cloud customer**. The CSP is responsible for **Security OF the Cloud**, which includes the physical data center, network infrastructure, hypervisor, and hardware. The customer is responsible for **Security IN the Cloud**, which includes data encryption, identity and access management (IAM), network configuration (security groups), and application security. The exact split depends on the service model: in **IaaS** the customer manages more (OS, runtime, middleware); in **PaaS** the CSP manages more; and in **SaaS** the customer typically manages only data and user access. This model ensures that no single party bears the entire security burden and clarifies accountability during incidents.

---

### Question A2: List and briefly explain the Three Pillars of Observability. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Model Answer:**

The **Three Pillars of Observability** are the foundational data types collected from cloud-native systems for comprehensive monitoring:

1. **Metrics:** Numerical data points collected at regular intervals (e.g., CPU utilization, request rate, latency). They are stored in time-series databases (TSDB) and are highly efficient for trend analysis and alerting. Example: Prometheus, CloudWatch Metrics.

2. **Logs:** Discrete, time-stamped records of events (e.g., error messages, audit trails). They are essential for debugging and forensic analysis. Example: ELK Stack, CloudWatch Logs.

3. **Traces:** Records of a single request's path as it propagates through multiple microservices. They are critical for identifying latency bottlenecks in distributed systems. Example: Jaeger, Zipkin, AWS X-Ray.

Together, these pillars enable engineers to understand both the *what* (metrics), *why* (logs), and *where* (traces) of system behavior.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Part B questions in KTU 2024 ESE have an internal choice — students answer either Question A or Question B. Each 14-mark question has 2 sub-parts of 7 marks each.**

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Shared Responsibility Model** in cloud security with a neat diagram. Compare the responsibilities across IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS service models. Provide **two real-world examples** of breaches caused by misunderstanding this model.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **CIA Triad** as applied to cloud computing. For each principle, list two specific cloud-native controls and tools that enforce it, with justification.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Define the model.** The Shared Responsibility Model is a security framework that explicitly partitions security and compliance duties between the Cloud Service Provider (e.g., AWS, Azure, GCP) and the customer. The CSP is responsible for *Security OF the Cloud* (infrastructure layers), while the customer is responsible for *Security IN the Cloud* (data, access, application layers).

**Step 2 — Diagram description (refer to SECTION 4.1).** Draw the layered diagram showing CSP-managed layers at the bottom (physical, network, hypervisor) and customer-managed layers at the top (data, access, application, OS, middleware). The diagram should clearly show the three service models as columns or layers.

**Step 3 — Compare IaaS vs. PaaS vs. SaaS:**

| Layer | IaaS (EC2) | PaaS (RDS) | SaaS (Office 365) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data | Customer | Customer | Customer |
| Application | Customer | Customer | CSP |
| Runtime | Customer | CSP | CSP |
| Middleware | Customer | CSP | CSP |
| OS | Customer | CSP | CSP |
| Virtualization | CSP | CSP | CSP |
| Physical | CSP | CSP | CSP |

**Step 4 — Real-world breach examples:**
- **Capital One Breach (2019):** A misconfigured Web Application Firewall (WAF) on AWS allowed an ex-Amazon employee to access 100+ million customer records. The breach was entirely the **customer's** responsibility under the shared model.
- **Code Spaces (2014):** An attacker gained access to the company's AWS console and deleted all data and backups. The lack of MFA on the root account was a **customer-side** failure.

**Conclusion:** Misunderstanding the shared model leads to catastrophic data loss. Customers must explicitly configure IAM, encryption, and logging to fulfill their half of the bargain.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Define the CIA Triad.** The CIA Triad is the foundational information security model comprising **Confidentiality** (data is accessible only to authorized parties), **Integrity** (data is accurate and unaltered), and **Availability** (data and services are accessible when needed).

**Step 2 — Apply to cloud computing with tools:**

| Principle | Tool 1 | Tool 2 | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | AWS KMS with AES-256 encryption at rest | TLS 1.3 for data in transit | KMS manages cryptographic keys securely; TLS prevents network eavesdropping. |
| **Integrity** | S3 Object Lock with WORM (Write Once Read Many) | SHA-256 hashing with digital signatures | S3 Object Lock prevents tampering; SHA-256 detects any modification. |
| **Availability** | Multi-AZ Auto Scaling Groups | CloudFront CDN with S3 origin | Multi-AZ survives data center failures; CDN absorbs traffic spikes and DDoS attacks. |

**Step 3 — Conclude with the operational linkage.** The three principles are interdependent: strong encryption (Confidentiality) must not degrade response time (Availability), and integrity checks (Integrity) must not become a single point of failure. Cloud-native architectures achieve this balance through **redundancy, automation, and zero-trust networking**.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Three Pillars of Observability** (Metrics, Logs, Traces) with examples. Explain how **Prometheus and Grafana** work together to implement metrics-based monitoring in a Kubernetes cluster.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cloud-hosted e-commerce application has **MTBF = 720 hours** and **MTTR = 2 hours**. Calculate:
   (i) System availability as a ratio and percentage.
   (ii) Maximum allowable annual downtime in minutes and hours.
   (iii) Identify the SLA tier (Two-Nines, Three-Nines, etc.) and recommend two architectural changes to improve it.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Define the three pillars.**
- **Metrics:** Aggregated numerical data sampled at fixed intervals. E.g., CPU% per pod, HTTP requests/sec, error rate. Stored in Time-Series Databases.
- **Logs:** Immutable, timestamped event records. E.g., "User 123 logged in at 10:32 AM from IP 192.168.1.1". Used for debugging and audit.
- **Traces:** The journey of a single request across multiple services, showing latency at each hop. E.g., OpenTelemetry trace from API Gateway → Auth Service → DB Query.

**Step 2 — Prometheus architecture.**
- **Prometheus Server** scrapes metrics from instrumented targets (e.g., Kubernetes pods with `/metrics` endpoint) via HTTP pull every 15 seconds.
- **Exporters** like the **Node Exporter** (for host metrics) and **kube-state-metrics** (for K8s objects) expose metrics in Prometheus format.
- **PromQL** is the query language used to compute aggregations (e.g., `rate(http_requests_total[5m])`).
- **Alertmanager** evaluates alert rules and routes notifications to PagerDuty, Slack, or email.

**Step 3 — Grafana integration.**
- Grafana is a **visualization and dashboarding tool** that connects to Prometheus as a **data source**.
- It executes PromQL queries, renders them as graphs/heatmaps/tables, and displays them on real-time dashboards.
- It does **not** store data — it queries Prometheus on demand.
- **Example dashboard panels:** "Pod CPU Usage by Namespace", "API Latency p95 vs p99", "Network I/O by Node".

**Step 4 — Why this combination?** Prometheus + Grafana is the **de-facto open-source standard** for K8s monitoring because it is CNCF-governed, free, pull-based (no data loss on agent failure), and integrates with thousands of exporters.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** MTBF = 720 hours, MTTR = 2 hours.

**(i) System availability as a ratio and percentage:**

$$\text{Availability Ratio} = A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

$$A = \frac{720}{720 + 2} = \frac{720}{722}$$

$$A \approx 0.99723$$

$$\text{Availability Percentage} = 0.99723 \times 100 \approx 99.72\%$$

**(ii) Maximum allowable annual downtime:**

$$D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$$

$$D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - 0.99723) \times 525{,}600$$

$$D_{\text{annual}} = 0.00277 \times 525{,}600$$

$$D_{\text{annual}} \approx 1455.91 \text{ minutes per year}$$

$$D_{\text{annual in hours}} = \frac{1455.91}{60} \approx 24.27 \text{ hours per year}$$

**(iii) SLA Tier and Architectural Recommendations:**

**SLA Tier:** **Three-Nines (99.9%)** — though the current 99.72% is below this threshold. The application is in the **Two-Nines to Three-Nines transition zone**.

**Two Architectural Improvements:**

1. **Multi-AZ Deployment with Auto Scaling:** Deploy the application across 3 Availability Zones within a region. This reduces the blast radius of a single data center failure. Combined with **Auto Scaling Groups**, it also reduces MTTR because failed instances are automatically replaced. Expected impact: availability rises to **99.95%** (≈ 4.4 hours downtime/year).

2. **Implement Database Read Replicas with Automated Failover:** Use Amazon RDS Multi-AZ with synchronous replication. This reduces MTTR from 2 hours to under 60 seconds via automatic DNS failover. Expected impact: availability rises to **99.99%** (≈ 52 minutes downtime/year).

**Final recommendation:** A combination of Multi-AZ compute and Multi-AZ database will push the system from 99.72% to **99.99% (Four-Nines)**, meeting enterprise-grade SLAs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing `(1 - A) × 365 × 24` instead of `× 525,600` for annual minutes. Always use the **525,600 constant**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **RTO** (downtime tolerance) with **RPO** (data loss tolerance). Examiners deduct 2-3 marks for this swap.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** In Shared Responsibility answers, students often forget to **draw the diagram** — losing up to 3 marks out of 7. Always include a diagram.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Listing tools without explaining *why* they map to the CIA principle. Examiners want **justification**, not just tool names.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Using outdated terminology (e.g., "AWS CloudWatch Events" — renamed to **EventBridge** in 2019). Use 2024-current terminology.
